January   1917 


Chicago  Public  Library 

Book  Bulletin 


WITH  this  number  the  Book  Bul- 
letin enters  upon  its  seventh 
volume.  For  six  years  it  has  supplied 
a  steadily  growing  constituency  with  a 
monthly  record  of  new  books,  together 
with  items  of  news  relating  to  the  work 
and  progress  of  the  library. 

By  way  of  enlargement  and,  it  is 
hoped,  further  improvement,  a  cover  of 
coated  paper  has  now  been  added  which 
will  make  possible  the  printing  of  half- 
tone cuts.  These  will  be  chosen  with 
reference  to  their  value  in  illustrating 
various  library  activities  or,  occasionally, 
to  lend  interest  or  call  attention  to  sub- 
jects of  a  special  nature  included  in  the 
Bulletin. 

The  picture  on  the  cover  of  this  issue 
presents  a  view  of  the  south  staircase  in 
the  Library  building,  with  a  glimpse  of 
the  Delivery  Room,  and  its  famous  glass 
dome.  This  staircase,  of  pure  Carrara 
marble,  embellished  with  inlays  of  rare 
colored  stones  and  mosaics,  is  both  archi- 
tecturally and  artistically  noteworthy, 
and  the  view  here  presented,  taken  from 
the  landing  just  below  the  third  floor,  is 
particularly  effective.  The  whole  scheme 
of  decoration  combining  dazzling  white 
marble  with  many  hued  mosaics  has  been 
most  successfully  carried  out  and  has 
made  this  portion  of  the  building  one  of 
the  show  places  of  Chicago. 


n- 


Chicago  Public  Library 

Book  Bulletin 


Volume  7.  January,  1917  No.  1 

One    Year's    Work  of  the  Library 
1916 

Reduced  to  a  basis  of  units,  the  work  of  the  Chicago  Public  Library  for  1916 
shows  and  average  issue  of  books  for  home  use  of  16,666  every  day,  or  2,083  for 
every  working  hour  of  the  year.  The  total  issues  for  home  use  were  5,014,745,  a 
gain  over  the  preceding  year  of  nearly  half  a  million  volumes.  No  account  was 
kept  of  books  from  open  shelves  used  for  reading  or  reference  on  the  premises,  but 
as  the  reading  room  attendance  was  3,744,979  and  each  visitor  used  at  least  one  book, 
the  total  recorded  use  of  the  Public  Library  reaches  the  large  total  of  8,759,724. 

The  Library  maintains  44  reading  rooms,  six  of  them  in  the  Loop  building,  and 
the  others  scattered  in  various  sections  of  the  city,  including  19  in  small  parks. 
For  every  day  of  the  366  comprised  in  1916  (Leap  year)  the  visitors  in  the  reading 
rooms  averaged  10,232.  Of  these,  the  year's  total  was  thus  divided:  Main  read- 
ing room  942,752;  branches,  2,289,191;  Reference  Department,  347,934;  Civics 
Room,  62,157;  Woman's  Study  Room,  31,790;  Art  Room  16,590;  Thomas  Hughes 
Room,  33,843;  Patents  Room,  20,722. 

There  are  now  729,763  volumes  in  the  Library,  but  only  about  half  of  these 
circulate,  the  others  being  used  for  reference  purposes. 

Among  the  innovations  of  the  year  was  the  issuance  of  a  general  card  allowing 
each  reader  to  take  out  five  books  at  one  time  for  home  use,  an  extension  of  privilege 
greatly  appreciated  by  students  and  teachers  especially. 

Another  new  policy  inaugurated  the  organization  of  strong  libraries  in  some  of 
the  High  Schools — including  the  Nicholas  Senn,  Lake  View,  Austin,  Englewood, 
John  Marshall  and  Carter  Harrison  Technical. 

By  dint  of  many  economies,  the  Library  Board  was  enabled  to  get  together  a 
fund  which  enabled  the  purchase  of  a  lot  and  erection  of  a  branch  building  on 
Kimbark  avenue  near  Sixty-third,  to  be  known  as  the  Woodlawn  branch.  It  will 
be  the  largest  branch  library  in  the  city,  covering  an  area  of  44  by  125  feet,  and  it 
cost  the  least  money  of  any — $18,500  in  addition  to  the  price  of  the  lot  which  was 
$8,000.  It  is  designed  on  entirely  new  lines,  and  it  is  hoped  by  the  Board  to  make 
it  the  model  for  additional  branches  of  the  same  type.  The  Board  has  received 
for  the  use  of  patrons  of  the  Woodlawn  branch  a  gift  of  500  pianola  records,  and 
these  are  to  be  circulated  for  home  use  in  the  same  manner  as  books  when  this 
branch  is  opened,  about  February  1. 


430741 


CHICAGO   PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Plans  for  the  coming  year  contemplate  an  entire  reorganization  of  the  system, 
whereby  it  is  hoped  within  five  years  to  realize  the  following  objective: 

"Library  service  within  walking  distance  of  home  for  every  person  in  the  City 
of  Chicago  who  can  read  or  wants  to  use  books,  in  place  of  being  compelled  to 
ride  on  street  cars  forth  and  back,  an  average  of  10  miles  for  more  than  one-half 
the  population." 

If  this  plan  is  realized,  through  the  contemplated  system  of  five  regional  branches 
(there  are  now  two),  and  seventy  local  branches  (there  are  now  35),  the  following 
striking  improvements  will  be  made  possible: 

The  Chicago  Public  Library  will  be  in  a  position  to  serve  well  700,000  persons 
who  now  get  no  library  service  by  reason  of  distance  or  are  but  indifferently  served. 

A  maximum  travel  of  32  miles  to  consult  special  reference  material  or  to  draw 
music  scores  for  home  use  will  be  reduced  to  an  average  of  less  than  one  mile. 

Automobile  delivery  routes  will  be  reduced  from  32  miles  per  round  trip  to  six 
miles,  enabling  a  unit  delivery  five  times  greater  than  now,  and  without  additional 
cost. 


An  Exhibit  of  the  Dune  Country 


An  exhibit  of  etchings  and  photo- 
graphs taken  by  members  of  the  Prairie 
Club  and  loaned  by  them,  has  been  in- 
stalled on  the  fourth  floor  of  the  Chicago 
Public  Library.  The  pictures  are  shown 
in  cases  in  the  corridor  on  Washington 
street  side  of  the  building,  and  the  ex- 
hibit which  opened  about  January  1st, 
will  remain  until  about  the  1st  of  March, 
being  changed  from  time  to  time. 

The  nucleus  of  the  exhibit  is  a  collec- 
tion of  pictures  owned  by  L.  H.  Drury, 
and  taken  by  fellow  club  members,  and 
includes  some  remarkable  flower  groups, 
and  a  panoramic  view  of  one  of  the 
"blow-outs."  Earl  Reed,  who  has  de- 
picted this  region  with  both  pen  and 
pencil,  has  loaned  some  of  his  famous 
etchings,  which  are  in  a  case  by  them- 
selves. There  are  also  sepia  photos  taken 
by  L.  T.  Gobel,  A.  E.  Anderson  and 
Charles  Harmon,  like  etchings  in  treat- 
ment and  subject,  weird  scenes  of  sand 
formation.  Charming  views  of  trails  and 
forest,  floral  and  animal  life  have  also 
been  caught  by  the  cameras  of  A.  E. 
Ormes,  C.  G.  Dudley,  Frances  LaFol- 
lette,  R.  W.  Flowers  and  others.  To- 
gether they  give  but  a  glimpse  of  the 
miles  of  this  unique  country  which  is  con- 
sidered by  authorities  one  of  the  "wonder 
spots"  of  the  world. 


Accompanying  the  picture  exhibit  is 
an  attractive  bulletin  board  containing 
material  relating  to  the  dunes  in  the  form 
of  poems  and  articles  illustrated,  which 
not  only  tell  the  story  of  the  need  for 
this  great  playground,  but  its  value  to  the 
scientist,  artist  and  teacher,  and  what  its 
restfulness  means  to  the  individual  who 
is  weary  with  the  city's  strain. 

To  the  Conference  on  National  Parks, 
sitting  this  month  in  Washington,  a  simi- 
lar group  of  pictures  has  been  sent  by 
nature-loving  organizations  in  Chicago, 
to  tell  the  story  of  these  hills  and  plead 
for  the  preservation  of  the  Dune  country. 

Concerning  this  exhibit  a  Dune  en- 
thusiast writes  as  follows  to  the  Library : 

"The  lovely,  wild,  weird  Dune  country, 
with  its  wooded  hills,  moving  dunes, 
blow-outs,  winding  trails,  tree-laced 
creeks,  lowland  marshes,  and  the  Great 
Lake  washing  its  shores,  is  known  and 
loved  by  a  few  hundred  fortunate  ones 
who  have  breathed  with  joy  its  breezes 
laden  with  spices  of  the  forest,  caught 
Mother  Nature  at  work  making  and  un- 
making hills  and  valleys,  watched  the 
violet  carpet  change  to  lupin  blue,  to 
rose,  to  red  and  gold,  then  brown  and 
white,  heard  the  songs  of  early  spring, 
and  caught  the  flashing  of  cardinal  wings 
against  the  snow.  There  are  thousands, 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


however,  who  have  not  seen  this  wonder- 
land, nor  felt  its  wealth  of  beauty, 
spaciousness  and  quiet,  lying  so  close  to 
Chicago's  crowds  and  noise.  These  will 
be  glad  of  the  opportunity  offered  by  the 
Chicago  Public  Library  to  see  this  fas- 
cinating country  through  the  medium  of 
this  fine  exhibit." 


An  Inquiry  and  the  Answer 

One  of  the  patrons  of  the  Library 
writes  for  advice  as  to  the  advisability 
of  placing  in  the  hands  of  women  of  20 
years  the  novels  of  George  Eliot,  Mau- 
passant, Balzac  and  Zola.  The  follow- 
ing answer  has  been  sent: 

"Any  woman  of  20  could  profitably 


read  any  of  George  Eliot's  novels,  but 
one  might  well  hesitate  to  recommend  to 
the  average  young  woman  of  that  age 
some  of  the  books  written  by  Zola  or  De 
Maupassant,  and  perhaps  even  some  of 
Balzac's.  Not  that  strongly  flavored 
literature  of  this  type  is  corrupting  when 
read  by  mature  persons,  but  because  the 
immature  and  impressionable  minds  of 
young  people  read  into  the  words  mean- 
ings which  are  not  there  and  are  influ- 
enced accordingly  in  their  attitude  to- 
wards life.  It  is  true,  however,  that 
much  of  current  day  fiction,  while  seem- 
ingly harmless,  is  more  immoral  than  the 
frankest  of  these  books,  because  their 
portrayal  of  life  is  untrue  and  their 
basis  of  ideals  false."

Books  by  John  Muir 

The  mountains  of  California.  I  4659 

Author,  an  enthusiastic  lover  of  nature,  has  a 
close  personal  acquaintance  with  region  he  describes. 
—A.  L.  A.  Booklist. 

My  first  summer  in  the  Sierra.  I  4616 

Revision   of  a  diary  kept  from  June   3,   to    Sep- 
tember   22,    1869.      The   author's    observations    and 
his  delight  in  outdoor  life  will  recommend  it  to  all 
nature  lovers. — A.  L.  A.  Booklist. 
In  American  fields  and  forest.  K  17193 

A    collection    of    representative    sketches    by    six 
eminent  writers  on  nature. — Bk.  Rev.  Digest. 
Our  national  parks.  I  4692 

Written    by    a    genuine    lover    of    nature    who 
knows    more    about    the    forests    and    streams,    the 
mountains    and    glaciers,    the    flowers    and    animals 
of  the   Pacific  slope  than  any   other  living  person. 
•  — Nation. 

Stickeen.  F 15549 

Relates  the  escape  of  the  explorer  and  his 
faithful  dog,  Stickeen,  during  a  storm  in  the 
Alaskan  glacier  country. — A.  L.  A.  Booklist. 

Travels  in  Alaska.  14702 

The  reader  is  reminded  in  manner  of  some  of 
the  best  passages  of  Thoreau,  but_  exceeding  even 
them  in  richness  of  descriptive  imagery.  Muir's 
Alaska  would,  it  would  seem,  long  remain  the  last 
word  in  arctic  travel. — Boston  Transcript. 
The  Yosemite.  I  4615 

It  is,  however,  only  in  general  plan  a  guide- 
book in  the  ordinary  sense  of  the  word.  But_  be- 
yond these  practical  matters  this  monograph  is  a 
rich  storehouse  of  observation  by  one  who  worships 
nature  in  all  her  moods. — Nation. 
Edward  Henry  Harriman.  C  22329 

A  heartfelt  tribute  paid  by  a  friend  to  the  great 
railroad  magnate. 

The  story  of  my  boyhood  and  youth. 

C  23506 

The  author's  adventures  as  a  boy,  his  emigra- 
tion to  America  and  his  life  at  the  University  of 
Wisconsin,  are  recounted  in  a  vivid  and  interesting 
style. 


Luther  Burbank 

Burbank,  Luther.  Luther  Burbank,  his 
methods  and  discoveries  and  their  practi- 
cal application;  prepared  from  his  original 
field  notes  covering  more  than  100,000  ex- 
periments made  during  forty  years  de- 
voted to  plant  improvement,  with  the 
assistance  of  the  Luther  Burbank  Society 
and  its  entire  membership,  under  the  edi- 
torial direction  of  John  Whitson  and  Rob- 
ert John  and  Henry  Smith  Williams. 
1914-15.  12  v.  *P3884 

Beautifully  illustrated.  For  reference  only. 

Burbank,  Luther.  The  training  of  the 
human  plant.  L  10263 

Mr.  Burbank's  investigation  into  plant  life — cre- 
ating new  forms — points  of  similarity  between  the 
development  of  plant  and  human  life.  He  shows 
human  plant  needs  environment  of  love,  sunshine, 
air,  and  nourishing  food— discusses  heredity,  pre- 
destination, growth,  training  and  character. — Bk.  Re- 
view Digest. 

Harwood,  W.  S.    New  creations  in  plant  life.

of  Luther  Burbank."

Extremely  interesting,  untechnical  and  trust-

of  the  beetles,  their  curious  art  and  wonderful  in- 
dustry. 


Book  Titles  from  Omar  Khayyam 

Commenting  on  an  inquiry  in  the  Chi- 
cago Public  Library  Book  Bulletin  about 
the  number  of  book  titles  derived  from 
Omar  Khayyam,  the  Boston  Transcript's 
Librarian  says  that  many  authors  have 
drawn  on  Omar  for  titles  during  the  past 
ten  years.  The  writer  adds: 

"Before  that  it  was  not  unusual  to  do 
so.  Perhaps  one  of  the  first  books  in 
this  country  to  derive  its  title  from  Omar 
Khayyam  was  Mrs.  Atherton's  'A 
Daughter  of  the  Vine.'  Marion  Craw- 
ford used  'A  Rose  of  Yesterday.'  Kip- 
ling's 'The  Seven  Seas'  is  also  from 
Omar,  one  of  the  versions  reading 
'When  you  and  I  behind  the  Veil  are  past 
Oh,  but  the  long,  long  while  the  world 

shall  last, 
Which  of   our   coming  and   departure 

heeds 

As  the  Seven  Seas  should  heed  a  pebble 
cast.' 

"  'The  Moving  Finger,'  The  Desert 
and  the  Sown,'  'The  Master  Knot'  are 
all  fairly  recent.  The  poem  abounds  in 
'possible'  titles  for  books,  many  of  them 
excellent  for  an  author's  purposes.  Such 
phrases  as  'False  morning,'  'The  fire  of 
spring,'  'Tomorrow's  tangle,'  'The  phan- 


The  life  of  the  fly.  K  18084 

Contains  chapters  of  autobiography,  quaint  recol- 
lections of  childhood,  seeing  himself  before  the 
first  birds  nest  found,  the  first  mushroom  gathered, 
his  first  chemistry  lesson. 

The  life  of  the  spider.  K  18080 

Record  of  observations  of  the  life  histories  of 
several  kinds  of  spiders,  delightfully  written  and 
well  translated. 

The  mason-bees.  K  18102 

Record  of  painstaking  and  affectionate  observa- 
tions of  these  bees.  Includes  chapters  on  red  ants, 
parasites  and  cats. 

Social  life  in  the  insect  world.  K  18046 

The  twenty-one  chapters  treat  of  the  life  his- 
tories and  habits  of  the  cicada,  praying  mantis, 
golden  scarabaeus,  crickets,  Sisyphus  beetles,  bee- 
hunting  Philanthus,  the  emperor  and  banded  moths 
and  various  species  of  weevils. 

Fabre,   poet   of   science;   by   G.   V.   Legros. 

C  21856 


torn  caravan,'  'The  vain  pursuit,'  'The 
master  of  the  show,'  'Shapes  of  clay,' 
'This  sorry  scheme  of  things' — all  of 
these  sound  so  much  like  titles  that  it  is 
hard  to  say  that  they  may  not  have  been 
used.  'False  Dawn'  is  the  name  of  one 
of  Kipling's  short  stories.  Certainly 
many  current  novels  have  much  poorer 
names  than  'The  fire  of  spring,'  and 
'Shapes  of  clay'." 


Market  Reports 

The  Civics  and  Documents  Depart- 
ment receives  daily  the  press  sheets  of 
Market  Reports  from  the  U.  S.  Office 
of  Markets  and  Rural  Organization. 

Current  bulletins  report  on  the  follow- 
ing subjects: 

Shipments  to  the  Chicago  market. 

State  of  market. 

Wires  received  from  other  cities. 

Quotations. 

Books  of  1916 

The  annual  cumulated  number  of  the 
Book  Bulletin  for  the  year  1916  is  now 
in  the  hands  of  the  printer  and  will  be 
issued  February  1st  at  the  same  price 
as  that  of  former  issues,  ten  cents  per 
copy.

the  Document  department.    *Ser.  represents  serials  which  do  not  circulate.

Church  Unity

Rules  and  Information  for  Patrons 


Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person  re- 
siding in  Cook  County  having  permanent  em- 
ployment in  the  city,  may  enjoy  the  privileges 
of  the  Library  by  having  a  resident  of  the 
city,  whose  name  must  appear  in  the  city  di- 
rectory, act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon  application 
and  compliance  with  the  rules  and  regulations 
of  the  Board  of  Directors  a  card  good  for 
three  years  will  be  issued,  which  will  permit 
the  applicant  to  draw  five  books  for  home  use. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges  upon 
application. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and  may 
be  once  renewed  for  the  same  period.  Books 
may  be  renewed  but  once.  Books  of  recent 
purchase  labeled  Seven-day  books  cannot  be 
transferred  or  renewed.  The  card  must  be 
presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken  out  or 
renewed. 

Books  issued  at  Circulating  Centers  or  De- 
livery Stations  may  be  returned  at  the  Main 
Library,  but  books  cannot  be  applied  for  at 
the  Main  Library  and  delivered  to  Circulating 
Centers  or  Delivery  Stations. 

Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  habits 
may  use  the  various  Reading  and  Reference 
Rooms  of  the  Library.  No  card  is  required 


Library  Departments  and  Hours 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  De- 
partments, third  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:30 
p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Cata- 
logue, fourth  floor,,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m., 
Sundays  and  holidays  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People, 
fourth  floor,  open  9:30  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to 
5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room, 
first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street 
entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.;  closed 
on  Sunday. 


to  use  the  books  on  the  open  shelves  or  the 
books  in  the  stacks,  which  must  be  applied  for. 
A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported 
to  the  Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books 
drawn  on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  presented, 
and  for  all  fines  incurred.  If  a  card  is  lost, 
immediate  notice  must  be  given  to  the  Library. 
A  new  card  will  be  issued  thirty  days  after 
such  notice. 

A  fine  of  one  cent  a  day  on  each  work  of 
non-fiction,  and  three  cents  a  day  on  each 
work  of  fiction,  is  imposed  whether  bound  in 
one  or  more  volumes,  which  is  not  returned 
within  two  weeks  from  the  date  of  issue.  An 
additional  charge  of  two  cents  is  made  for 
each  notice  sent  for  the  return  of  a  book,  and 
no  other  books  will  be  delivered  to  the  person 
incurring  the  fine  until  it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the  call 
number  is  crossed  off  the  order  slip  whenever 
a  book  is  drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohibited, 
and  all  injuries  to  books  beyond  reasonable 
wear  and  all  losses  must  be  promptly  adjusted 
to  the  satisfaction  of  the  Librarian. 


Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and 
newspapers,  fourth  floor,  Randolph  street  en- 
trance; open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and 
holidays  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Music  Room,  fifth  floor,  Washington  street 
entrance ;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5 :30  p.  m.,  closed  on 
Sunday. 

Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin 
are  issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and 
August,  and  are  distributed  free  of  charge 
in  all  departments  of  the  Main  Library  and 
in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be  mailed  to 
any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year  to 
cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published 
in  January  of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten 
cents  per  copy. 


February  1917 


Chicago  Public  Library 

Book  Bulletin 


THIS  MAN 

THIS  man,  this  long,  bony, 
wiry,  sad  man,  floated  into 
our  country  in  1831,  in  a  frail 
canoe,  down  the  north  fork  of  the 
Sangamon  river  friendless,  pen- 
niless, powerless  and  alone — beg- 
ging- for  work  in  this  city — rag- 
ged, struggling  for  the  common 
necessaries  of  life.  This  man,  this 
peculiar  man,  left  us  in  1861,  the 
President  of  the  United  States, 
backed  by  friends  and  power,  by 
fame,  and  all  human  force;  and  it 
is  well  to  inquire  how. 

WM.  H.  HERNDON. 
Springfield,  1882. 


Chicago  Public  Library 

Book  Bulletin 


Volume  7. 


February,  1917 


No.  2 


Woodlawn  Branch 

6247-6249  Kimbark  Avenue,  at  Sixty-Third  Street 
Dedicated,  February  12th.     Opened  for  Service  February  13th 

Woodlawn  Branch  is  the  thirty-seventh  branch  in  the  Chicago  Public  Li- 
brary system,  but  the  first  branch  building  entirely  provided  from  public  funds. 
It  is  only  the  fourth  branch  to  be  housed  in  independent  quarters  designed  for 
library  use,  the  other  three  being  Blackstone  Branch,  now  ten  years  old,  the  gift 
of  Mrs.  T.  B.  Blackstone,  and  costing  $250,000;  Hiram  Kelly  Branch,  built  at 
a  cost  of  $55,000  from  proceeds  of  a  legacy,  and  Geo.  C.  Walker  Branch,  for- 
merly the  Walker  Library  of  Morgan  Park,  a  substantial  and  picturesque  village 
library  building,  acquired  through  annexation.  Thirty-three  out  of  the  thirty- 
seven  Chicago  branches  are  housed  in  rented  quarters,  in  schools  and  institu- 
tional houses  or  in  rooms  in  park  field  houses  provided  without  expense  through 
the  whole-hearted  co-operation  of  the  Park  Boards. 


14  CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Woodlawn  Branch  represents  a  conscious  and  deliberate  attempt  at  a  depar- 
ture from  the  conventional  plan  of  branch  buildings  hitherto  regarded  as  the 
ideal  of  library  architecture,  which  is  based  upon  the  Greek  cross  or  radial  ar- 
rangement, with  administration  desk  in  the  middle  and  public  rooms  and  book 
stacks  disposed  in  wings  around  this  central  point,  as  ensuring  maximum  con- 
trol and  supervision  with  minimum  number  of  attendants.  The  chief  trouble 
with  this  very  desirable  plan  lies  in  the  circumstance  that  a  building  so  designed 
is  usually  wider  than,  or  as  wide  as,  it  is  deep,  and  thus  requires  a  piece  of 
ground  of  considerable  frontage  while  depth  is  relatively  of  minor  importance. 
In  large  cities  where  real  estate  values  are  constantly  rising,  especially  in  the 
more  populous  sections  and  on  the  main-travelled  streets  which  offer  just  the 
right  conditions  for  effective  library  work,  this  consideration  alone  is  enough  to 
make  library  officials  pause.  In  New  York  City,  with  a  Carnegie  gift  of  over 
three  millions  for  branches,  the  problem  has  been  only  partly  met  by  construct- 
ing branch  buildings  of  narrow  frontage  and  several  stories  in  height,  an  expe- 
dient which  reduces  the  property  investment  but  involves  an  increased  staff  for 
administration. 

The  average  branch  library  building  of  the  type  here  described  costs  any- 
where from  $50,000  to  $200,000  according  to  style,  dimensions  and  ornamenta- 
tion. The  broad  facade  and  symmetrical  proportions  of  such  buildings  lend 
themselves  admirably  to  decorative  or  monumental  treatment  and  the  tendency 
to  indulge  in  these  luxuries  is  usually  restrained  only  by  the  size  of  the  build- 
ing fund. 

In  Chicago,  one  of  the  three  large  American  cities  which  have  not  accepted 
the  benefactions  of  the  great  Patron  of  libraries,  the  Board  of  Directors  was 
confronted  with  the  choice  between  adopting  a  more  economical  building  pol- 
icy or  waiting  for  the  slow  accumulation  of  a  surplus  from  the  annual  revenue. 
The  prospects  for  the  latter  contingency  may  be  disposed  of  by  the  statement 
that  the  income  for  1917  will  be  less  by  $50,000  than  that  for  1916— through  the 
operation  of  a  peculiar  provision  of  law  which  seems  to  have  outlived  its  use- 
fulness and  which  the  Board  hopes  to  have  rescinded  by  the  present  General 
Assembly. 

It  was,  therefore,  decided  to  authorize  the  construction  of  a  long  and  nar- 
row building  to  fit  an  average  city  lot ;  to  dispense  with  all  monumental  or 
ornamental  preconceptions  and  to  proceed  with  the  single  object  in  view  of  sup- 
plying reasonably  adequate  library  facilities  for  a  given  section  of  the  city  lit- 
erally "at  the  least  cost."  The  district  known  as  Woodlawn  was  chosen  because 
of  the  lively  interest  and  insistent  demand  awakened  by  the  small  deposit  sta- 
tion placed  there  four  years  ago  which  so  speedily  demonstrated  both  the  great 
need  for  library  service  in  this  populous  residence  community  and  its  own  inade- 
quacy to  meet  that  need.  Fortunately  a  42xl25-foot  lot  with  alleys  on  both 
sides,  assuring  perpetual  light  and  air,  was  found  available  at  a  cost  of  $8,000. 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


15 


On  this,  using  every  inch  of  space,  a  42x  125-foot  building  has  been  placed  at  a 
cost  of  $18,500.  On  the  street  front  a  tapestry  brick  facade  rises  to  a  two-story 
height,  the  second  floor  containing  a  work  room  and  staff  rest  rooms.  Beyond 
this  there  is  only  one  story,  carrying  the  main  floor  at  the  street  level.  The 
charging  desk  occupies  the  front  end,  under  the  overhanging  second  floor  and 
commanding  the  entrance.  At  the  rear  the  floor  has  been  elevated  to  form  a 
spacious  platform  which  will  serve  as  the  children's  alcove,  with  leaded  win- 
dows set  in  hinged  frames  and  a  special  emergency  exit.  The  side  walls  are 
lined  with  book  cases  and  the  long  expanse  of  floor  space  has  been  skillfully 
broken  by  book  cases,  magazine  racks  and  file  cases  set  through  the  middle. 
Tables  and  chairs  to  accommodate  200  persons  are  provided  and  shelf  space  for 
14,000  volumes.  An  interesting  special  feature  is  the  collection  of  500  piano 
player  rolls,  presented  to  Woodlawn  Branch  by  the  Starr  Piano  Company,  which 
will  be  issued  for  home  use  on  the  same  terms  as  books. 

Thus,  at  a  cost  of  little  more  than  $25,000,  a  branch  library  fully  equipped 
with  the  usual  branch  facilities  has  been  provided  and,  while,  as  has  been  said, 
the  entire  conception  is  frankly  an  experiment  and  constitutes  a  wide  depar- 
ture from  established  standards  of  library  architecture,  the  Board  of  Directors 
and  officials  of  the  Chicago  Public  Library  look  forward  with  confidence  to  the 
complete  justification  of  their  faith  that  Woodlawn  Branch  will  prove  both  a 
happy  solution  of  a  present  problem  and  a  pattern,  or  at  all  events,  a  point  of 
departure,  for  what  may  be  called  another  Chicago  Plan. 

The  Directors  have  asked  for  a  bond  issue  of  $500,000.  If  this  be  approved 
at  the  April  election  and  if,  meanwhile,  the  revenue  law  be  amended  so  that  the 
Library  shall  cease  to  be  the  football  of  the  "taxing  bodies,"  while,  at  the  same 
time,  the  bugbear  of  ill-considered  "Consolidation"  be  finally  and  forever  dis- 
posed of,  Chicago  will,  within  five  years  realize  a  vision  worthy  of  its  own  past 
achievements  as  a  dreamer  of  dreams  that  come  true.

Abraham  Lincoln 
Ten  Readable  Biographies 

Browne,  F.  F.    The  every-day  life  of  Abra- 
ham Lincoln.     1913.  C  2008 
An  old  favorite,  by  the  late  editor  of  the  "Dial," 
who   was   a  personal   friend   of   Lincoln.      Still    one 
of  the  best  of  the  lives. 

Charnwood,    G.    R.    B.      Abraham    Lincoln. 
1916.  C  23052 

By  an  Englishman  for  English  readers.  Gives 
a  good  sketch  of_  contemporary  American  politics 
in  an  effort  to  visualize  Lincoln  to  English  eyes. 
Unfortunately  revives  the  exploded  yarn  of  the 
illegitimate  birth  of  Lincoln's  mother. 

Hapgood,   N.     Abraham   Lincoln;   the   man 
of  the  people.     1899.  C  18386 

Uses  the  collected  materials  of  others  in  drawing 
a  portrait  in  the  realistic  style.  Tries  to  connect 
the  homely  but  apt  illustrations  of  Mr.  Lincoln 
with  the  acts  and  scenes  of  his  life. 

Morgan,  J.    Abraham  Lincoln,  the  boy  and 
the  man.     1908.  C  18305 

Plain,  straightforward  story  of  the  essentials  of 
Lincoln's  life,-his  struggles  and  achievements. 

Morse,  J.  T.     Abraham  Lincoln.     1899. 

.    C  11199 

In  the  "American  Statesmen"  series,  and  the 
best  short  life,  concise  and  reliable. 

Nicolay,  J.  G.     Short  life  of  Abraham  Lin- 
coln.    1902.  C  18392 

So  well  done  that  no  one  ignorant  of  its  rela- 
tion to  the  larger  book  would  suspect  it  of  being 
an  abridgement. — Nation. 

Nicolay,  J.  G.  and  Hay,  J.     Abraham  Lin- 
coln.    1901.  C 11202 

The_  great  ten-volume  work  called  by  the  Nation 
"certainly  one  of  the  most  notable  literary  works 
of  our  time."  An  interesting  account  of  its  writ- 
ing, covering  a  period  of  twenty  years,  is  given 
in  Thayer's  Life  of  John  Hay,  C  22348. 

Schurz,  C.    Abraham  Lincoln.    1891.  C  11204 

An    essay    which    quickly    became    a    classic    of 

English    style.       First    published    as    a    review    of 

Nicolay    and    Hay,    by    the    greatest    of    German 

Americans. 

Tarbell,  I.   M.     Life  of  Abraham   Lincoln. 
1909.  C  18387 

First  published  as  a  magazine  serial;  rich  in 
anecdotes  and  pictures  and  contains  much  original 
human  interest,  though  not  always  vital,  material. 


Whitlock,   B.     Abraham   Lincoln.     1909.

of  Lincoln."  —  Nation.

Hardin  Square  Branch 

The  twentieth  park  field  house  branch 
library  was  opened  for  service  at  Hardin 
Square,  Wentworth  avenue  and  Twenty- 
Sixth  street,  on  Saturday,  January  27th. 
It  is  housed  in  a  separate  building  pro- 
vided by  the  Commissioners  of  the  South 
Park,  conveniently  situated  near  the  park 
entrance  and  most  practically  arranged 
for  library  work.  For  the  opening  day 
the  Commissioners  loaned  a  profusion 
of  plants  from  the  park  greenhouses, 
which,  setting  off  the  new  furniture  and 
brightly  bound  books,  combined  to  make 
the  handsome  interior  of  the  branch  still 


18 


CHICAGO   PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


more  attractive.  About  2500  volumes 
have  been  placed  on  the  shelves  and  the 
circulation  for  the  first  week  showed  the 
genuine  appreciation  which  the  branch  is 
receiving  from  the  community.  Miss 
Kate  B.  Chase  is  the  librarian  of  the  new 
branch. 


The  Cherry  Tree  Story 

The  Library  owns  a  dingy,  time- 
stained  little  volume  entitled :  The  Life 
of  George  Washington;  with  Curious 
Anecdotes,  equally  honourable  to  him- 
self and  Exemplary  to  his  Young 
Countrymen.  .  .  .  By  M.  L. 
Weems,  formerly  Rector  of  Mount 
Vernon  Parish. 

Our  copy  is  one  of  the  fourteenth 
edition,  printed  in  Philadelphia  in 
the  year  of  the  battle  of  Waterloo, 
and  is  not  intrinsically  very  valuable, 
but  the  little  book  bears  the  distinction 
of  being  the  source  whence  the  fa- 
mous story  of  the  Cherry  Tree  was 
launched  upon  the  world.  The  Rev. 
Mason  L.  Weems,  self-styled  Rector 
of  Mount  Vernon  Parish,  although 
there  was  no  such  parish,  was  one  of 
hundreds  of  clergymen  who  observed 
the  first  Birthday  of  Washington  fol- 
lowing his  death  by  preaching  a  me- 
morial sermon.  This  formed  the  basis 
of  the  Life,  which  was  amplified 
through  succeeding  editions  by  the  in- 
sertion of  stories,  traditions  and  such 
miscellaneous  information  as  came  lo 
the  author's  notice.  The  Cherry  Tree 
story  does  not  appear  until  the  book 
had  passed  through  several  printings, 
and  whether  the  tale  was  wholly  in- 
vented or,  as  Parson  Weems  stoutly 
declares,  was  communicated  to  him 
"twenty  years  ago  by  an  aged  lady, 
who  was  a  distant  relative,"  has  never 
been  clearly  determined.  Several  others 
of  the  moral  tales  attached  by  Weems  to 
the  youthful  Washington  have  been 
traced  to  other  origins,  but  this,  the  most 
famous  of  them  all,  appears  to  have  had 
no  other  source  than  the  mind  or  the 
memory  of  our  author.  The  common  ac- 
ceptance of  the  truth  of  this  body  of 


quaint  legends,  at  all  events,  had  much 
to  do  with  the  popular  conception  of 
Washington  as  a  character  of  superhu- 
man excellences  and  priggish  goodness. 
The  Cherry  Tree  story  appears  on 
page  13  of  the  copy  in  the  Library  and  is 
given  below  as  printed,  ostensibly  in  the 
words  of  the  above  mentioned  "aged 
lady": 

"When  George,"  said  she,  "was  about 
six  years  old,  he  was  made  the  wealthy 
master  of  a  hatchet!  of  which,  like  most 
little  boys,  he  was  immoderately  fond; 
and  was  constantly  going  about  chopping 
every  thing  that  came  in  his  way.  One 
day,  in  the  garden,  where  he  often  amused 
himself  hacking  his  mother's  pea-sticks, 
he  unluckily  tried  the  edge  of  his  hatchet 
on  the  body  of  a  beautiful  young  English 
cherry-tree,  which  he  barked  so  terribly, 
that  I  don't  believe  the  tree  ever  got  the 
better  of  it.  The  next  morning  the  old 
gentleman,  finding  out  what  had  befallen 
his  tree,  which,  by  the  by,  was  a  great 
favourite,  came  into  the  house;  and  with 
much  warmth  asked  for  the  mischievous 
author,  declaring  at  the  same  time,  that 
he  would  not  have  taken  five  guineas  for 
his  tree.  Nobody  could  tell  him  any  thing 
about  it.  Presently  George  and  his 
hatchet  made  their  appearance.  'George,' 
said  his  father,  'do  you  know  who  killed 
that  beautiful  little  cherry  tree  yonder  in 
the  garden?'  This  was  a  tough  question; 
and  George  staggered  under  it  for  a  mo- 
ment; but  quickly  recovered  himself:  and 
looking  at  his  father,  with  the  sweet  face 
of  youth  brightened  with  the  inexpressible 
charm  of  all-conquering  truth,  he  bravely 
cried  out,  'I  can't  tell  a  lie,  Pa;  you 
know  I  can't  tell  a  lie.  I  did  cut  it  with 
my  hatchet.' — Run  to  my  arms,  you' 
dearest  boy,  cried  his  father  in  transports, 
run  to  my  arms;  glad  am  I,  George,  that 
you  killed  my  tree;  for  you  have  paid  me 
for  it  a  thousand  fold.  Such  an  act  of 
heroism  in  my  son  is  more  worth  than  a 
thousand  trees,  though  blossomed  with 
silver,  and  their  fruits  of  purest  gold." 


Holiday  Hours 

On  Lincoln's  Birthday,  Monday,  Feb- 
ruary 12th,  and  on  Washington's  Birth- 
day, Thursday,  February  22d,  the  cir- 
culating department  of  the  central  build- 
ing will  be  closed.  Reading  and  refer- 
ence rooms  will  be  open  from  9  a.  m.  to 
6  p.  m.  All  branch  libraries  will  be  open 
from  2  to  6  p.  m.

Books  Added  to  the  Library 


Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those  marked  Doc.  eie  in 
the  Document  department.    *Ser.  lepresents  serials  which  do  not  circulate.

March  1917 


Chicago  Public  Library 


Book  Bulletin 


Chicago  Public  Library 

Book  Bulletin 

Volume  7.  March,  1917  No.  3 

The  Market  Value  of  Old  Books 

That  old  books  must  needs  be  worth  much  money  merely  because  they  are  old 
appears  to  be  one  of  the  commonest  and  most  persistent  of  popular  delusions. 
Scarcely  a  week  passes  which  does  not  bring  to  any  public  library  at  least  one 
anxious  inquirer  about  the  price  of  an  old  book.  The  precious  volume  is  brought 
in,  swathed  in  voluminous  wrappings  which  are  unfolded  simultaneously  with  its 
family  history.  It  is  always  a  very  old  book,  having  lain  in  an  ancient  trunk  in 
the  garret  for  many  years.  It  was  brought  hither  by  a  venturesome  immigrant  an- 
cestor. It  is  a  large,  ponderous,  shabby,  age-browned  volume,  probably  a  Bible, 
in  English,  German,  Scandinavian,  occasionally  in  Italian,  Bohemian,  Russian  or 
Polish.  Its  date  is  almost  always  in  the  seventeenth  or  eighteenth  century,  and 
one  glance  at  the  title  page  usually  suffices  to  confirm  the  librarian  in  his  premoni- 
tion that  he  is  once  again  confronted  with  the  thankless  task  of  reducing  to  dross  a 
supposititious  treasure  around  which  family  legends  have  spun  a  web  of  gold  for 
generations.  During  the  past  twenty-five  years  probably  no  fewer  than  five  hun- 
dred such  volumes  have  been  brought  to  the  Chicago  Public  Library  for  appraisal, 
but  only  one  was  ever  discovered  which  could  be  honestly  valued  at  more  than 
twenty  dollars.  That  one  was  a  dingy  little  Dutch  Bible,  itself  of  no  intrinsic 
worth  whatever,  but  bound  in  heavy  covers  of  solid  silver  marvellously  carved 
and  beaten  by  some  devout  and  inspired  worker  in  the  elder  days  of  art. 

The  truth  is  that  practically  no  book  printed  later  than  the  beginning  of  the 
sixteenth  century,  or,  say,  1520  to  be  safe,  is  worth  money  simply  by  reason  of  its 
years.  Indeed,  many  of  the  books  printed  within  the  period  indicated  are  worth  no 
more  than  modern  works  of  similar  size  and  workmanship  would  be;  in  many 
cases  not  so  much.  The  first  book  ever  printed  on  a  press  with  movable  type, 
known  as  the  Gutenberg  Bible  and  printed  somewhere  between  1445  and  1450 — the 
first  fruits  of  one  of  the  very  greatest  of  man's  inventions — is  worth  a  king's  ran- 
som. A  perfect  copy  was  sold  quite  recently  for  $50,000.  But  the  stately,  beau- 
tifully printed  Bible,  now  in  this  Library,  which  issued  from  the  press  of  the  Ko- 
bergers  in  Nuremberg  in  1479,  hardly  thirty  years  later  and  fourteen  years  before 
the  discovery  of  America,  would  probably  bring  not  more  than  fifty  dollars  at  a  sale. 
There  is  only  one  "first  printed  book."  Not  its  age  but  its  primacy  is  the  factor 
conditioning  its  present  worth.  During  the  fifty  years  after  its  appearance  print- 
ing spread  so  rapidly  and  books  were  multiplied  in  such  numbers  that  it  is  not  with- 
out reason  that,  the  poor  calligraphers,  robbed  of  their  occupation  by  this  new  de- 
vice of  typography  earnestly  condemned  it  as  a  "black  art."  And  today  there  is 
no  great  difficulty  in  picking  up  well-preserved  examples  of  books  produced  in  the 
"cradle-time"  of  printing,  and  hence  called  by  the  Greek  word  incunabula,  at  prices 
that  compare  very  favorably  with  current  quotations  for  first  editions  of  O.  Henry. 


30  CHICAGO   PUBLIC  LIBRARY 

The  Chicago  Public  Library  possesses  three  incunabula :  the  Bible  of  1479,  already 
mentioned,  a  Golden  Legend  of  1483,  and  a  Nuremberg  Chronicle  with  German 
text,  dated  1493.  The  latter  work  bears  the  added  distinction  of  being  among  the 
first  printed  books  in  which  illustrations  were  introduced  into  pages  of  letter  press. 
Yet  it  is  quite  safe  to  say  that  these  three  books  together  are  not  worth  over  $200. 

Books  become  desirable,  then  scarce,  then  rare,  in  proportion  as  they  are  de- 
sired by  many  people  at  the  same  time  and  with  the  means  of  gratifying  their  de- 
sires. There  are  many  books  that  are  desired  by  many  people  for  many  and  divers 
reasons,  but  the  mere  age  of  a  book  does  not  happen  to  be  one  of  them.  It  is  quite 
correct  to  say  "happen  to  be,"  for  among  the  divers  reasons  which  cause  all  sorts 
and  conditions  of  books  to  be  desired  and  collected  there  are  a  number  that  are 
purely  whimsical,  eccentric,  accidental  or  idiosyncratic.  Nevertheless,  the  mere  age 
of  a  book  still  does  not  happen  to  be  one  of  them.  Examples  of  this  kind  of  book 
collecting  are  the  various  English  Bibles  which,  by  the  perversity  of  chance,  have 
incurred  ambiguous  fame  because  of  certain  typographical  errors  undetected  by 
the  printer,  often  to  his  grievous  undoing.  The  He  and  She  Bibles,  the  two  earliest 
issues  of  King  James's  version,  printed  in  1611,  and  literally  the  parents  of  all  our 
modern  English  Bibles  are  so  distinguished  because  those  printed  from  one  original 
give  a  part  of  the  15th  verse  of  the  third  chapter  of  the  book  of  Ruth  as:  "He 
went  into  the  city,"  while  those  following  another  prototype  have  it:  "She  went 
into  the  city."  Yet  copies  of  these  books,  despite  the  fact  that,  in  addition  to  this 
oddity,  they  really  constitute  as  important  a  monument  in  the  literary  history  of  the 
Scriptures  as  the  Gutenberg  Bible  does  in  the  history  of  printing,  can  be  bought 
without  much  difficulty  at  a  price  ranging  from  twenty-five  to  seventy-five  dollars, 
according  to  the  enthusiasm  of  the  purchaser.  The  famous  Breeches  Bible  of  1560. 
so  named  because  of  its  description  of  the  first  garments  fashioned  in  the  Garden 
of  Eden  as  "breeches"  instead  of  aprons,  commands  a  similar  price.  The  Wicked 
Bible,  printed  in  1631,  which  omits  a  highly  essential  "not"  from  one  of  the  Ten 
Commandments,  is  not  much  more  costly,  although  this  volume  is  rare  in  a  very 
literal  sense,  only  a  few  copies  having  escaped  the  prompt  destruction  of  the  whole 
edition  on  the  part  of  the  scandalized  authorities.  The  Bug  Bible,  Vinegar  Bible, 
and  others,  "collected  for  their  faults,"  are  obtainable  at  much  lower  figures. 

Rarity  alone,  however,  besides  being  an  exceedingly  variable  quantity,  is  by  no 
means  a  prime  factor  in  enhancing  the  market  value  of  an  old  book.  With  it  there 
must  be  demand.  There  are  plenty  of  sixteenth,  seventeenth  and  eighteenth  cen- 
tury books  of  which  only  a  very  small  number  of  copies  exist;  probably  a  good 
many  of  which  only  a  single  copy  has  survived.  But  if  they  do  not  happen  to  be- 
long to  one  of  the  classes  which  are  collected  for  one  reason  or  another,  the  acci- 
dent of  having  outlived  their  generation  and  such  usefulness  as  they  may  have  pos- 
sessed will  not  avail  to  render  them  desirable.  They  are  simply  old  books,  and  like 
old  garments,  utensils  and  other  relics  of  other  days,  they  are  merely  discarded  and 
despised. 

Meanwhile  the  fascinating  occupation  of  collecting  old  books  which  appeal  to 
the  motives,  tastes  and  interests  of  collectors  continues  to  flourish,  and,  as  has  been 
said,  such  motives,  tastes  and  interests  are  many  and  various.  Chief,  perhaps, 
among  the  elements  making  a  book  desirable  is  the  quality  of  "firstness."  The  first 
printed  book ;  the  first  printed  in  England,  France,  Italy,  Holland,  America ;  the  en- 
tire product  of  the  first  presses,  these  are  eagerly  sought  and  bring  high  prices.  The 
first  folios  of  Shakespeare,  miserably  printed,  bristling  with  misprints,  and  in  no 
way  distinguished  above  tons  of  other  books  issuing  from  Elizabethan  presses,  are 
among  the  two  or  three  most  precious  literary  jewels  of  the  world.  First  editions 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


31 


of  famous  authors  have  been  in  every  age  the  objects  of  desire  on  the  part  of  con- 
noisseurs. In  our  own  day  the  works  of  modern  writers,  even  of  contemporaries 
like  Mark  Twain,  Walt  Whitman,  Poe,  Lowell,  or  Joseph  Conrad,  H.  G.  Wells 
and  O.  Henry,  have  been  similarly  exalted.  Not  age,  therefore,  but  another  quality 
of  far  more  compelling  interest  conditions  the  demand  for  this  class  of  books.  Sec- 
ondly, the  attribute  of  beauty  of  execution  or  embellishment ;  of  type,  paper  format 
and  binding  has  supplied  the  impulse  to  many  a  famous  collection.  Splendid  books 
were  printed  with  unapproachable  skill,  taste  and  ingenuity  in  the  very  infancy  of 
the  craft,  and  these,  with  their  successors,  a  royal  line  progressing  through  all  the 
generations,  not  omitting  the  present,  have  been  coveted  by  book  lovers  of  all  time. 
From  Johann  Gutenberg  to  William  Morris  and  beyond,  printers  who  were  artists 
first  and  craftsmen  afterwards  have  added  to  the  world's  scant  store  of  beauty, 
and  the  products  of  their  handiwork  have  not  wanted  for  admiration  and  have  not 
failed  to  arouse  the  desire  to  possess  them.  Again  it  is  not  age  but  an  element 
of  a  sublimer  sort  which  determines  the  position  of  such  books  in  the  esteem  of 
bibliophiles.  Books  associated  with  famous  personages,  especially  if  they  bear  the 
marks  of  ownership  of  such  personages,  are  highly  prized  by  many  collectors.  Nar- 
ratives of  early  explorations,  particularly  of  travels  to  or  on  the  American  conti- 
nent, together  with  early  historical  tracts,  Indian  histories,  and  all  the  great  group 
known  as  Americana  have  for  a  long  time  enjoyed  a  vogue  not  in  all  cases,  perhaps, 
justified  by  any  merits  they  possess. 

These  and  many  similar  points  of  extraneous  interest  have  caused  many  a 
humble  volume  to  be  cherished  above  its  deserts.  Fashions  in  book  collecting  have 
waxed  and  waned  as  in  other  human  affairs.  Many  books  have  been  collected  for 
reasons  that  are  sound  and  worthy  by  bibliophiles,  and  many  others  for  reasons 
neither  worthy  nor  sound  by  those  who  were  merely  bibliomaniacs.  But,  except 
for  a  very  few  of  the  oldest  books,  the  element  of  age  has  not  happened  to  be 
among  the  reasons  impelling  either  the  one  or  the  other  class  of  book  collectors  to 
collect  old  books.

William  DeMorgan 
The  interesting  excerpt  from  an  au- 
tograph letter  of  William  DeMorgan 
which  we  reproduce  on  the  inside  front 
cover  page  was  courteously  loaned  to 
the  Library  by  Mr.  George  S.  Seymour, 
of  Chicago,  to  whom  the  letter  was 
written  in  acknowledgment  of  some 
original  verses  addressed  to  Mr.  De- 
Morgan.  It  calls  to  mind  the  recent 
death  of  the  author  and  his  remarkable 
success  as  a  novelist,  though  his  first 
book  was  written  only  ten  years  ago, 
when  he  was  already  sixty-six  years 
old.  Following  is  a  list  of  his  novels : 
An  affair  of  dishonor.  1910.  F 12402 

Alice-for-short.     1907.  F 12403 

It  never  can  happen  again.     1909.        F  12404 
Joseph    Vance.      1906.  F 12405 

A  likely  story.     1911.  F 12406 

Somehow  good.     1908.  F 12407 

When  ghost  meets  ghost.    1914.          F  12408 


Hiram  Kelly  Branch,  a  picture  of 
which  forms  our  cover  illustration,  is 
situated  at  the  corner  of  Normal  Boule- 
vard and  Sixty-second  Street  in  Engle- 
wood.  It  was  built  from  the  accumu- 
lated income  of  a  trust  fund,  the  gift  of 
the  public-spirited  citizen  whose  name 
it  commemorates  and  is  typical  of  mod- 
ern branch  library  architecture.  In  the 
plan  for  reorganizing  the  library  serv- 
ice for  the  whole  city,  Hiram  Kelly 
Branch  will  serve  as  one  of  the  five 
large  regional  branches.

Book  Bulletin 


THE  VOICES  or  THE  DUNES 
From  the  original  etching  by  Earl  H.  Reea 


THE  VOICES  OF  THE  DUNES 

MUSIC  is  everywhere: 
No  place  so  bleak  and  bare 
But  hath  a  gentle  spirit 
To  sustain  and  cheer  it; 
And  even  these  sad  dunes 
Have  their  enchanting  tunes 
That  crowd  the  seasons  full — 
The  wind,  the  wave,  the  gull, 
And  here  and  there  some  grass, 
Sighing  as  zephyrs  pass. 

So,  even  Grief  doth  find 
Some  solace  for  her  mind ; 
She  hath  her  music,  too, 
Although  its  notes  are  few— 
The  fountain  of  her  tears 
And  some  few  hopes  and  fears 
That  sigh,  or  sweetly  croon 
Some  dear  remembered  rune, 
That  once,  ere  life  turned  gray, 
Was  Love's  own  roundelay. 

Charles  G.  Bland  en. 


Chicago  Public  Library 

Book  Bulletin 

Volume  7  April,  1917  No.  -4 

A  Procession  of  Wild  Flowers 

Beginning  this  month  specimens  of  the  wild  flowers  native  in  the  Chicago 
region  will  be  displayed  suitably  in  the  exhibition  corridors  on  the  fourth  floor 
of  the  Main  building,  Washington  street  side.  The  exhibit  will  be  furnished  by 
a  Committee  of  the  Chicago  Woman's  Club,  and  will  comprise  such  floral  repre- 
sentation as  the  season  progressively  affords.  Mrs.  John  Sherman  is  chairman 
of  the  forestry  class  of  the  Club,  who  will  be  responsible  for  the  exhibit.  As 
planned,  it  will  extend  from  early  spring,  when  the  hepaticas  and  violets  first 
appear  until  in  the  fall  the  wild  sage  prophesies  the  coming  sleeping  time  of 
plant  life. 

In  connection  with  the  flower  exhibit,  the  Library  proposes  to  have  illus- 
trative material  on  adjacent  bulletin  boards.  It  is  planned  by  its  sponsors  to 
make  the  exhibit  the  nucleus  of  a  campaign  to  save  wild  flowers  from  exter- 
mination through  indiscriminate  gathering. 

Concerning  the  Chicago  movement  for  the  conservation  of  the  wild  flowers 
in  the  neighborhood  of  Chicago,  Miss  Lena  M.  McCauley  writes  as  follows : 

Petitions  to  florists  to  discourage  the  sale  of  spring  wild  flowers  are  being 
circulated  by  the  Wild  Flower  Preservation  Society  of  America,  Chicago  Chapter. 
Mrs.  Charles  F.  Millspaugh,  in  charge  of  the  movement,  states  that  dealers  and 
thousands  of  club  women  are  signing  these  petitions  and  giving  the  protective 
movement  for  wild  flowers  hearty  support.  The  publicity  work  gives  hope  that 
by  the  time  spring  beauties,  blood  root  and  shooting  stars  are  in  bloom  no  dealer 
in  Chicago  will  offer  them  for  sale.  So  thoro  has  been  the  devastation  of  wild 
flower  haunts  by  children  gathering  flowers  to  sell  to  city  florists  that  whole 
sections  have  been  robbed  and  species  destroyed  never  to  grow  again. 

Golfers  are  warned  that  their  golf  courses  are  being  despoiled  of  their  nat- 
tural  charms.  The  strips  of  woods,  banks  of  ravines  and  hedgerows  were 
adorned  with  anemones,  hepaticas,  marsh  marigolds,  columbines,  mandrakes  and 
trilliums.  The  coming  spring  will  reveal  the  absence  of  the  flowers  pulled  up  by 
boys  who  roam  the  courses  or  by  the  caddies  who  are  hired  by  florists'  agents 
from  the  city.  It  is  very  easy  to  exterminate  the  spring  flowers.  Violets  have 
strong  rootings  which  enable  them  to  survive  longer,  but  the  millions  of  violets 
taken  to  town  will  soon  kill  out  the  plants. 


46 


CHICAGO   PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


The  Conservation  Council  composed  of  delegates  from  fourteen  clubs,  three 
of  which  are  state  districts  of  the  Illinois  Federation  of  Women's  Clubs,  and 
others  the  big  nature-loving  organizations,  the  Prairie  Club,  the  Outdoor  Art 
League,  the  Audubon  Society  of  Illinois,  the  Geographic  Society  of  Chicago,  the 
floriculture  department  of  the  Ridge  Woman's  Club,  the  Municipal  Art  League, 
the  Arche  Club  and  the  West  End  Woman's  Club  and  the  Horticultural  So- 
ciety of  Chicago  are  interesting  their  members  in  this  movement  to  save  the  wild 
flowers  and  shrubs  from  wanton  waste.  The  Friends  of  Our  Native  Landscape 
are  active.  The  spring  Sunday  tripper  will  start  for  the  woods  in  a  few  weeks 
to  uproot  flowers  and  tear  branches  from  the  wild  crab  apple  trees,  which, 
withering  soon,  will  be  thrown  away  to  clutter  paths  and  the  floors  of  railway 
cars. 

To  educate  the  ignorant  flower  picker,  the  signs  committee  of  the  conserva- 
tion council,  Miss  Catherine  Mitchell  of  Riverside,  chairman,  is  posting  warn- 
ings at  railway  stations  to  the  dunes,  the  Des  Plaines  and  elsewhere.  Residents 
along  the  north  shore  whose  grounds  are  robbed  of  wild  flowers  by  automo- 
bilists  are  erecting  signs.  Penalties  are  to  be  exacted  by  local  police.  The  Out- 
door Art  League  is  distributing  warnings  from  the  Wild  Flower  societies  to  chil- 
dren in  the  schools,  who  must  be  taught  to  respect  flowers  and  birds.

American  Indian  Day 
SATURDAY,  MAY  12xH. 

While  not  a  "legal"  holiday,  American 
Indian  Day,  the  second  annual  celebra- 
tion of  which  will  occur  on  the  second 
Saturday  in  May,  was  set  apart  at  the 
request  of  the  Society  of  American  In- 
dians, which  calls  upon  "every  person 
of  American  Indian  ancestry  to  specially 
observe  this  day  as  one  set  apart  as  a 
memorial  to  the  red  race,  and  a  wise 
consideration  of  its  future."  Many 
white  schools  and  communities  have 
taken  up  the  suggestion  and  the  follow- 
ing list  may  be  helpful  in  planning  exer- 
cises : 

Indian  Pageants

in*  activities.    1915).  K  30906

Book  Bulletin 


THE  ROTUNDA,  T.  E.  BLACKSTONE  BRANCH 


JOIN  THE  RED  CROSS 

TO  AID  in  the  campaign  for  the 
enlistment  of  new  members  of 
the  Red  Cross  in  Chicago,  the  Public 
Library  has  established  enrollment 
stations  in  the  Central  Library  and  in 
all  branches,  in  charge  of  members  of 
the  Library  staff.  Patrons  of  the  Li- 
brary are  invited  to  avail  themselves  of 
these  stations  in  joining  this  great  vol- 
unteer relief  agency,  which  is  operated 
under  a  national  charter  and  of  which 
the  President  of  the  United  States  is 
the  head. 

No  other  agency  provides  so  large, 
so  practical  and  so  comprehensive  an 
opportunity  for  service  in  this  time  of 
crisis.  Membership  is  divided  into 
six  classes.  Registration  and  payment 
of  the  fee  are  the  only  conditions  of 
membership.  No  field  service  is  re- 
quired of  members. 


CLASSES  OF  MEMBERSHIP 

Annual  Member I] 

Subscribing  Member annually 

Contributing    Member..  .  .annually 
Sustaining  Member.  . . ...  .annually 

Life  Member (one  payment) 

Patron  (life)  Member  (do.) 


;     1 

2 

5 
10 

25 
100 


Enrollment  blanks  and  all  informa- 
tion can  be  had  at  the  stations  above 
mentioned. 


Volume  7  May,  1917  No.  5 


The  Library  and  the  War 

At  its  last  meeting  the  Board  of  Directors  adopted  the  following  resolutions  : 

Resolved,  That  the  Board  of  Directors  of  the  Chicago  Public  Library  hereby 
makes  formal  tender  of  the  buildings  resources  and  equipment  of  the  Library  to  the 
United  States  government  for  any  purpose  or  use  deemed  necessary  and  proper  in 
the  present  crisis;  to  become  a  distributing  station  for  official  literature,  military 
and  naval  instruction  manuals  or  other  material,  to  serve  as  a  registration  center 
for  any  activities  properly  or  conveniently  administered  from  or  in  the  Library 
building  or  in  any  other  way  conformable  to  and  not  unduly  interfering  with  its 
own  public  activities,  to  contribute  toward  the  general  organization  of  the  national 
forces. 

That  the  sum  of  $2,500  be  set  aside  from  the  Book  Fund  for  the  purchase 
of  drill  manuals  and  other  books  on  military  organization  and  instruction  in  such 
quantities  as  may  be  found  to  be  necessary,  and  that  the  War  Department  be 
requested  to  recommend  books  which  should  be  made  available  to  the  citizens  to 
fit  them  for  military  or  other  national  duty  in  this  emergency. 
Also  the  following: 

Resolved,  That  the  Board  tender  its  good  offices  and  that  of  its  employees  in 
promotion  of  the  campaign  for  enrollment  of  members  in  the  Red  Cross  Society, 
and  that  the  Librarian  be  instructed  to  make  the  necessary  arrangements  whereby 
the  branch  librarians  may  serve  in  this  capacity  in  the  enrollment  of  members  who 
make  application  at  the  branch  libraries. 

Immediately  following  the  declaration  of  war,  the  demand  for  books  on  military 
training  in  the  various  branches  of  the  service  became  urgent.  The  normal  supply, 
in  the  book  stores  as  well  as  in  the  Library,  was  soon  exhausted  and  inquiries  and 
emergency  orders  hastily  sent  to  publishers  met  with  only  partial  response.  The 
Public  Library  has,  however,  succeeded  in  assembling  what  is  believed  to  be  a 
representative  collection  of  the  most  useful  books  on  the  several  topics  interesting 
to  the  professional  as  well  as  the  citizen  soldier.  The  list  as  given  below  is  based  upon 
the  official  classified  list  of  required  and  recommended  books  of  the  U.  S.  Military 
Academy,  issued  in  1916.  To  this  have  been  added  titles  of  newer  books  of  value, 
as  well  as  some  works  of  general  interest  on  the  various  aspects  of  war. 


58 


CHICAGO   PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


While  most  of  these  books  were  already  in  the  Library  the  supply  of  copies  has 
been  greatly  increased ;  from  fifty  to  one  hundred  and  twenty-five  copies  of  each  of 
the  important  titles  were  added  to  the  stock  as  soon  as  they  could  be  procured.  Some 
of  these  were  distributed  among  the  branches  but  most  of  them  are  to  be  had  at  the 
main  Library. 

In  addition  the  Library  has  a  complete  file  of  the  United  States  government 
military  publications,  the  most  authoritative  and  useful  of  all.  Extra  copies  were 
immediately  ordered  from  Washington,  but  have  been  slow  in  arriving  because  of 
the  tremendous  demands  upon  the  government  printing  office.  Such  extra  copies  of 
government  manuals  as  have  been  received  have  been  set  aside  for  circulation.  One 
copy  of  each  of  these  manuals  is  kept  on  the  shelves  for  reference  use  only. 

The  following  lists  are  complete  to  the  date  of  compilation.  Other  works  are 
being  received  on  delayed  orders,  and  new  titles  will  be  ordered  as  required  from 
time  to  time,  and  these  will  be  included  in  subsequent  lists.  Meanwhile,  information 
regarding  them  can  be  had  by  personal  inquiry  at  the  information  desk,  third  floor, 
Main  Library.

The  Municipal  Flag 

By  ordinance  adopted  by  the  City 
Council  April  4,  a  municipal  flag  designed 
by  Wallace  Rice  was  adopted  for  Chi- 
cago. While  the  municipal  flag  is  made 
the  official  flag  of  the  several  departments 
of  the  city,  each  department  may  place 
upon  any  flag  intended  for  its  own  use, 
the  appropriate  symbol  or  emblem  de- 
signating such  department.  That  symbol 
for  the  Public  Library  is  designated  as 
follows:  "An  open  book,  gold  on  a  pur- 
ple roundel." 

The  ordinance  thus  describes  the  mu- 
nicipal flag :  "The  Chicago  municipal  flag 
shall  be  white,  with  two  blue  bars,  each 
taking  up  a  sixth  of  its  space,  and  set  a 
little  less  than  one-sixth  of  the  way  from 
the  top  and  bottom  of  the  flag  respec- 
tively. There  shall  be  two  bright  red 
stars  with  sharp  points;  six  in  number, 
set  side  by  side  close  together,  next  the 
staff  in  the  middle  third  of  the  surface 
of  the  flag." 

Holiday  Hours 

On  Decoration  Day,  Wednesday,  May 
30th,  the  circulating  department  of  the 
Main  Library  will  be  closed  all  day; 
reference  and  reading  rooms  will  be  open 
from  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.  Branches  will 
be  open  during  the  afternoon,  from  2  to  6 
o'clock. 


62 


CHICAGO    PUBLIC   LIBRARY 


As  Others  See  Us 


THE  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 

Editorial  in  Chicago  Tribune,  April  20, 

19*7- 

As  long  as  the  Chicago  public  library 
was  merely  a  stack  of  books  and  its  li- 
brarians were  caretakers,  it  made  little 
difference  to  the  public  whether  the  li- 
brary had  a  large  income  or  a  small. 
More  books  were  bought  or  fewer  books 
were  bought. 

The  library  to-day  is  something  entirely 
different.  It  is  mostly  service.  It  has 
built  up  a  system  of  offering  books  to 
the  public  in  the  most  convenient  way. 
The  library  is  a  live  thing.  It  is  not 
a  museum. 

Thus  its  present  shortage  of  income 
means  destruction  to  its  usefulness,  not  a 
mere  cessation  of  development.  Under 
the  Juul  law  its  income  was  scaled  down 
to  .588  of  a  mill  for  every  dollar  of  as- 
sessed value.  The  library  board  is  not 
guaranteed  any  minimum  taxing  power. 

The  state  senate  has  before  it  a  bill 
which  would  guarantee  it  a  minimum  tax 
rate  of  eight  tenths  of  a  mill.  Because 
the  Chicago  library  has  developed  itself 
into  one  of  the  most  vital  agencies  for 
good  citizenship  it  should  be  guaranteed 
an  adequate  income. 


PUBLIC  LIBRARY  REVENUES 
Editorial  in  Daily  News,  April  ip, 

One  of  Chicago's  governing  agencies 
which  serves  the  public  with  marked 
efficiency  is  the  public  library  board. 
That  body  is  progressive  in  its  policy  and 
seeks  continually  to  extend  its  facilities 
for  public  service  at  the  lowest  possible 


cost.  Its  revenues  are  inadequate,  how- 
ever, and  its  work  is  seriously  hampered 
in  consequence. 

The  library  board  in  a  peculiar  sense  is 
harmfully  affected  by  the  operation  of 
the  Juul  law,  as  that  operation  has  been 
affected  by  successive  enactments. 

The  taxing  powers  of  the  board,  like 
those  of  other  taxing  bodies,  are  limited 
in  a  particular  way.  Unlike  the  other 
agencies,  with  the  exception  of  the  Chi- 
cago sanitary  district,  the  library  board 
is  not  guaranteed  any  minimum  taxing 
power.  Consequently,  the  scaling  pro- 
cess applied  under  the  Juul  law  reduces 
its  revenues  nearly  one-half. 

The  maximum  tax  rate  for  public  li- 
brary purposes  originally  was  six-tenths 
of  a  mill  on  each  dollar  of  assessed  valua- 
tion. The  general  assembly  at  its  last 
regular  session  raised  the  rate  to  a  mill. 
Under  the  Juul  law  the  rate  for  1917  was 
scaled  to  .568  of  a  mill.  This  rate  was 
supposed  to  produce  a  revenue  of  $576,- 
335  but  the  actual  revenue  will  be  about 
$30,000  less  on  account  of  losses  in  col- 
lection and  costs  of  collection.  This  is 
about  $52,000  less  than  the  sum  which 
was  available  for  library  purposes  in 
1916. 

There  is  pending  in  the  senate  at 
Springfield  a  bill  which  by  its  terms 
would  give  the  Chicago  library  board  a 
minimum  tax  rate  of  eight-tenths  of  a 
mill.  If  any  increases  in  Chicago's  heavy 
tax  burdens  are  to  be  allowed,  appar- 
ently the  small  degree  of  protection 
asked  for  the  library  fund  should  be 
given  preference.

serials  •which  do  not  circulate.

CHICAGO    PUBLIC   LIBRARY 


Package  Libraries 


A  package  library  is  a  sheaf  or  packet 
of  clippings,  pamphlets,  public  docu- 
ments, articles  from  magazines  and  other 
loose  material  gathered  together  in  an 
envelope  or  folder.  It  is  intended  to  sup- 
ply the  demand  for  "quick-reference" 
material  and  date  too  recent  to  be  found 
in  books.  Its  chief  merit  is  timeliness 
and  when  it  has  outlived  that  quality  it 
can  be  discarded  or  superseded  without 
entailing  serious  loss.  The  Public  Li- 
brary maintains  a  constantly  growing  and 
changing  collection  of  package  libraries, 
especially  on  current  political,  economic 
and  sociological  topics,  with  some  others 
bearing  on  subjects  of  immediate  and 
urgent  popular  interest. 

The  package  libraries  can  be  obtained 
in  the  Civics  Department  or  through  the 
branches  and  stations  and  are  issued  on 
the  regular  library  card.  Ordinarily  but 
one  package  is  issued  to  a  borrower,  but 
teachers  may  by  special  arrangement  ob- 
tain more  than  one  for  the  use  of  their 
classes.

New  Library  Centers 


Two  new  branches  were  opened  during 
the  past  month,  both  on  the  northwest 
side,  one  at  the  Morse  School,  N.  Sawyer 
avenue  and  W.  Ohio  street,  and  the  other 
at  Kosciuszko  Park,  2732  Avers  avenue. 
This  makes  a  total  of  forty  branches. 

Eight  new  deposit  stations  were  estab- 
lished during  the  same  period,  in  con- 
formity with  the  plans  of  the  Board  to 
convert  the  old-fashioned  and  unsatis- 
factory delivery  stations  into  deposits 
where  a  limited  number  of  books  can  be 
kept  on  shelves  from  which  patrons  may 
make  their  selection  instead  of  sending  a 
list  of  catalog  numbers  to  the  Main  Li- 
brary and  waiting  for  the  return  delivery. 
The  plan  contemplates  the  ultimate  dis- 
continuation of  all  delivery  stations  and 


the  substitution  of  deposit  stations.  The 
first  eight,  now  open,  are : 

Association  House,  2150  W.  North 
Ave. 

Hegewisch  Traveling  Library,  13320 
Brandon  Ave. 

Jefferson  Park  Traveling  Library,  4774 
Milwaukee  Ave. 

Jewish  Educational  Alliance,  1243  N. 
Wood  St. 

Larrabee  Street  Traveling  Library, 
2004  Larrabee  St. 

Madison  Street  Traveling  Library, 
4216  W.  Madison  St. 

Montrose  Traveling  Library,  4410 
Milwaukee  Ave. 

Thirty-first  Street  Traveling  Library, 
304  E.  31st  St. 


CHICAGO    PUBLIC    LIBRARY 


Rules  and  Information  for  Patrons 


Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person 
residing  in  Cook  County  having  permanent 
employment  in  the  city,  may  enjoy  the  priv- 
ileges of  the  Library  by  having  a  resident 
of  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear  in  the  j 
city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon 
application  and  compliance  with  the  rules  j 
and  regulations  of  the  Board  of  Directors 
a  card  good  for  three  years  will  be  issued, 
which  will  permit  the  applicant  to  draw 
five  books  for  home  use. 

Teachers   are   accorded   special   privileges  j 
upon  application. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and 
may  be  once  renewed  for  the  same  period. 
Books  may  be  renewed  but  once.  Books 
of  recent  purchase  labeled  Seven-dav  books 
cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed.  The  card 
must  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 
out  or  renewed. 

Books  issued  at  Circulating  Centers  or 
Delivery  Stations  may  be  returned  at  the 
Main  Library,  but  books  cannot  be  applied 
for  at  the  Main  Library  and  delivered  to 
Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery  Stations. 

Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  hab- 
its mav  use  the  various  Reading  and  Refer- 
ence Rooms  of  the  Library.  No  card  is 


required  to  use  the  books  on  the  open 
shelves  or  the  books  in  the  stacks,  which 
must  be  applied  for. 

A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported 
to  the  Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books 
drawn  on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  pre- 
sented, and  for  all  fines  incurred.  If  a  card 
is  lost,  immediate  notice  must  be  given  to 
the  Library.  A  new  card  will  be  issued 
thirty  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  one  cent  a  day  on  each  work  of 
non-fiction,  and  three  cents  a  day  on  each 
work  of  fiction,  is  imposed  whether  bound 
in  one  or  more  volumes,  which  is  not  re- 
turned within  two  weeks  from  the  date  of 
issue.  An  additional  charge  of  two  cents 
is  made  for  each  notice  sent  for  the  return 
of  a  book,  and  no  other  books  will  be  de- 
livered to  the  person  incurring  the  fine  until 
it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the 
call  number  is  crossed  off  the  order  slip 
whenever  a  book  is  drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohib- 
ited, and  all  injuries  to  books  beyond  reas- 
onable wear  and  all  losses  must  be  promptly 
adjusted  to  the  satisfaction  of  the  Librarian. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours 
Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  De- 
partments, third  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:30 
p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Cata- 
logue, fourth  floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m., 
Sundays  and  holidays,  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People, 
fourth  floor,  open  9:30  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a,  m.  to 
5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room, 
first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street 
entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  closed 
on  Sunday. 


Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and 
newspapers,  fourth  floor,  Randolph  street 
entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays 
and  holidays,  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Music  Room,  fifth  floor,  Washington 
street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulle- 
tin are  issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and 
August,  and  are  distributed  free  of  charge 
in  all  departments  of  the  Main  Library  and 
in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be  mailed  to 
any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year  to 
cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published 
in  January  of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at 
ten  cents  per  copy.

Library  Sch, 


June  1917 


Chicago  Public  Library 


Book  Bulletin 


GEORGE  C.  WALKER  BRANCH 


HAVE  YOU  A  BOOK  TO  SPARE? 


The  Chicago  Public  Library  Invites 
Gifts  of  Books  for 

Soldiers'  Traveling  and  Knapsack  Libraries 

Books  donated  by  the  citizens  of  Chicago 
will  be  assembled  at  the  Library  and  made  up 
into  Traveling  and  Knapsack  Libraries  for  the 
Soldiers  in  trenches  and  in  field  and  the  Nurses 
in  camps  and  hospitals. 


Will  you  give  one  or  more  books  for  this  purpose? 


Leave  with  or  send  to  the  Branch 
Librarian  or  the  Main  Library,  marked 
"Books  for  Soldiers." 


Chicago  Public  Library 

Book  Bulletin 


Volume  7 


June,  1917 


No.   6 


Have  You  a  Book  to  Spare? 

The  appeal  issued  by  the  Library  under  the  above  head  is  printed  on  the 
inside  front  cover  page  of  this  number  of  the  Book  Bulletin.  It  has  been  spread 
broadcast  by  means  of  placards  and  with  the  generous  co-operation  of  the  news- 
papers, and  the  results  have  been  most  gratifying. 

At  this  writing  over  seven  thousand  books  have  been  given  to  this  cause 
by  citizens  of  Chicago.  Telephone  requests  to  call  for  books  in  quantities  too 
large  to  be  delivered  in  person  are  being  received  at  the  rate  of  from  a  dozen  to 
twenty  a  day.  Nearly  one  hundred  moving  picture  theaters  responded  favorably 
to  a  letter  asking  that  a  slide  prepared  by  the  Library  and  carrying  the  appeal 
be  shown  at  every  performance  for  a  month.  The  Library  hopes  and  fully 
expects  to  assemble  fifty  thousand  books. 

Distribution  is  made  through  the  Y.  M.  C.  A.,  to  whom  the  books  are 
being  turned  over  at  its  camp  buildings  in  numbers  proportioned  to  the  size  of 
the  encampment.  The  Association  assumes  custody  and  issues  them  to  appli- 
cants on  a  simple  loan  system.  The  books  are  regarded  as  free  gifts  from  the 
citizens  to  the  men  and  boys  in  the  service,  and  not  as  property  of  the  Library, 
which  relinquishes  all  claim  and  responsibility  when  they  are  delivered  to  the 
Y.  M.  C.  A.  officials.  The  only  mark  of  ownership  is  the  label  pasted  on  the 
cover : 


SOLDIERS'  LIBRARY 

Given  by  Citizens  of  Chicago 

Collected  by  the  Chicago   Public  Library 

Distributed  by  the  Y.  M.  C.  A. 


Handle  Carefully.     Return  Promptly 
Give  the  Next  Man  a  Chance 


PROPERTY   OF   THE    SOLDIERS'    LIBRARY 


The  first  delivery  was  made  by  Library  auto  truck  to  Fort  Sheridan  and  the 
Great  Lakes  Naval  Training  Station  on  Registration  Day,  June  5th.  Fifteen 
hundred  books,  packed  in  thirty  unit  boxes,  holding  fifty  each,  were  taken.  At 
Fort  Sheridan,  where  the  Reserve  Officers'  Training  Corps  is  stationed,  five 
hundred  books  were  delivered.  These  had  been  carefully  selected  with  regard  to 
the  average  age  and  educational  equipment  of  this  body  of  men,  mostly  college 
graduates.  The  remaining  thousand  books  of  the  initial  shipment,  of  a  lighter 
character,  adapted  to  the  lads  in  training  at  the  Great  Lakes  station,  were  then 


78 


CHICAGO   PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


conveyed  to  the  latter  place,  where  over  8,000  young  men  are  at  present 
encamped.  In  both  places  the  arrival  of  the  books  was  greeted  with  very  evi- 
dent appreciation.  Officials  of  the  Y.  M.  C.  A.  who  had  experience  at  the  Mexi- 
can border  were  enthusiastic  in  their  approval  of  the  idea,  and  emphatically 
declared  the  practical  importance  and  value  of  the  undertaking. 

One  of  the  most  gratifying  features  is  the  very  high  character  of  the  books 
received.  The  people  of  Chicago  are  evidently  giving  of  their  best,  and  their 
best  indicates  a  very  excellent  standard  of  taste.  Fiction  forms,  as  it  should, 
the  bulk  of  the  collection.  Practically  all  of  the  good  writers  are  represented,  from 
Dickens,  Scott  and  Thackeray  to  Winston  Churchill,  H.  G.  Wells,  Rex  Beach, 
Jack  London  and  Arnold  Bennett.  A  beautiful  set  of  Bret  Harte's  works,  and 
a  sumptuously  bound  edition  of  Dumas  have  been  received.  Well  illustrated 
works  of  travel  and  popular  history,  as  well  as  essays  and  poetry,  are  coming 
in  daily.  Experience  at  the  Mexican  border  shows  that  poetry  is  by  no  means 
the  least  popular  class  with  the  soldiers. 

Patrons  of  the  Library  who  have  not  already  made  contributions  are  invited 
to  do  so  as  soon  as  possible,  and  to  assist  in  making  the  movement  known  as 
widely  as  possible.  Indications  are  that  within  sixty  or  ninety  days  not  less 
than  250,000  men  will  have  assembled  at  various  stations  within  this  state.  It 
is  the  aim  of  the  Library  to  be  able  to  supply  books  in  the  ratio  of  one  volume 
for  every  five  men,  or  a  total  of  fifty  thousand  volumes.  The  co-operation  of 
all  interested  persons  is  urgently  asked  to  make  this  possible. 

It  should  be  noted  that  magazines,  bound  or  unbound,  are  also  acceptable. 
These,  however,  are  being  handled  by  the  Y.  M.  C.  A.  alone,  and  should  be 
sent  to  that  organization  and  not  to  the  Public  Library.

Legal  Holidays 

The  entire  Library  building  and  all 
branches  will  be  closed  on  Indepen- 
dence Day,  Wednesday,  July  4th. 

On  Labor  Day,  September  3d,  the 
reference  and  reading  rooms  will  be 
open  from  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.,  the 
branches  from  2  to  6  p.  m.,  while  the 
circulating  department  will  be  closed.

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those 

marked  Doc.  are  in  the  Document  department.  *Ser.  represents 

serials  which  do  not  circulate.

nence."
