CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Rules  and  Information  for  Patrons 


Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person 
residing  in  Cook  County  having  permanent 
employment  in  the  city,  may  enjoy  the  priv- 
ileges of  the  Library  by  having  a  resident 
of  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear  in  the 
city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon 
application  and  compliance  with  the  rules 
and  regulations  of  the  Board  of  Directors 
a  card  good  for  three  years  will  be  issued, 
which  will  permit  the  applicant  to  draw 
five  books  for  home  use. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges 
upon  application. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and 
may  be  once  renewed  for  the  same  period. 
Books  may  be  renewed  but  once.  Books 
of  recent  purchase  labeled  Seven-day  books 
cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed.  The  card 
must  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 
out  or  renewed. 

Books  issued  at  Circulating  Centers  or 
Delivery  Stations  may  be  returned  at  the 
Main  Library,  but  books  cannot  be  applied 
for  at  the  Main  Library  and  delivered  to 
Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery  Stations. 

Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  hab- 
its may  use  the  various  Reading  and  Refer- 
ence Rooms  of  the  Library.  No  card  is 


required  to  use  the  books  on  the  open 
shelves  or  the  books  in  the  stacks,  which 
must  be  applied  for. 

A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported 
to  the  Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books 
drawn  on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  pre- 
sented, and  for  all  fines  incurred.  If  a  card 
is  lost,  immediate  notice  must  be  given  to 
the  Library.  A  new  card  will  be  issued 
thirty  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  one  cent  a  day  on  each  work  of 
non-fiction,  and  three  cents  a  day  on  each 
work  of  fiction,  is  imposed  whether  bound 
in  one  or  more  volumes,  which  is  not  re- 
turned within  two  weeks  from  the  date  of 
issue.  An  additional  charge  of  two  cents 
is  made  for  each  notice  sent  for  the  return 
of  a  book,  and  no  other  books  will  be  de- 
livered to  the  person  incurring  the  fine  until 
it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the 
call  number  is  crossed  off  the  order  slip 
whenever  a  book  is  drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohib- 
ited, and  all  injuries  to  books  beyond  reas- 
onable wear  and  all  losses  must  be  promptly 
adjusted  to  the  satisfaction  of  the  Librarian. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  De- 
partments, third  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:30 
p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Cata- 
logue, fourth  floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m., 
Sundays  and  holidays,  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People, 
fourth  floor,  open  9:30  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a,  m.  to 
5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room, 
first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street 
entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  closed 
on  Sunday. 


Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and 
newspapers,  fourth  floor,  Randolph  street 
entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays 
and  holidays,  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Music  Room,  fifth  floor,  Washington 
street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulle- 
tin are  issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and 
August,  and  are  distributed  free  of  charge 
in  all  departments  of  the  Main  Library  and 
in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be  mailed  to 
any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year  to 
cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published 
in  January  of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at 
ten  cents  per  copy. 


September  1917 


Chicago  Public  Library 


Book  Bulletin 


Chicago  Public  Library 

Book  Bulletin 

Volume  7  September,  1917  No.  7 


Henry  E.  Legler 
1861-1917 


The  career  that  is  abruptly  closed  while  in  full  flight  presents  such  aspects 
of  tragedy  that  it  is  not  always  easy  to  recover  one's  balance  and  perspective 
in  the  face  of  so  severe  a  blow.  The  well-rounded  life  is  assumed  to  be  one 
composed  of  a  due  proportion  of  struggle  and  achievement  with  an  ample  mar- 
gin at  the  end  for  the  enjoyment  of  whatever  rewards,  spiritual,  intellectual  or 
material,  the  active  years  may  have  yielded. 

Those  who  knew  him  best  will  question  whether  the  late  Librarian  of  the 
Chicago  Public  Library  would  have  chosen — had  the  choice  been  his — to  live 
out  his  life  unto  such  a  fulfillment.  To  his  indomitable  spirit  obstacles  were 
only  an  inspiration  and  the  ever  increasing  pressure  of  the  task  merely  a  call 
to  doubled  and  redoubled  labors.  The  prospect  of  a  period  of  waning  powers 
and  enforced  relinquishment  of  effort  could  not  have  brought  him  pleasure. 
His  conception  of  the  responsibilities  to  which  he  was  called ;  the  idealism  and 
vision  which  he  brought  to  them  and  which  his  genius  for  execution  transmuted 
into  practical  development  of  the  library  system  in  Chicago,  were  too  large  to 
be  defined  by  any  physical  limitations.  The  time  would  never  have  come  when 
he  would  have  willingly  admitted  that  his  work  was  finished,  even  as  the  time 
never  came  when  he  would  admit  that  the  plans  and  projects  evolved  from  his 
tireless  brain  were  too  large  for  one  man  to  control  or  bring  to  fruition.  Un- 
willing to  share  his  burden  with  anyone,  unsparing  of  his  time  and  strength  to 
the  point  of  unreasonableness,  always  ready  to  do  more  work  and  face  new 
problems,  this  man  who  has  been  stricken  down  at  the  height  of  his  pow- 
ers and  the  climax  of  professional  achievement  could  not  have  confronted  a  leis- 
urely old  age — full  of  honors  though  it  must  have  been — with  anything  less 
than  the  dread  of  imprisonment.  His  daily  associates  are  grateful  that  such  a 
fate  was  mercifully  spared  him.  So  much  of  consolation,  at  any  rate,  may  be 
recovered  from  the  sorrow  and  despair  which  overwhelm  the  Board  of  Directors 
and  Staff  of  the  Chicago  Public  Library  at  the  passing  of  the  Chief. 

Henry  Eduard  Legler  was  the  son  of  a  Swiss-German  father  and  a  Sicilian 
mother,  and  was  born  in  Palermo,  February  22d,  1861.  In  his  physical  pro- 
portions, his  flashing  brown  eyes  and  swarthy  complexion — so  quickly  turning 


94  CHICAGO   PUBLIC  LIBRARY 

to  sallowness  in  ill  health — he  revealed  that  Italian  heritage  which  was  also 
strikingly  manifested  in  his  mental  traits.  The  quick  comprehension  and  swift 
decision  and  execution;  the  force  and  power  of  command,  and  the  fiery  temp- 
erament so  firmly  controlled  and  effectually  concealed  beneath  an  exterior  of 
singular  gentleness  and  suavity  bespoke  his  Latin  kinship.  Withal  a  dreamer, 
a  lover  of  beauty,  a  loyal  and  courageous  friend,  a  passionate  champion  of  hu- 
manity, his  life  presents  an  extraordinary  combination  of  qualities,  soft  and 
rugged,  stern  and  gentle,  practical  and  romantic,  all  blended  and  merged  into 
one  harmonious  entity  such  as  it  is  seldom  the  fortune  of  one  human  being 
to  possess. 

After  a  few  childhood  years  spent  in  Switzerland,  he  was  brought  by  his 
parents  to  America,  the  family  settling  in  LaCrosse,  Wis.,  where  he  attended 
the  public  schools.  The  death  of  his  father  cut  short  his  period  of  study  and 
turned  his  thoughts  toward  a  livelihood.  An  early  predilection  for  literary 
work,  finding  expression  in  some  adventures  in  amateur  journalism,  together 
with  a  term  of  service  in  a  local  printing  office,  shaped  his  course  toward  news- 
paper work.  Leaving  home,  he  entered  that  field  in  Milwaukee,  finally  rising 
to  an  editorial  position  on  the  Milwaukee  Sentinel.  In  this  capacity  he  reported 
the  National  Republican  Convention  in  Chicago  in  1888.  The  fondness  for 
"printer's  ink,"  which  is  facetiously  said  to  be  ineradicable,  did  indeed  never 
leave  him,  and  it  is  only  by  the  narrowest  margin  that  his  talents  did  not  find 
expression  in  literary  fields.  He  became  greatly  interested  in  the  romantic  and 
thrilling  history  of  the  Northwest,  particularly  of  Wisconsin,  and  formed  one 
of  that  enthusiastic  band  of  local  history  students  who,  under  the  tutelage  of 
Reuben  Gold  Thwaites,  have  made  notable  and  voluminous  contributions  to 
the  historical  and  legendary  lore  of  this  region.  The  famous  Parkman  Club, 
now  defunct,  was  organized  in  Milwaukee  by  this  group,  and,  under  the  secre- 
taryship of  Henry  E.  Legler,  issued  a  series  of  studies  which  are  historically 
of  considerable  importance  and  bibliographically  of  great  rarity.  Many  of  them 
are  the  work  of  the  secretary  of  the  club,  who,  during  this  period,  also  pub- 
lished a  life  of  Tonty,  an  account  of  the  Mormon  attempts  to  colonize  on  Lake 
Michigan  and  various  pamphlets  on  kindred  topics,  involving  much  research 
and  bringing  into  play  that  tireless  industry  and  persistence  which  remained 
one  of  his  truest  characteristics.  In  1897  he  issued  a  study  of  the  life  of  James 
Gates  Percival,  the  poet  and  geologist,  which  he  set  up  and  printed  on  a  small 
press  in  his  home  in  Milwaukee.  Among  the  by-products  of  his  historical  avo- 
cation are  a  large  collection  of  books  bearing  on  Wisconsin  history,  which  is 
now  the  property  of  the  public  library  at  Superior,  Wis.,  and  a  splendid  and  va- 
ried assortment  of  lantern  slides  bearing  upon  the  same  subject,  particularly  the 
Indian  legends,  life  in  pioneer  days  and  kindred  topics.  It  was  his  pleasure  to 
give  illustrated  lectures,  particularly  to  audiences  of  young  people.  A  second 
collection  of  slides  dealing  with  rare  books,  illustrators,  early  printing  and  other 
bookish  matters  attest  his  interests  in  this  direction.  For  many  years,  until  the 


BOOK  BULLETIN  95 


accumulation  of  duties  forbade  it,  he  made  an  annual  trip  to  the  east,  visiting 
the  large  libraries  and  giving  one  or  more  lectures  before  the  library  schools, 
where  he  was  always  received  with  enthusiasm  and  affection,  and  where  his 
decision  to  discontinue  these  visits  caused  sincere  regret. 

In  1889  the  ambitious  young  newspaper  man  was  elected  to  the  state  leg- 
islature. He  served  but  one  term  and  in  the  following  year  became  secretary  of 
the  Milwaukee  school  board,  which  position  he  held  for  fourteen  years.  Here 
his  talents  for  administration  first  came  into  play  and  marked  him  as  the  pos- 
sessor of  high  abilities,  a  manager  of  men  and  of  affairs. 

Meanwhile  his  attention  had  been  turned  to  librarianship  as  a  rising  pro- 
fession and  a  relatively  undeveloped  field  of  usefulness.  A  lover  of  books  since 
boyhood,  a  journalist  and  writer  of  books,  and  head  of  the  business  manage- 
ment of  Milwaukee's  schools,  he  naturally  formed  an  early  acquaintance  with 
the  Milwaukee  public  library.  That  institution  was  then  under  the  direction  of 
a  woman  rich  in  the  gifts  of  personality  and  human  sympathy,  a  true  librarian 
in  the  new  sense  and  at  that  time  a  very  pioneer  in  the  New  Learning  which 
today  is  the  mark  of  efficient  library  administration — Theresa  West,  now  known 
and  loved  throughout  the  library  world  as  Mrs.  H.  L.  Elmendorf  of  Buffalo. 
Mr.  Legler  frequently  spoke  of  the  influence  of  Mrs.  Elmendorf  in  determin- 
ing his  career,  while  she  has  laughingly  declared  that,  years  before  he  was  identi- 
fied with  the  work,  he  was  "a  better  librarian  than  many  in  the  business."  It 
was  almost  inevitable,  therefore,  that  when  the  state  of  Wisconsin  came  to  seek 
a  secretary  for  its  state  library  commission  the  choice  should  fall  upon  Henry 
E.  Legler. 

The  commission  had  been  formed  a  few  years  before  through  the  joint  ef- 
forts of  State  Senator  James  H.  Stout  and  Frank  A.  Hutchins,  the  latter  be- 
coming its  first  executive  officer  with  the  title  of  Secretary.  Under  his  guidance 
the  Wisconsin  Free  Library  Commission  soon  showed  the  whole  country  what 
might  be  accomplished  by  wisely  administered  state  aid  in  promoting  the  estab- 
lishment and  aiding  the  growth  of  public  libraries  in  cities,  towns  and  rural 
communities,  through  the  services  of  experts  in  library  architecture  and  man- 
agement, the  co-operation  of  a  central  authority  in  awakening  public  interest, 
help  in  the  selection  of  books,  librarians  and  staff  and,  ultimately,  in  realizing 
the  full  and  proper  function  of  these  institutions  as  an  integral  part  of  public 
education.  Hutchins,  the  enthusiast  and  inspirer  of  enthusiasm,  was  wisely 
chosen  as  the  first  executive  of  this  enterprise.  When  he  died — as  his  succes- 
sor was  destined  to  die,  in  harness — he  was  followed  by  Legler,  the  resource- 
ful organizer,  the  fearless  innovator,  the  diplomat  and  politician  in  the  best 
sense  of  that  term.  For  the  next  five  years  the  work  and  influence  of  the  Wis- 
consin Free  Library  Commission  grew  by  leaps  and  bounds.  A  school  was  es- 
tablished at  Madison  for  the  training  of  librarians.  Close  relations  were  estab- 
lished with  the  great  University  Extension  Division  of  the  state  university  by 


96 CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 

which  books  were  sent  out  broadcast  in  the  wake  of  lecturers  and  for  the  use 
of  study  clubs.  One  of  the  first  and  most  efficient  of  the  now  generally  adopted 
Legislative  Reference  Bureaus  was  instituted.  Traveling  libraries  penetrated 
the  forests  to  the  remotest  lumber  camps,  and  the  public  libraries  of  the  state 
were  firmly  knit  into  that  fabric  of  state  activities  for  progress  and  enlighten- 
ment collectively  known  the  world  over  as  the  Wisconsin  Idea. 

In  1909  occurred  the  vacancy  in  the  headship  of  the  Chicago  Public  Li- 
brary. The  Civil  Service  Commission  announced  an  examination  to  fill  the  posi- 
tion and  nominated  three  of  the  leading  librarians  of  the  country  to  conduct  the 
test.  Much  doubt  was  expressed  in  professional  circles  as  to  the  practicability 
of  this  mode  of  choosing  the  incumbent  of  what  was  regarded  as  a  difficult  po- 
sition, and  the  outcome  was  awaited  with  the  keenest  interest  both  in  Chicago 
and  in  library  and  civil  service  circles  generally.  When  it  was  finally  announced 
that  Henry  E.  Legler  was  the  choice  of  the  examining  committee  there  were 
few  who  were  not  ready  to  declare  that  under  the  circumstances  the  wisest 
possible  selection  had  been  made.  The  problems  presented  by  the  Chicago 
situation  as  it  then  existed  involved  practically  a  complete  reversal  of  the  policy 
prevailing  in  the  conduct  of  that  institution,  characterized  by  a  conservatism 
that  had  degenerated  almost  into  stagnation.  The  application  of  new  meth- 
ods, the  opening  of  new  activities,  the  extension  of  library  service  to  cover  the 
whole  vast  area  of  the  city ;  these  were  recognized  as  among  the  essential  ele- 
ments of  the  proper  conduct  of  the  Library.  Meanwhile  there  was  no  immedi- 
ate prospect  of  an  increase  in  the  revenue — which  the  previous  administration 
had  declared  wholly  inadequate  for  such  developments.  The  task  awaiting 
the  new  Librarian,  who  entered  upon  his  duties  on  October  10th,  1909,  was  there- 
fore somewhat  akin  to  that  of  making  bricks  without  straw. 

There  is  not  space  here,  nor  is  this  occasion,  for  a  detailed  record  of  these 
strenuous  eight  years.  The  story  has  been  told  in  the  official  records  and  else- 
where, and  will  perhaps  be  told  again  at  the  proper  time  and  with  more  leisure 
to  compile  it.  Chicagoans  will  scarcely  need  to  be  reminded  of  the  enormous 
gains  in  usefulness  which  the  Library  has  shown  in  eight  years :  in  the  home  use 
of  books,  which  grew  from  1,800,000  to  6,000,000;  in  the  number  of  branches, 
increased  from  one  to  forty;  in  the  varied  and  numerous  activities  added  to 
the  machinery  for  the  service  of  the  people  and  in  the  large  projects  formulated 
for  the  still  further  extension  of  that  service  in  the  future. 

To  this  great  commission  the  Chief  Librarian  devoted  all  of  his  strength 
and  thought,  his  gifts  and  resources,  his  soul  and  body.  No  other  interests, 
avocation  or  diversion  was  allowed  to  absorb  a  moment  of  time  or  an  ounce  of 
energy.  And  in  the  end  he  gave  his  life ;  for  if  any  man  ever  "died  in  harness," 
so  did  Henry  E.  Legler  die.  And  so,  as  has  been  said,  one  must  believe  he 
would  have  wished  to  die.  For  nearly  two  years  ominous  and  multiplying  signs 
of  illness  were  apparent  to  all  save  himself.  Sheer  physical  inability  to  con- 
tinue alone  determined  his  absence  from  his  post  even  for  a  day.  Scorning  the 


BOOK  BULLETIN  97 


counsels  of  common  prudence,  the  importunities  of  anxious  friends  and  the  of- 
ficial commands  of  his  Board  to  take  thought  of  his  bodily  health,  he  persisted 
until  a  malignant  disease  had  time  to  fasten  its  tentacles  upon  him,  and  the  end 
came  as  a  merciful  release  from  cruel  pain.  So  valiant  a  devotion  to  duty,  thus 
brought  to  a  close  but  never  conquered,  cannot  but  inspire  those  who  witnessed 
the  good  fight  with  renewed  faith  in  the  power  of  ideals  and  with  gratitude  for 
this  brief  communion  with  a  noble  and  selfless  soul. 

Henry  E.  Legler's  contribution  to  the  city  which  called  him  was  the  vast  ex- 
tension of  library  service  to  all  the  people.  A  hater  of  red  tape  and  a  foe  of 
convention,  he  saw  to  it  that  no  potential  reader  was  deprived  of  a  book  by  rea- 
son of  any  formalities  that  could  be  dispensed  with.  A  booklover  himself,  he 
was  no  lover  of  an  idle  book.  He  could  not  wait  for  a  building  fund  with 
which  to  erect  monumental  branch  buildings  because  he  could  not  forget  the 
thousands  who  would  have  to  wait  with  him  without  access  to  books.  He 
taught  his  public  that  the  Library  was  not  a  repository  but  a  distributing  cen- 
ter. Without  either  the  gift  or  the  desire  for  self-advertisement,  he  contrived 
to  make  the  Library  almost  a  household  word  in  this  community  of  nearly  three 
millions.  His  successor  will  have  no  easy  task;  for  he  taught  Chicago  to  ex- 
pect much  of  its  Public  Library  and  gave  his  life  to  the  task  of  keeping  ever — 
not  abreast  with,  but  ahead  of — those  expectations.  One  of  his  few  literary  pas- 
sions was  for  Walt  Whitman,  to  whom  he  devoted  his  last  personal  literary 
work.  A  line  he  loved  to  quote  from  the  good  gray  poet  sums  up,  perhaps  as 
well  as  anything  else  could,  his  personal  creed  of  duty  for  duty's  sake  and  the 
joy  of  the  laborer  in  his  labors. 

"The  song  is  for  the  singer,  and  comes  back  most  to  him." 


This  is  written  by  his  first  assistant,  in  part  as  an  attempt  to  set  out  for 
himself  an  orderly  record  of  personal  impressions  and  so  to  fix  the  outline  of  a 
mental  picture  which  must  henceforth  serve  as  the  only  remembrance  of  that 
vivid  personality.  At  the  same  time  it  may  serve  as  the  last  effort  on  the  part 
of  the  staff — by  one  who.  perhaps  without  due  warrant,  assumes  to  speak  for 
the  staff — to  visualize  its  final  image  of  the  Chief.  To  the  readers  of  the  Book 
Bulletin  it  is  offered  as  a  contribution  toward  a  clearer  understanding  of  the 
powers,  merits  and  achievements  of  the  man  who  was  known  to  so  many  thou- 
sands of  patrons  of  the  Chicago  Public  Library  through  his  unfailing  courtesy, 
his  tireless  activity  in  their  behalf,  and  through  the  results  of  those  labors  of 
which  they  and  theirs  are  and  ever  shall  be  the  beneficiaries. 

C.  B.  RODEN. 


98 CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 

RESOLUTIONS  ADOPTED  BY  THE  BOARD 
SEPT.  13TH,  1917 

It  is  with  the  keenest  and  most  genuine  sense  of  profound  bereavement  that 
the  Board  of  Directors  of  the  Chicago  Public  Library  places  on  record  this 
small  tribute  to  the  memory  of  its  departed  librarian,  Henry  E.  Legler,  and  each 
and  every  member  of  the  Board  of  Directors  feels  this  bereavement  no  less  as 
an  official  than  a  personal  one. 

The  Directors  recognized  in  Henry  Legler  one  of  those  men  of  vision  and 
ideals  whose  devotion  and  enthusiasm  have  helped  to  build  up  the  noble  City 
of  Chicago.  Placed  in  a  station  where  his  work  was  necessarily  quiet  and  un- 
illumined  by  the  glare  of  publicity,  he  was  uplifted  and  carried  along  by  a 
lofty  conception  of  the  inherent  greatness  of  his  work  and  the  good  it  would  do 
in  the  minds  of  the  men,  women  and  children  of  Chicago,  to  be  later  translated 
by  them  into  action  in  their  practical  lives.  His  work,  like  that  of  the  teacher, 
was  of  the  silent  unassuming,  but  nevertheless  fundamental  and  constructive 
kind,  aiming  to  expand  the  minds  of  his  fellows  and  strengthen  them  for  the 
tasks  they  might  be  called  upon  to  fulfill  in  their  own  behalf,  that  of  their  city 
and  country.  To  the  strength,  the  intelligence,  the  character,  the  happiness  of 
the  citizens  of  Chicago  his  contribution  was  great  though  unostentatious. 

It  was  his  overruling  devotion  to  his  work  that  hastened  his  death.  While 
suffering  for  years  with  an  ailment  that  called  on  him  to  be  sparing  of  his 
strength,  he  refused  to  leave  his  labors  for  temporary  rest,  and  so  fell  literally 
in  the  harness.  His  life  was  given  for  the  good  of  his  fellows  as  truly  as  that 
of  the  soldier  who  falls  on  the  field  of  honor. 

Henry  E.  Legler  enjoyed  the  esteem  of  librarians  as  one  of  the  ablest 
among  them.  Not  only  was  he  an  administrator  of  pre-eminent  ability,  con- 
ducting the  affairs  of  this  library  with  the  highest  degree  of  efficiency,  but  also 
a  seer  whose  visions  of  the  expansion  of  its  usefulness  and  for  bringing  its 
benefits  near  to  the  homes  of  every  resident  inspired  all  of  us.  It  is  to  his 
sole  initiative  that  the  great  plan  of  regional  libraries  for  the  better  circulation 
of  books  among  the  people  owes  its  origin  and  organization  —  a  sublime  and, 
let  us  hope,  permanent  memorial  of  his  fidelity  and  genius. 

Among  the  staff  of  the  Chicago  Public  Library,  Henry  E.  Legler  was  an 
object  of  affectionate  adoration.  The  unvarying  sweetness  of  his  temper  and 
unfailing  justice  of  his  judgment  won  for  him  their  undivided  and  cordial  love. 

The  Directors  likewise  found  in  Henry  E.  Legler  more  than  the  official 
and  administrator  of  a  public  utility.  His  ability  and  character  bound  them 
to  him,  each  and  every  one,  in  the  bonds  of  the  firmest  and  warmest  friendship. 

Thus  they  lament  in  his  death  the  loss  of  a  friend,  while  feeling  that  the 
City  of  Chicago  has  cause  to  mourn  the  loss  of  a  public  servant  of  exceptional 
ability,  unswerving  loyalty,  deep  devotion  and  far-reaching  efficiency  whose 
silent  influence  will  live  in  the  minds  of  thousands  for  many  years  to  come. 

It  is  ordered  that  this  minute  be  spread  upon  the  records  of  this  Board, 
and  that  a  copy  in  proper  form  be  communicated  to  Mrs.  Legler  and  her  sons 
as  a  token  of  the  sympathy  and  the  sincere  and  affectionate  solicitude  which 
this  Board  feels  lor  her  and  hers  in  this  hour  of  trial 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


99 


BY  THE  STAFF 

The  Staff  of  the  Chicago  Public  Library  has  met  today  to  take  action  on 
the  passing  of  its  Chief. 

The  relationships  which  arise  between  those  engaged  in  a  common  task, 
bound  together  by  the  same  interest,  are  often  of  a  nature  purely  official,  pene- 
trating no  farther  into  their  lives  than  the  prosecution  of  their  duties  may 
demand.  Temporary  in  their  character,  such  relationships  in  all  propriety 
exert  no  lasting  influence  and  leave  no  memories.  They  close  when  the  task 
is  finished  and  we  turn  to  new  responsibilities,  or  activities  in  fresh  fields. 

So  vividly  contrasted  from  the  condition  here  described  has  been  the  rela- 
tionship between  this  Staff  and  the  Chief  Librarian — now  abruptly  severed  in 
mid-stream — that  we,  the  members  of  the  Staff,  individually  and  corporately, 
find  ourselves  oppressed  by  but  one  thought — solely  and  primarily  inspired  by 
the  overwhelming  sense  of  the  personal  loss  we  have  suffered. 

The  people  of  Chicago  are  deprived  of  the  ministrations  of  a  public  servant 
of  singular  ability,  zeal  and  purity.  The  Chicago  Public  Library  has  lost 
the  leader  who  for  eight  years  has  guided  its  progress  and  fostered  its  develop- 
ment with  a  measure  of  brilliant  success  amid  stupendous  difficulties,  unequalled 
in  the  annals  of  librarianship.  His  profession  mourns  the  passing  of  one  of  its 
greatest  exponents,  whose  career,  stimulating  even  in  its  victorious  conclusion, 
serves  as  a  perpetual  light  to  lighten  the  path  of  professional  achievement. 

But  we  have  lost  a  Friend,  a  Counsellor  and  Fellow-servant;  wise  in  deci- 
sion, firm  in  execution,  patient  with  our  importunities,  zealous  in  the  promotion 
of  the  interests  committed  to  his  charge,  but  first  of  all  the  Friend  of  all  of  us. 
At  this  time  we  can  do  no  more  than  to  offer  this  record  of  our  personal  be- 
reavement; to  bid  farewell  to  this  Friend  whose  cheery  smile  and  hearty  grasp 
shall  greet  us  no  more;  to  big  Godspeed  to  Henry  Eduard  Legler,  faithful 
servant,  brilliant  administrator  and  true  Friend. 

Be  It  Resolved,  That  a  copy  of  this  minute  be  submitted  to  Mrs.  Legler 
and  her  family  as  a  feeble  expression  of  our  deep  and  affectionate  sympathy. 
And  be  it  further 

Resolved,  That  a  second  copy  be  offered  to  the  Board  of  Directors  of  the 
Chicago  Public  Library  with  the  request  that  it  be  accepted  and  printed  in  the 
Minutes  of  the  Board  at  its  next  regular  meeting.

attention  to  the  highly  important  and  useful 
documents  issued  by  the  national  government 
in  great  profusion  and  variety.  Complete  files

partment, where  they  may  be  consulted  and 
where  duplicates  of  many  of  them  may  be

may  also  be  purchased  from  the  Sup't  of  Docu- 
ments, Washington,  D.  C.  Payment  must  be 
made  at  time  of  ordering,  preferably  in  stamps 
or  postal  order.

Scrap  Books  for  Soldiers 

For  wounded  or  convalescent  soldiers  and  sailors — too  ill  to  care  for  a  book 
and  too  weak  to  manage  one — a  scrap  book  of  bright  pictures  and  brief,  cheerful 
stories,  poems  or  jokes  has  proven  highly  useful  and  entertaining.  In  England, 
where  all  war  work  is  conducted  along  national  lines,  such  scrap  books  have  been 
made  by  hundreds  of  thousands.  Rudyard  Kipling  is  credited  with  originating  the 
plan  and  the  "Kipling  scrap  books"  have  been  classed  among  the  most  important 
contributions  made  by  the  British  people  at  home  to  their  boys  at  the  front. 

Adopting  the  British  idea,  the  Chicago  Public  Library  has  invited  the  public 
to  help  in  making  scrap  books  for  American  soldiers.  The  response  was  immedi- 
ate and  overwhelming.  Over  seven  hundred  of  the  books  are  now  out  in  the  hands 
of  eager  volunteers,  who  have  undertaken  to  fill  them  in  accordance  with  instruc- 
tions issued  with  each  book.  Some  beautifully  finished  books  have  already  been 
turned  in,  revealing  much  skill  and  thought  and  bearing  individual  touches  of  high 
artistic  merit.  At  present  the  stock  of  paper  is  entirely  exhausted  and  many  appli- 
cants have  been  disappointed  pending  the  receipt  of  a  new  supply. 

The  Chicago  Daily  News  has  offered  to  pay  for  10,000  scrap  books  and  the 
order  for  the  paper  has  been  given.  As  soon  as  the  manufacturers  are  able  to  fill 
this  order,  and  the  books  can  be  made  up,  the  Library  will  again  issue  scrap  books 
to  all  applicants.  It  is  expected  that  this  will  be  not  later  than  October  first.  Due 
announcement  will  be  made  in  the  Daily  News. 

For  the  benefit  of  others  who  may  desire  to  make  up  scrap  books  on  their  own 
account  the  following  specifications  are  given: 

A  soft,  heavy  brown  paper,  known  as  "Stronghold  Kraft,"  is  used  for  the  body, 
in  sheets  of  48x60  inches.  One  sheet,  folded  and  trimmed,  makes  two  16-page 
books  14  inches  long  by  11  inches  high.  The  cover  is  of  brown  Kenesaw  Rope  Bris- 
tol, of  the  same  shade,  which  comes  in  sheets  22^x28H.  Cover  and  body  are 
stitched  together  with  two  wire  staples,  or  may  be  sewed  with  stout  thread  or  bright 
colored  twine.  Books  of  these  materials  and  dimensions  can  be  made  for  a  little  less 
than  five  cents  apiece  when  the  stock  is  bought  in  bulk.  The  Library  scrap  books 
bear  the  following  title  on  the  front  cover : 

A  SCRAP  BOOK 

FOR  OUR  SOLDIERS  AND  SAILORS 

MADE  BY  CITIZENS  OF  CHICAGO 

AND  THE  CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


102 


CHICAGO   PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Books  Added  to  the  Library 


Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those 

marked  Doc.  are  in  the  Document  department.  *Ser.  represents 

serials  which  do  not  circulate.

CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Rules  and  Information  for  Patrons 


Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person 
residing  in  Cook  County  having  permanent 
employment  in  the  city,  may  enjoy  the  priv- 
ileges of  the  Library  by  having  a  resident 
of  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear  in  the 
city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon 
application  and  compliance  with  the  rules 
and  regulations  of  the  Board  of  Directors 
a  card  good  for  three  years  will  be  issued, 
which  will  permit  the  applicant  to  draw 
five  books  for  home  use. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges 
upon  application. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and 
may  be  once  renewed  for  the  same  period. 
Books  may  be  renewed  but  once.  Books 
of  recent  purchase  labeled  Seven-day  books 
cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed.  The  card 
must  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 
out  or  renewed. 

Books  issued  at  Circulating  Centers  or 
Delivery  Stations  may  be  returned  at  the 
Main  Library,  but  books  cannot  be  applied 
for  at  the  Main  Library  and  delivered  to 
Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery  Stations. 

Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  hab- 
its may  use  the  various  Reading  and  Refer- 
ence Rooms  of  the  Library.  No  card  is 


required  to  use  the  books  on  the  open 
shelves  or  the  books  in  the  stacks,  which 
must  be  applied  for. 

A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported 
to  the  Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books 
drawn  on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  pre- 
sented, and  for  all  fines  incurred.  If  a  card 
is  lost,  immediate  notice  must  be  given  to 
the  Library.  A  new  card  will  be  issued 
thirty  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  one  cent  a  day  on  each  work  of 
non-fiction,  and  three  cents  a  day  on  each 
work  of  fiction,  is  imposed  whether  bound 
in  one  or  more  volumes,  which  is  not  re- 
turned within  two  weeks  from  the  date  of 
issue.  An  additional  charge  of  two  cents 
is  made  for  ea,ch  notice  sent  for  the  return 
of  a  book,  and  no  other  books  will  be  de- 
livered to  the  person  incurring  the  fine  until 
it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the 
call  number  is  crossed  off  the  order  slip 
whenever  a  book  is  drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohib- 
ited, and  all  injuries  to  books  beyond  reas- 
onable wear  and  all  losses  must  be  promptly 
adjusted  to  the  satisfaction  of  the  Librarian. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours 
Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  De- 
partments, third  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:30 
p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Cata- 
logue, fourth  floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m., 
Sundays  and  holidays,  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People, 
fourth  floor,  open  9:30  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to 
5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room, 
first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street 
entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  closed 
on  Sunday. 


Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and 
newspapers,  fourth  floor,  Randolph  street 
entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays 
and  holidays,  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Music  Room,  fifth  floor,  Washington 
street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulle- 
tin are  issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and 
August,  and  are  distributed  free  of  charge 
in  all  departments  of  the  Main  Library  and 
in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be  mailed  to 
any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year  to 
cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published 
in  January  of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at 
ten  cents  per  copy. 


October  1917 


Book  Bulletin 


TWENTY  YEARS  OLD 

DEDICATED  OCTOBER  9TH,  OPENED  FOR  SERVICE  OCTOBER  HTH,  1897 


YEARS  AGO,  on  Saturday,  October  9th,  1897, 
this  building  was  formally  dedicated.  Appropriate  exercises 
were  held  in  the  evening  in  the  presence  of  about  three  thousand 
invited  guests,  who  afterwards  inspected  the  various  rooms  under 
guidance  of  members  of  the  staff.  The  building  was  formally 
turned  over  to  the  City  by  the  President  of  the  Board  of  Directors, 
the  Hon.  Azel  F.  Hatch,  one  of  the  eminent  lawyers  of  the  day, 
whose  scholarly  address  detailed  the  history  of  the  Public  Library 
from  its  inception  after  the  Fire  of  1871  until  its  final  housing  in 
this  splendid  new  home.  The  orator  of  the  occasion  was  the  Rev. 
Dr.  Emil  G.  Hirsch,  who  had  been  President  of  the  Board  for  a 
number  of  years  preceding,  and  who  delivered  an  oration  which 
ranks  among  the  ablest  efforts  of  that  master  of  formal  eloquence. 
The  Board- took  occasion  to  express  a  justifiable  pride  in  the  suc- 
cessful issue  of  its  stewardship  as  shown  in  the  completion  of  the 
building  well  within  the  promised  time  and  for  a  sum — $1,940,000 
— materially  under  the  official  appropriation.  "Our  experience," 
said  President  Hatch,  "has  demonstrated  that  a  public  building 
can  be  erected  without  extras  and  within  the  appropriation,  and 
as  economically  as  a  private  structure."  Present  day  patrons  are 
invited  to  observe  the  wonderfully  sound  condition  of  the  structure 
after  two  decades  of  constant  use — the  flawless  mosaics,  the  glis- 
tening marbles  and  the  superb  woodwork,  bearing  ample  testi- 
mony to  the  devotion  and  integrity  of  these  men  and  their  builders. 
On  Monday,  October  llth,  the  doors  were  opened  at  nine 
o'clock  and  the  work  of  the  Library  was  begun  in  its  new  quarters. 
After  many  vicissitudes  and  wanderings  the  Chicago  Public  Li- 
brary had  "moved"  for  the  last  time  and  was  permanently  at  home 
in  its  majestic  and  beautiful  new  building. 


Chicago  Public  Library 

Book  Bulletin 

Volume  7  October,  1917  No.  8 

The  Library  Exhibit  of  Native  Wild  Flowers 

All  through  the  spring,  summer  and  autumn  the  south  corridor  of  the  fourth 
floor  of  the  Library  building  has  been  a  fragrant  garden  of  wild  flowers,  plants  and 
shrubs,  collected  by  the  Forestry  Class  of  the  Chicago  Woman's  Club  and  displayed 
in  their  seasonal  order,  making  a  gorgeous  and  fascinating  procession  of  our  native 
plant  life.  Great  public  interest  and  appreciation  have  been  manifested  throughout 
the  exhibit,  and  much  astonishment  was  expressed  at  the  number  and  variety  of  wild 
flowers  found  in  the  fields  and  woods  about  Chicago.  From  every  standpoint,  educa- 
tional, artistic,  botanical  as  well  as  that  of  pure  popular  interest,  this  exhibit  has 
been  worth  while.  We  are  glad  to  publish  the  following  modest  report  of  the  Chair- 
man of  the  Class,  Mrs  Caroline  K.  Sherman,  to  whose  unremitting  labors  much  of 
the  success  of  the  plan  is  due.  We  call  especial  attention  to  the  remarkable  botani- 
cal index,  compiled  by  Mrs  Sherman,  recording  no  less  than  439  separate  specimens 
of  Chicago  wild  flowers  included  in  this  exhibit: 

The  Forestry  Class  of  the  Chicago  Woman's  Club  has  exhibited  in  the  Library  dur- 
ing the  season  just  passed  some  four  hundred  and  fifty  species  of  wild  flowers  native  to 
Chicago  and  vicinity.  The  Committee  in  charge  consisted  of  Mrs.  Caroline  K.  Sher- 
man, Chairman;  Mrs.  A.  C.  Harding  and  Mrs.  A.  G.  Becker. 

The  object  of  the  exhibit  was  to  acquaint  people  with  our  rich  and  varied  flora 
and  to  arouse  public  sentiment  to  save  our  wild  flowers  which  are  fast  disappearing  in 
the  so-called  march  of  civilization. 

The  interest  in  the  exhibit  exceeded  all  expectation.  The  common  name  of  the 
plants,  as  well  as  the  scientific,  was  given  with  the  result  that  it  appealed  to  various 
classes  in  various  ways,  to  some  from  sentiment  and  a  love  of  'beauty,  to  others  from 
a  desire  to  know  the  names  of  plants  familiar  from  childhood  but  as  yet  the  name 
unknown.  Some  came  with  notebook,  making  close  and  careful  study  in  a  scientific 
way,  while  not  a  few  took  pains  to  make  a  copy  of  the  rarer  or  more  peculiar  plants,  so 
that  on  the  whole  the  Committee  has  been  assured  that  the  work  of  the  Forestry  Class 
has  been  well  worth  while  as  to  the  pleasure  and  instruction  given  to  the  public. 

In  regard  to  the  main  purpose  for  which  the  exhibit  was  intended — the  conservation 
of  our  wild  flowers — the  conclusion  reached  by  the  Committee  was  that  although  there 
is  a  wide-spread  public  sentiment  against  the  ruthless  destruction  of  our  native  plants, 
sentiment  alone  is  not  sufficient  to  save  them.  To  be  effective  this  sentiment  must 
be  expressed  by  law  and  furthered  by  an  increased  number  of  forest  reservations  pro- 
tected from  vandalism  by  efficient  police.  Only  in  this  way  can  our  rare  plants  be 
saved,  our  orchids,  our  lotus,  and  above  all  our  treasures  in  the  Dunes. 

The  demands  on  the  public  purse  because  of  the  war  should  not  lead  to  a  neglect 
of  the  immediate  demand  of  saving  for  generations  to  come  the  rich  and  varied  flora  in 
and  about  Chicago. 


122  CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY

Books  Added  to  the  Library 


Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those 

marked  Doc.  are  in  the  Document  department.  *Ser.  represents 

serials  which  do  not  circulate.

CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Rules  and  Information  for  Patrons 


Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person 
residing  in  Cook  County  having  permanent 
employment  in  the  city,  may  enjoy  the  priv- 
ileges of  the  Library  by  having  a  resident 
of  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear  in  the 
city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon 
application  and  compliance  with  the  rules 
and  regulations  of  the  Board  of  Directors 
a  card  good  for  three  years  will  be  issued, 
which  will  permit  the  applicant  to  draw 
five  books  for  home  use. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges 
upon  application. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and 
may  be  once  renewed  for  the  same  period. 
Books  may  be  renewed  but  once.  Books 
of  recent  purchase  labeled  Seven-day  books 
cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed.  The  card 
must  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 
out  or  renewed. 

Books  issued  at  Circulating  Centers  or 
Delivery  Stations  may  be  returned  at  the 
Main  Library,  but  books  cannot  be  applied 
for  at  the  Main  Library  and  delivered  to 
Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery  Stations. 

Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  hab- 
its may  use  the  various  Reading  and  Refer- 
ence Rooms  of  the  Library.  No  card  is 


required  to  use  the  books  on  the  open 
shelves  or  the  books  in  the  stacks,  which 
must  be  applied  for. 

A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported 
to  the  Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books 
drawn  on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  pre- 
sented, and  for  all  fines  incurred.  If  a  card 
is  lost,  immediate  notice  must  be  given  to 
the  Library.  A  new  card  will  be  issued 
thirty  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  one  cent  a  day  on  each  work  of 
non-fiction,  and  three  cents  a  day  on  each 
work  of  fiction,  is  imposed  whether  bound 
in  one  or  more  volumes,  which  is  not  re- 
turned within  two  weeks  from  the  date  of 
issue.  An  additional  charge  of  two  cents 
is  made  for  each  notice  sent  for  the  return 
of  a  book,  and  no  other  books  will  be  de- 
livered to  the  person  incurring  the  fine  until 
it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the 
call  number  is  crossed  off  the  order  slip 
whenever  a  book  is  drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohib- 
ited, and  all  injuries  to  books  beyond  reas- 
onable wear  and  all  losses  must  be  promptly 
adjusted  to  the  satisfaction  of  the  Librarian. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours 
Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  De- 
partments, third  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:30 
p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Cata- 
logue, fourth  floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m., 
Sundays  and  holidays,  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People, 
fourth  floor,  open  9:30  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to 
5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room, 
first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street 
entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  closed 
on  Sunday. 


Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and 
newspapers,  fourth  floor,  Randolph  street 
entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays 
and  holidays,  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Music  Room,  fifth  floor,  Washington 
street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulle- 
tin are  issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and 
August,  and  are  distributed  free  of  charge 
in  all  departments  of  the  Main  Library  and 
in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be  mailed  to 
any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year  to 
cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published 
in  January  of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at 
ten  cents  per  copy. 


November  1917 


Chicago  Public  Library 


Book  Bulletin 


THE   ROTUNDA 


proclamation 


BY  THE  PRESIDENT  OF  THE  UNITED  STATES  OF  AMERICA. 

A  Proclamation. 

/T  HAS  long  been  the  honored  custom  of  our  people  to  turn  in  the  fruitful 
autumn  of  the  year  in  praise  and  thanksgiving  to  Almighty  God  for  His 
many  blessings  and  mercies  to  us  as  a  nation.  That  custom  we  can  follow 
now  even  in  the  midst  of  the  tragedy  of  a  world  shaken  by  war  and  im- 
measurable disaster,  in  the  midst  of  sorrow  and  great  peril,  because  even  amidst 
the  darkness  that  has  gathered  about  us  we  can  see  the  great  blessings  God  has 
bestowed  upon  us,  blessings  that  are  better  than  mere  peace  of  mind  and  pros- 
perity of  enterprise. 

We  have  been  given  the  opportunity  to  serve  mankind  as  we  once  served  our- 
selves in  the  great  day  of  our  Declaration  of  Independence,  by  taking  up  arms 
against  a  tyranny  that  threatened  to  master  and  debase  men  everywhere  and 
joining  with  other  free  peoples  in  demanding  for  all  the  nations  of  the  world 
what  we  then  demanded  and  obtained  for  ourselves.  In  this  day  of  the  revela- 
tion of  our  duty  not  only  to  defend  our  own  rights  as  a  nation  but  to  defend  also 
the  rights  of  free  men  throughout  the  world,  there  has  been  vouchsafed  us  in-s 
full  and  inspiring  measure  the  resolution  and  spirit  of  united  action.  We  have 
veen  brought  to  one  mind  and  purpose.  A  new  vigor  of  common  counsel  and 
common  action  has  been  revealed  in  us.  We  should  especially  thank  God  that 
in  such  circumstances,  in  the  midst  of  the  greatest  enterprise  the  spirits  of  men 
have  ever  entered  upon,  we  have,  if  we  but  observe  a  reasonable  and  practicable 
economy,  abundance  with  which  to  supply  the  needs  of  those  associated  with  us 
as  well  as  our  own.  A  new  light  shines  about  us.  The  great  duties  of  a  new  day 
awaken  a  new  and  greater  national  spirit  in  us.  We  shall  never  again  be  divided 
or  wonder  what  stuff  we  are  made  of. 

And  while  we  render  thanks  for  these  things  let  us  pray  Almighty  God  that 
in  all  humbleness  of  spirit  zve  may  look  always  to  Him  for  guidance;  that  we 
may  be  kept  constant  in  the  spirit  and  purpose  of  service;  that  by  His  grace  our 
minds  may  be  directed  and  our  hands  strengthened;  and  that  in  His  good  time 
liberty  and  security  and  peace  and  the  comradeship  of  a  common  justice  may  be 
vouchsafed  all  the  nations  of  the  earth. 

WHEREFORE,  I,  Woodrow  Wilson,  President  of  the  United  States  of 
America,  do  hereby  designate  Thursday,  the  twenty-ninth  day  of  November* 
next,  as  a  day  of  thanksgiving  and  prayer,  and  invite  the  people  throughout  the 
land  to  cease  upon  that  day  from  their  ordinary  occupations  and  in  their  several 
homes  and  places  of  worship  to  render  thanks  to  God,  the  great  rider  of  nations. 

IN  WITNESS  WHEREOF  I  have  hereunto  set  my  hand  and  caused  the 
seal  of  the  United  States  to  be  affixed. 

Done  in  the  District  of  Columbia  this  seventh  day  of  November  in  the  year 
of  our  Lord  one  thousand  nine  hundred  and  seventeen  and  of  the  Independence 
of  the  United  States  of  America  the  one  hundred  and  forty-second. 

WOODROW  WILSON. 
By  the  President: 

ROBERT  LANSING, 

Secretary  of  State. 


Volume  7 


November,  1917 


No.  9 


Opera  Scores,  Librettos  and  Plots 

From  six  to  ten  copies  of  the  vocal  scores  of  all  operas  in  the  Chicago 
repertory,  so  far  as  obtainable,  will  be  found  in  the  Masic  Room,  where  they 
may  be  borrowed  on  a  music  card  issued  to  all  registered  book-borrowers  on 
application.  Much  difficulty  was  experienced  in  securing  the  new  operas  an- 
nounced for  production  this  year.  Some  have  not  been  published;  of  others, 
published  abroad,  only  a  small  quantity  has  reached  the  dealers  in  this  city. 
At  least  one  copy  of  all  the  novelties  that  could  be  supplied — which  comprises 
about  half  the  list — has  been  reserved  for  room  use.  In  a  few  cases  extra  copies 
for  circulation  have  been  found.  Librettos  are  just  as  scarce.  At  present  only 
two  are  in  print.  Below  is  a  list  giving  references  to  books  containing  plots.

CHICAGO    PUBLIC   LIBRARY 


U.  S.  Civil  Service 
Examinations 

Attention  is  called  to  the  opportuni- 
ties for  service  presented  in  the  great 
number  and  variety  of  examinations 
for  government  positions  announced  by 
the  United  States  Civil  Service  Com- 
mission. Men  and  women  of  all  ages 
and  nearly  all  degrees  of  education  and 
experience  are  needed  by  the  nation  in 
many  capacities.  A  recent  bulletin  of 
the  Commission  says:  "The  United 
States  government  is  in  urgent  need  of 
thousands  of  typewriter  operators  and 
stenographers  ....  It  is  the  manifest 
duty  of  citizens  with  this  special  knowl- 
edge to  use  it  at  this  time  where  it  will 
be  of  most  value  to  the  Government." 

Notices  of  examinations  are  received 
daily  in  the  Civics  and  Documents  De- 
partment of  the  Library,  where  they  are 


posted  and  kept  on  file  for  reference. 
The  following  announcements  have  re- 
cently been  received,  some,  but  not  all, 
for  positions  requiring  residence  in 
Washington : 

Biologist, 

Bookbinder, 

Calculating  machine  operators, 

Clerks, 

Domestic  science  teachers, 

Draftsmen, 

Engineers, 

Inspectors, 

Indexers, 

Laboratory  aids, 

Manual  training  teachers, 

Sanitary  bacteriologists, 

Special  agents, 

Statistical  clerks, 

Stenographers, 

Typewriters. 


Thanksgiving  Day 

The  Circulating  Department  will  be 
closed  all  day  Thursday,  Nov.  29th; 
reading  and  reference  rooms  will  be 
open  from  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.,  branches 
from  2  to  6  p.  m.

Books  Added  to  the  Library 


Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those 

marked  Doc.  are  in  the  Document  department.  *Ser.  represents 

serials  which  do  not  circulate.

AMERICAN  RED  CROSS

THE  RED  CROSS  CHRISTMAS  SEAL 
By  Theodosia  Garrison 

OH,  happy  folk,  contented  folk,  and  ye  that  go  with  gold 
To  seek  within  the  noisy  mart  the  gifts  to  mark  the  day 
Jolly  toys  and  gems  and  lace  and  trinkets  manifold 

Here  be  better  wares  to  buy  along  the  crowded  way. 

Buy  a  pair  of  red  cheeks  to  give  a  little  lad  again, 

Buy  a  pallid  woman's  face  the  bright  eyes  of  health, 

Buy  a  broken  man  a  hope,  buy  the  strength  he  had  again, 
Here  arc  bargains  wonderful  awaiting  on  your  wealth. 

Oh,  happy  folk  and  careless  folk,  the  world's  bazar  is  piled 
With  lovely  gifts  and  lasting  gifts  to  mark  a  holiday. 

You  who  seek  the  fairest  thing  for  lover,  friend  and  child. 
Surely  ye  shall  pause  awhile  and  buy  the  while  ye  stay. 

Buy  a  mother  back  her  bairn,  buy  a  man  his  wife  again, 
Buy  a  lad  the  right  to  love,  a  child  the  right  to  play. 

Buy  the  wistful  kindred  all,  home  and  health  and  life  again. 
And  God  be  with  you  gentlefolk  who  purchase  these  today. 

Reprinted  from  The  Surrey. 


Chicago  Public  Library 

Book  Bulletin 

Volume  7  December,  1917  No.  10 

War  Service  of  the  Public  Library 

At  its  first  meeting  following  the  Declaration  of  War  the  Board  of  Directors 
by  resolution  made  a  formal  tender  of  the  building,  resources  and  equipment  of 
the  Library  to  the  United  States  government  "for  any  purpose  or  use  deemed  nec- 
essary and  proper  in  the  present  crisis ;  to  become  a  distributing  station  for  official 
literature,  military  and  naval  instruction  manuals  or  other  material,  to  serve  as  a 
registration  center  for  any  activities  properly  or  conveniently  administered  from 
or  in  the  Library  building  or  in  any  other  way,  conformable  to  and  not  unduly 
interfering  with  its  own  public  activities,  to  contribute  toward  the  general  organ- 
ization of  the  national  forces." 

It  was  further  ordered  that  $2,500  be  set  aside  from  the  Book  Fund  for  the 
purchase  of  drill  manuals  and  other  books  on  military  organization  and  instruc- 
tion in  such  quantities  as  may  be  found  to  be  necessary,  and  that  the  War  Depart- 
ment be  requested  to  recommend  books  which  should  be  made  available  to  the  citi- 
zens to  fit  them  for  military  or  other  national  duty  in  this  emergency. 

Subsequent  measures  coming  under  the  head  of  war  time  activities  are  the 
following : 

Red  Cross  registration  desks  were  installed  in  the  Main  Library  and  all 
branches  in  charge  of  members  of  the  Library  staff  for  the  week  of  May  7th  to 
12th.  Nearly  four  hundred  persons  were  registered  and  contributions  of  over 
$600  were  received. 

The  movement  for  the  collection  of  books  for  the  various  camps  was  inaugu- 
rated April  12th.  Appeals  to  contribute  books  were  issued  in  the  form  of  placards, 
newspaper  notices,  slides  in  moving  picture  theaters  and  communications  to  clubs. 
Prompt  and  hearty  response  was  received  and  books  began  to  pour  in  at  once  in 
great  quantities.  Up  to  September  first  about  60,000  volumes  were  received.  These 
were  inspected  as  to  fitness,  sorted  into  convenient  lots,  labelled,  boxed  and  shipped 
to  eleven  national  guard  camps  in  Illinois,  among  them  Fort  Sheridan  and  Great 
Lakes  Naval  Training  Station,  to  which  1,000  and  3,000  books  were  delivered  on 
June  6th. 

Twelve  thousand  books  were  sent  to  Camp  Grant,  Rockford,  111.,  soon  after 
the  opening  of  this  camp;  a  second  shipment  of  15,000  volumes  has  now  been 
added. 


166 CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 

Since  September  first  the  Public  Library  has  become  the  receiving  station 
for  all  books  for  soldiers  received  in  public  libraries  in  Wisconsin,  northern  Illi- 
nois and  western  Michigan,  acting  in  co-operation  with  the  American  Library  Asso- 
ciation, which  has  assumed  charge  of  a  nation-wide  campaign.  About  80,000 
books  were  received  from  this  source  during  September,  October  and  November. 
After  sorting  and  labeling,  48,000  have  been  distributed  between  thirteen  southern 
encampments  and  Camp  Grant  and  Great  Lakes.  Over  12,000  volumes  are  at 
present  boxed  and  stored  awaiting  shipping  directions. 

Of  a  total  of  nearly  150,000  books  thus  far  received  about  eighty  per  cent 
were  found  fit,  in  character  and  condition,  for  the  use  of  the  soldiers.  The  re- 
mainder are  being  disposed  of  to  second-hand  dealers  or,  when  dilapidated  or  in- 
complete, as  waste  paper,  and  the  proceeds  applied  to  the  purchase  of  new  books  or 
the  defrayal  of  incidental  expenses  for  which  no  appropriation  is  available.  Books 
are  shipped  to  the  camps  at  government  charge  under  U.  S.  Quartermaster's  label. 

All  the  work  of  receiving,  handling,  inspecting,  labeling  and  shipping  this  enor- 
mous mass  of  books  has  been  performed  by  the  regular  Library  staff,  and  by  far 
the  greater  portion  of  it  by  volunteer  service  contributed  by  employees  after  work- 
ing hours  and  on  holidays. 

Contributions  were  received  at  the  Main  Library  and  all  branches  for  the  mil- 
lion dollar  Library  War  Fund.  In  one  branch  alone — Austin — over  one  hundred 
dollars  was  collected.  The  total  received  from  Library  patrons  and  staff  exceeded 
$500. 

Scrap  books  were  offered  to  the  public  to  be  filled  with  bright  pictures  and 
suitable  reading  matter  for  sick  and  wounded  soldiers  in  hospitals  at  home  or 
abroad.  The  first  thousand  scrap  books  were  supplied  at  the  expense  of  Dr.  Max 
Henius,  former  President  of  the  Board.  These  were  quickly  taken  up.  An  addi- 
tional ten  thousand  was  then  supplied  by  the  Chicago  Daily  News.  All  the 
scrap  books  have  now  been  given  out  and  most  have  been  returned.  This  service 
has  been  discontinued. 

At  the  request  of  the  American  Library  Association  War  Service  Committee 
the  Acting  Librarian  has  assumed  general  supervisi  on  of  the  erection  of  library 
buildings  and  the  organization  of  thoroughly  equipped  libraries  at  Camp  Grant  and 
Great  Lakes.  Specially  planned  library  buildings  have  been  erected,  fitted,  fur- 
nished and  stocked  with  splendid  libraries  in  charge  of  trained  librarians.  This 
service  is  made  possible  by  the  successful  conclusion  of  the  campaign  for  one  mil- 
lion dollars  for  camp  libraries  conducted  by  the  Association. 

By  order  of  the  Board  the  expert  services  of  Mr  John  F.  Phelan,  Chief  of 
Branches,  have  been  placed  at  the  disposal  of  the  Library  War  Service  Commit- 
tee for  the  installation  and  general  management  of  the  libraries  above  described. 

Special  efforts  have  been  made  by  means  of  bulletins,  posters  and  exhibits  to 
call  attention  to  the  numerous  and  highly  useful  pamphlets  and  other  publications 
of  the  U.  S.  government  relating  to  matters  of  urgent  public  interest.  The  fullest 
co-operation  has  been  arranged  with  the  Food  Administration,  Council  of  Na- 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


167 


tional  Defense  and  other  similar  agencies.  Literature  issued  by  them  has  been 
promptly  made  available  and,  where  possible,  has  been  secured  in  sufficient  quanti- 
ties for  free  distribution.  Thirty  thousand  copies  of  the  valuable  bulletins  of  the 
National  Emergency  Food  Garden  commission  have  been  so  distributed.  A  mem- 
ber of  the  staff  familiar  with  this  work  has  been  assigned  to  attend  and  address 
meetings  of  clubs  and  other  bodies  interested  in  food  conservation  and  similar 
topics. 

Space  has  been  freely  granted  in  the  Main  Library  and  various  branches  for 
the  registration  of  women,  for  the  work  of  the  draft  exemption  boards  and  for 
the  display  of  official  posters  or  proclamations.  A  Food  Conservation  Exhibit 
will  be  installed  in  the  near  future. 

The  Collegiate  Periodical  League  was  invited  to  make  the  Public  Library  the 
headquarters  for  its  activities  in  the  collection  and  distribution  of  current  period- 
icals for  soldiers  and  sailors  in  Camp  Grant  and  Great  Lakes. 

A  Red  Cross  Chapter  with  a  membership  of  45  was  organized  among  the 
women  of  the  staff,  with  weekly  sessions,  after  service  hours,  in  the  Library 
building. 

Opportunity  was  given  to  official  solicitors  to  address  assemblies  of  the  staff 
in  behalf  of  both  the  first  and  second  Liberty  Loan.  Nearly  sixty  members  are  en- 
rolled as  subscribers. 

Three  hundred  thousand  printed  slips,  issued  by  the  Woman's  Liberty  Loan 
Committee,  appealing  for  subscriptions  to  the  second  loan,  were  distributed  in  li- 
brary books  to  borrowers  during  October. 

Space  has  been  granted  in  the  Library  building  for  a  rest  room  and  canteen 
for  soldiers  and  sailors  on  furlough  in  the  city,  under  the  management  of  a  com- 
mittee of  the  Chicago  Woman's  Club. 

A  service  flag  with  fifteen  stars  was  purchased  by  the  Board  and  hangs  in 
the  rotunda  of  the  Circulation  Department. 


Library  Not  Open  Christmas 

Every  department  of  the  Main  Li- 
brary will  be  closed  on  Tuesday,  Dec. 
25,  and  likewise  all  branches.  On 
Christmas  Eve  the  branches  will  be 
closed  at  5 :30  p.  m. 

On  New  Year's  day  all  branches  will 
remain  closed.  The  Circulation  de- 
partment of  the  Main  Library  will  also 
be  closed.  Reading  and  Reference 
Rooms  open  from  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.

Books  Added  to  the  Library 


Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those 

marked  Doc.  are  in  the  Document  department.   *Ser.  represents 

serials  which  do  not  circulate.

ILLINOIS 

By  thy  rivers  gently  flowing,  Illinois,  Illinois, 
O'er  the  prairie  verdant  growing,  Illinois,  Illinois, 

Comes  the  echo  o'er  the  breeze, 

Rustling  through  the  leafy  trees; 
And  its  mellow  tones  are  these,  Illinois,  Illinois, 
And  its  mellow  tones  are  these,  Illinois,  Illinois, 

From  a  wilderness  of  prairies,  Illinois,  Illinois, 
Straight  thy  way  and  never  varies,   Illinois,   Illinois, 

Till  upon  the  inland  sea 

Stands  thy  great  commercial  tree; 
Turning  all  the  world  to  thee,  Illinois,  Illinois, 
Turning  all  the  world  to  thee,  Illinois,  Illinois, 

Not  without  thy  wondrous  story,  Illinois,  Illinois, 
Can  be  writ  the  Nation's  glory,  Illinois,  Illinois, 

On  the  record  of  thy  years 

Ab'ram  Lincoln's  name  appears, 
Grant  and  Logan  and  our  tears,  Illinois,  Illinois, 
Grant  and  Logan  and  our  tears,  Illinois,  Illinois, 


Volume  8  January,  1918  No.  1 


Illinois 

The  Birth  of  a  State 
(From  the  Annals  of  Congress,  1818,  vols.  31  and  32} 

On  Friday,  January  16th,  1818,  Mr.  Nathaniel  Pope,  Territorial  Delegate 
from  the  Territory  of  Illinois,  presented  a  petition  of  the  Legislative  Council 
and  House  of  Representatives  of  the  Territory  of  Illinois,  "  praying  that  the 
said  Territory  may  be  formed  into  a  State  government,  and  that  the  State, 
when  formed,  may  be  admitted  into  the  Union,  on  an  equal  footing  with  the 
original  States;  which  was  referred  to  a  select  committee;  and  Messrs.  Pope, 
Claiborne,  Johnson  of  Kentucky,  Spencer,  and  Whitman,  were  appointed  the 
committee." 

On  Thursday,  January  22d:  "Mr.  Pope,  from  the  committee  appointed  on 
the  petition  of  the  Legislative  Council  and  House  of  Representatives  of  the  Ter- 
ritory of  Illinois,  by  leave  of  the  House,  reported  a  bill  to  enable  the  people 
of  the  Illinois  Territory,  to  form  a  constitution  and  State  government,  and  for 
the  admission  of  such  State  into  the  Union,  on  an  equal  footing  with  the  orig- 
inal States;  which  was  read  twice,  and  committed  to  a  Committee  of  the 
Whole,  on  Monday  next." 

Why  Chicago  is  not  in  Wisconsin 

In  the  original  bill  the  northern  boundary  of  the  new  state  was  established 
on  "an  east  and  west  line  drawn  through  a  point  ten  miles  north  of  the  south- 
ern extreme  of  Lake  Michigan."  Mr.  Pope,  acting  either  on  his  own  initiative 
or  upon  the  suggestion  of  others,  promptly  objected  to  this  limitation.  Four 
days  after  the  bill  was  introduced  he  wrote: 

"You  will  remark  that  the  northern  line  is  ten  miles  north  of  the  southernly  extrem- 
ity of  Lake  Michigan — Indiana  goes  as  far  north.  When  the  bill  is  taken  up,  I  will  en- 
deavor to  procure  twenty  or  thirty  miles  farther  north,  and  make  Lake  Michigan  a  part 
of  our  eastern  boundary.  I  shall  not  attempt  to  explain  the  importance  of  such  an 
accession  of  territory;  it  is  too  obvious  to  every  man  who  looks  to  the  prospective  weight 
and  influence  of  the  state  of  Illinois."  (Quoted  in  Buck:  Illinois  in  1818). 

When  the  bill  came  up  on  April  3d,  1818,  Mr.  Pope  moved  to  amend  the 
bill  by  striking  out  the  lines  defining  the  boundaries  of  the  new  States,  and 
to  insert  the  following: 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


"Beginning  at  the  mouth  of  the  Wabash  river;  thence  up  the  same,  and  with  the 
line  of  Indiana  to  the  northwest  corner  of  said  State;  thence  east  with  the  line  of  the 
same  State  to  the  middle  of  Lake  Michigan ;  thence  north  along  the  middle  of  said 
lake  to  north  latitude  42  deg.  30  minutes ;  thence  west  to  the  middle  of  the  Mississippi 
river;  and  thence  down  along  the  middle  of  that  river  to  its  confluence  with  the  Ohio 
river;  and  thence  up  the  latter  river  along  its  northwestern  shore  to  the  beginning." 

"The  object  of  this  amendment,  Mr.  P.  said,  was  to  gain,  for  the  proposed  State, 
a  coast  on  Lake  Michigan.  This  would  afford  additional  security  to  the  perpetuity  of 
the  Union,  inasmuch  as  the  State  would  thereby  be  connected  with  the  States  of  Indiana, 
Ohio,  Pennsylvania,  and  New  York,  through  the  Lakes.  The  facility  of  opening  a  canal 
between  Lake  Michigan  and  the  Illinois  river,  said  Mr.  P.,  is  acknowledged  by  every 
one  who  has  visited  the  place.  Giving  to  the  proposed  State  the  port  of  Chicago,  (em- 
braced in  the  proposed  limits),  will  draw  its  attention  to  the  opening  of  the  communi- 
cation between  the  Illinois  river  and  that  place,  and  the  improvement  of  that  harbor 
It  was  believed,  he  said,  upon  good  authority,  that  the  line  of  separation  between  In- 
diana and  Illinois  would  strike  Lake  Michigan  south  of  Chicago,  and  not  pass  west  of  it, 
as  had  been  supposed  by  some  geographers  who  had  favored  us  with  maps  of  that  country; 
and,  Mr.  P  added,  that  all  the  country  north  of  the  proposed  State,  and  bounded  by 
Lakes  Michigan,  Huron,  Superior,  and  of  the  Woods,  and  the  Mississippi  river,  must 
form  but  one  State,  Congress  being  restricted,  by  the  ordinance  of  1787,  from  erecting 
more  than  five  States  in  the  Northwestern  Territory. 

The  motion  was  agreed  to  without  a  division." 

The  amended  bill  was  then  sent  to  third  reading  and  passed  by  the  House 
on  April  6th.  In  the  Senate  the  question  of  the  northern  boundary  was  again 
raised  but  not  pressed  and  the  bill  was  passed  with  some  minor  amendments 
on  April  14th.  To  these  amendments  the  House  concurred  without  debate  and 
the  measure  became  a  law  by  the  signature  of  President  James  Monroe  on 
April  18th,  1818. 

Under  the  provisions  of  this  "enabling  act"  a  Constitutional  Convention 
Was  assembled  at  Kaskaskia,  the  territorial  capital,  in  August,  a  constitution 
drafted  and  adopted,  and  sent  for  approval  to  the  second  session  of  the  fifteenth 
Congress  in  November.  On  Friday  the  twentieth  November  "Mr.  Anderson 
of  Kentucky,  from  the  select  committee  to  whom  was  referred  the  constitu- 
tion from  the  state  of  Illinois,  reported  a  resolution  declaring  the  admission 
of  the  State  of  Illinois  into  the  Union  on  an  equal  footing  with  the  original 
states."  Some  ungracious  and  embarrassing  questions  were  asked  by  a  gentle- 
man from  New  York  as  to  "whether  it  appeared  from  any  documents  trans- 
mitted to  Congress  that  the  state  had  the  number  of  inhabitants  required  by 
the  law."  To  this  the  gentleman  from  Kentucky  was  able  to  reply  only 
"that  the  committee  had  no  information  on  that  subject  before  them,  beyond 
what  was  contained  in  the  preamble  to  the  constitution,  which  states,  that  the 
requisitions  of  the  act  of  Congress  had  been  complied  with,  and  that  the  con- 
vention had  therefore  proceeded  to  the  formation  of  a  constitution.  Mr.  A. 
said,  the  committee  had  considered  that  evidence  sufficient;  and  he  had,  in  ad- 
dition, himself  seen,  in  the  newspapers,  evidence  sufficient  to  satisfy  him  of  the 
fact,  that  the  population  did  amount  to  forty  thousand  souls,  the  number  re- 
quired." 

This  apparently  satisfied  the  suspicious  member  from  New  York,  for  the 
resolution  was  ordered  engrossed  for  third  reading. 

On  November  23d  it  came  up  for  passage  and  was  subjected  to  debate,  too 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


long  to  quote  here,  but  interesting  and  ominous  in  view  of  the  fierce  contro- 
versy engendered  early  in  the  following  year  over  the  admission  of  Missouri. 
The  chief  question  was  that  of  the  sufficiency  of  the  constitutional  safeguards 
against  slavery.  When  the  bill  was  finally  passed  by  the  House  on  the  same 
day,  by  a  vote  of  117  to  34,  the  alignment  was  significant  of  the  bitter  sec- 
tional feeling  which  was  to  arise  over  this  issue  during  the  next  forty  years,  to 
be  finally  swallowed  up  in  the  din  of  civil  war. 

The  President  approved  the  resolution  on  December  3d,  1818,  and  so  Illi- 
nois was  at  last  made  a  State  and  duly  proclaimed  as  such  in  these  words : 


Resolved  by  the  Senate  and  House  of  Representatives  oj  the 
United  States  of  America  in  Congress  assembled,  That,  whereas, 
in  pursuance  of  an  act  of  Congress,  passed  on  the  eighteenth  day  of 
April,  one  thousand  eight  hundred  and  eighteen,  entitled  "An  act  to 
enable  the  people  of  the  Illinois  Territory  to  form  a  constitution  and 
State  government,  and  for  the  admission  of  such  State  into  the 
Union,  on  an  equal  footing  with  the  original  States,"  the  people  of 
said  Territory  did,  on  the  twenty-sixth  day  of  August,  in  the  pres- 
ent year,  by  a  convention  called  for  that  purpose,  form  for  them- 
selves a  constitution  and  State  government,  which  constitution  and 
State  government,  so  formed,  is  republican,  and  in  conformity  to 
the  principles  of  the  articles  of  compact  between  the  original  States 
and  the  people  and  States  in  the  territory  northwest  of  the  river 
Ohio,  passed  on  the  thirteenth  day  of  July,  one  thousand  seven  hun- 
dred and  eighty-seven :  Resolved  by  the  Senate  and  House  of  Rep- 
resentatives of  the  United  States  of  America  in  Congress  assembled, 
That  the  State  of  Illinois  shall  be  one,  and  is  hereby  declared  to  be 
one,  of  the  United  States  of  America,  and  admitted  into  the  Union 
on  an  equal  footing  with  the  original  States,  in  all  respects  whatever.
