Chicago  Public  Library 

Book  Bulletin 


The  O'Connor  Lincoln 
(See  inside  cover) 


The  O'Connor  Lincoln 

ANDREW    O'CONNOR'S     "Lincoln     of     the 
Farewell  Address"  will  be  unveiled  on  the 
capitol  grounds  at  Springfield  on  April    18, 
with  impressive  ceremonies.     The  statue  is  of  heroic 
size,   about   ten    feet   six    inches   in    height,    and    will 
stand   on   a   carefully    designed   base   of   granite. 

The  sculptor  has  represented  the  great  American 
as  he  appeared  when  he  left  Springfield  on  February 
1-1,  1861,  to  go  to  Washington  and  assume  the  duties 
of  the  presidency.  The  pose  is  of  marked  simplicity, 
but  not  lacking  in  strength  and  dignity.  The  head  is 
most  remarkable.  The  face  reflects  all  the  human 
sympathy,  vision  and  reverence  expressed  in  his  fare- 
well address. 

Probably  no  artist  has  ever  succeeded  in  so  blend- 
ing all  the  essential  attributes  of  the  immortal  Lincoln. 
Here  is  a  man  of  the  common  people,  tall,  angular 
and  of  humble  simplicity,  and  yet  here  is  revealed 
with  impelling  impressiveness  the  lofty  spiritual  quali- 
ties that  made  Lincoln  the  incarnation  of  the  highest 
ideals  of  American  democracy. 

The  work  has  been  under  the  management  of  the 
State  Art  Commission  from  the  beginning,  who  are 
highly  pleased  with  the  results.  The  statue  will  be 
cast  in  bronze,  and  set  up  ready  for  unveiling  in 
April.  The  head  on  the  front  cover  is  of  the  com- 
pleted bronze. 

— From  the  Centennial  Bulletin. 


Chicago  Public  Library 

Book  Bulletin 

Volume  8  February,  1918  No.  2 

Illinois 

Some  Notes  on  the  Bibliography  of  its  History 

In  common  with  most  of  the  states  of  the  Union,  particularly  those  younger 
than  the  original  thirteen,  Illinois  has  never  had  her  history  adequately  recorded 
by  a  competent  historian.  The  reader  whom  this  centennial  year  may  move  to 
seek  an  acquaintance  with  her  pleasant  and  not  uneventful  story  will  find  him- 
self confronted  by  a  mass  of  undistinguished  efforts,  rambling  recollections,  arid 
recitals  of  political  events,  and  a  few  well-meaning  but  severely  pedagogical  school 
books.  Here  and  there  a  readable  monograph  deals  with  a  single  romantic  chap- 
ter, but  the  connected  story  of  our  state  must  still  be  pieced  together  from  ac- 
counts of  the  great  Northwest  Territory  as  a  whole  or  from  more  or  less 
doubtful  productions  ranging  from  a  lecture  by  an  early  settler  to  a  prefatory 
chapter  in  one  of  the  elephantine  county  histories  which  a  few  years  ago  waxed 
fat  upon  the  short  and  simple  annals  of  the  countryside.  A  complete  bibliog- 
raphy of  the  history  of  Illinois  would  serve  no  useful  purpose  and  would  take 
up  much  valuable  space.  Meanwhile  a  few  notes  on  some  of  the  more  profit- 
able books  may  be  of  interest. 

The  best  and  most  important  contribution  to  the  study  of  Illinois  history 
will,  as  is  proper  and  laudable,  be  found  in  the  several  series  of  publications 
of  the  State  Historical  Society  and  the  State  Historical  Library.  The  scholarly 
and  exhaustive  volumes  forming  the  Collections  of  the  Society  are  mainly  the 
work  of  a  group  of  historians  on  the  faculty  of  the  University  of  Illinois  who 
have  made  the  early  history  of  the  state  their  special  avocation.  An  even  more 
important  work  now  rapidly  progressing  from  promise  to  realization  is  the  Cen- 
tennial History  to  be  completed  in  six  volumes  under  the  imprint  of-  the  Illi- 
nois Centennial  Commission  created  by  the  General  Assembly  some  years  ago  to 
prepare  for  the  proper  observance  of  the  hundredth  anniversary  of  statehood. 
The  history  is  under  the  general  editorship  of  Professor  C.  W.  Alvord,  one  of  the 
above  mentioned  group.  Only  the  preliminary  volume  has  thus  far  been  issued, 
Illinois  in  1818,  by  Solon  J.  Buck.  If  those  to  follow  maintain  the  high  level 
of  historical  scholarship,  human  interest  and  sheer  readableness  of  Professor 
Buck's  narrative,  Illinois  will  have  a  noble  monument  raised  to  her  glorious  past 
and  the  centennial  year  will  not  have  come  and  gone  in  vain. 


14  CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 

Of  general  histories  now  in  print  the  one  by  John  Moses,  secretary  of  the 
Chicago  Historical  Society  in  the  eighties  and  an  enthusiast  in  local  history,  de- 
serves first  place  in  a  very  indifferent  company.  Bateman  and  Selby's  Historical 
Encyclopedia  is  also  useful  as  a  reference  book,  but  the  ponderous  makeup  of 
both  these  works  makes  them  far  from  inviting.  Moreover  they,  as  well  as  all 
other  existing  histories  of  Illinois  have  been  fairly  rendered  obsolete  by  the  labors 
of  the  new  historians  and  their  discoveries  of  new  facts  and  sources  recorded 
in  the  state  historical  publications  already  alluded  to.  Various  phases  of  state 
history  have  been  made  the  subject  of  separate  monographs.  Carter's  prize  es- 
say on  Great  Britain  and  the  Illinois  country  is  good  reading  as  well  as  good 
history,  as  also  are  the  various  contributions  of  Professors  Alvord  and  Boggess 
scattered  through  the  volumes  of  the  Illinois  State  and  the  Chicago  Historical 
Societies.  Illinois  under  French  and  British  rule  is  carefully  but  drily  treated 
in  Wallace  (all  books  here  mentioned  will  be  found  in  the  appended  list).  Noth- 
ing better  can  be  found,  nor  asked,  than  M.  M.  Quaife's  able  treatment  of  the 
historical  background  of  northern  Illinois  in  his  Chicago  and  the  Old  Northwest. 
Randall  Parrish  has  made  an  entertaining  book  of  the  by-products  of  his  studies 
in  the  writing  of  historical  romance.  Clark  E.  Carr's  I  Mini  has  been  classed  as 
fiction  but  gives  pictures  of  pioneer  life  in  the  early  years  of  the  nineteenth  cen- 
tury probably  as  trustworthy  as  many  others  put  out  with  more  serious  intent. 
The  late  Edward  G.  Mason's  Chapters  from  Illinois  history  are  excellent  and 
authentic.  The  important  episode  of  the  English  Settlement  on  the  Wabash  forms 
the  subject  of  a  book  by  Edwin  Erie  Sparks  and  one  by  George  Flower. 

There  are  a  number  of  essays,  lectures  and  whole  books  giving  the  recol- 
lections of  early  settlers.  These  vary  in  worth  in  accordance  with  the  experiences 
and  achievements  which  they  seek  to  record  as  well  as  with  the  ability  of  the 
writer  to  substitute  the  pen  of  the  historian  for  the  axe  of  the  pioneer.  The 
Fergus  Historical  Series,  an  admirable  collection  of  local  historical  papers  due  to 
the  public  spirit  of  a  Chicago  printer  of  a  generation  ago,  is  largely  made  up  of 
such  material.  This  collection  is  important  in  the  study  of  Illinois  and  Chicago 
history,  and  most  of  the  papers  are  interesting  and  well  written.  Exactly  the  same 
is  true  of  the  collections  of  the  Chicago  Historical  Society,  which  contain  many 
valuable  and  entertaining  contributions  to  local  history.  Chief  among  the  reminis- 
cences of  the  pioneers  must  be  named  the  two  books  of  John  Reynolds,  (1789- 
1865),  governor  in  the  thirties,  a  rugged,  Davy  Crockett  sort  of  a  figure  in  the 
life  of  the  period,  and  a  man  of  much  ability.  Copies  of  the  very  rare  first  edi- 
tions of  both  these  works,  printed  in  Belleville,  111.,  are  in  this  Library.  For 
the  discriminating  student  they  afford  much  valuable  collateral  material  and  local 
color.  In  them  the  "Old  Ranger"  has  not  only  recorded  his  recollections  of  the 
good  old  times,  but  has  taken  occasion  to  exalt  his  friends  and  "flay"  his  foes  in 
characteristic  fashion.  Finally  there  are  half  a  dozen  short  "histories"  compiled 
for  school  purposes,  and  useful  as  rapid  surveys  of  the  outstanding  and  uncon- 
troverted  facts. 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


15 


The  careful  student,  however,  will  not  need  to  be  reminded  that  this  hasty 
survey  is  both  incomplete  and  superficial,  and  that  a  large  quantity  of  source 
material  of  various  sorts,  many  contemporary  records  and  works  of  highly  impor- 
tant collateral  value,  are  available  in  this  Library  for  the  research  worker.  Atten- 
tion is  also  called  to  the  admirable  and  exhaustive  bibliographies  appended  to  the 
works  listed  below  under  Alvord,  Boggess,  Buck,  Carter,  Quaife  and  others.

"The  author  of  the  following  pages  has  en- 
deavored, by  interweaving  fact  with  fiction,  to  give 
his  conception  of  the  position  and  influence  of 
Illinois  among  the  sisterhood  of  states,  as  well  a* 
his  estimate  of  events,  and  of  those  Ilhnoisans  who 
were  conspicuous  actors  in  them,  from  1850,  when 
the  Fugitive-slave  law  was  enacted,  to  the  opening 
of  the  civil  war." 

Carter,  Clarence  Edwin.  Great  Britain  and 
the  Illinois  country,  1763-1774.  1910. 

B7644 

Chadwick,  Mrs.  Mara  Louise  Pratt.  Stories 
of  Illinois.  1898.  H  3563 

For  children.

17 


Illinois  in  Fiction 


Brown,  Katharine  Holland.  Diane;  a  ro- 
mance of  the  Icarian  settlement  on  the 
Mississippi  river.  1904.  F  4640 

The  time  of  this  story  is  before  the  Civil  war, 
the  scene  on  the  Illinois  and  Iowa  banks  of  the 
Mississippi,  chiefly  at  the  settlement  of  the  ill-fated 
French  Commune." 

Icaria  was  established  in  1849  at  Nauvoo,  Illinois, 
and  removed  in  1857  to  Adams  county,  Iowa. 

Catherwood,  Mrs.  Mary  Hartwell.  Old 
Kaskaskia.  1893.  F3356 

Kaskaskia  was  the  first  permanent  European  set- 
tlement in  the  Mississippi  valley,  and  the  first  capital 
of  Illinois  territory.  It  was  settled  by  the  French 
at  the  close  of  the  seventeenth  century.  The  story 
gives  a  good  description  of  the  city,  a  picture  of 
the  life  and  social  character  of  the  French  and 
English,  well  contrasted,  and  a  vivid  description  of 
the  rise  and  overflow  .of  the  Mississippi  river, 
partially  destroying  the  city. 

Spanish  Peggy,  a  story  of  young  Illi- 
nois. 1899.  F  8158 

A  story  of  early  Illinois  (1836),  containing  an 
attractive  picture  of  Lincoln  as  a  young  man. 

The  spirit  of  an  Illinois  town  and  The 

little   Renault;   two  stories   of   Illinois  at 
different  periods.     1897.  F  3348 

The  first  story  describes  the  social  life  and 
customs  of  the  early  settlers,  also  gives  a  vivid  pic- 
ture of  a  tornado  (the  prairies  were  frequently 
visited  by  them)  and  the  terrible  results. 

Renault  was  a  French  youth  who  traveled  with 
the  explorers  DeTonty  and  Boisrondet  in  1680  to 
Illinois. 

Douglas,  Amanda  Minnie.  A  little  girl  in 
old  Chicago.  1904.  H  3619 

Descriptive  of  the  early  school-days,  and  the 
social  life  and  customs  of  Chicago. 

Eggleston,  Edward.  The  Graysons;  a  story 
of  Illinois.  1887.  F  3235 

"Detailed  picture  of  the  turbulent  life  of  the 
pioneers,  about  18SO.  Abraham  Lincoln  is  intro- 
duced as  counsel  in  a  murder  trial. 


Kinzie,  Mrs.  Juliette  Augusta.  Wau-bun; 
the  early  day  in  the  Northwest.  1873. 

F1008 

Same.     1856.  *B  1341 

Descriptive  of  the  early  manners  and  customs  of 
the  settlers;  also  a  vivid  description  of  the  Fort 
Dearborn  massacre. 

Kirkland,  Joseph.     The   McVeys;    (an   epi- 
sode).    1888.  F4664 
The   time   is   in   the    early    30*8   and   40's    of   the 
Illinois    farming   community. 

Zury;     the     meanest     man     in     Spring 

County;   a   novel   of   western    life.     1887. 

F4665 

Accurate  detail  of  Illinois  life  in  the  pioneer  days 
(early  30's  and  40's). 

Parrish,  Randall.  Beyond  the  frontier;  a 
romance  of  early  days  in  the  middle  west. 
1915.  F  16034 

Practically  all  of  the  action  of  the  story  is  occu- 
pied with  a  long  journey  from  Quebec  to  old  St. 
Louis  (Starved  Rock),  on  the  Illinois  river. 

When   wilderness   was   king;  a   tale   of 

the  Illinois  country.     1904.  F9624 

A  romance  of  the  second  war  with  England  and 
the  massacre  of  Fort  Dearborn  (1812). 

Reed,  Myrtle.  The  shadow  of  victory;  a 
romance  of  Fort  Dearborn.  1903.  F  9697 

Story  of  a  frontier  fort  in  the  days  of  Indian 
wars  (massacre  of  Fort  Dearborn,  1812);  strongly 
anti-English,  and  inspired  with  the  Munroe  doctrine. 

Richardson,  Major  John.  Hardscrabble;  a 
tale  of  Chicago.  1850.  F  17172 

Life  and  times  of  the  early  settlers,  with  a  descrip- 
tion of  the  massacre  at  Fort  Dearborn,  1812. 

Pageantry

an  historical  pageant  of  Illinois,  produced 
at  Northwestern  University  October  7, 
8,  and  9,  1909.  1909.  E 13823

MORE  BOOKS 

|     for  our  SOLDIERS  and  SAILORS      |

The  Camp  Library  at  Camp  Grant 


These  two  Camp  Libraries  were  organized  under  the  General 
Supervision  of  the  Chicago  Public  Library 


Volume  8  March,  1918  No.  3 


WANTED:     MORE  BOOKS  FOR  SOLDIERS 

MORE  BOOKS  —  hundreds  of  thousands  of  them  —  are  needed  for  the  Camp 
Libraries  erected,  equipped  and  maintained  by  the  librarians  of  the  country 
through  their  national  organization,  the  American  Library  Association. 

A  Fund  of  $1,700,000  (including  grants  for  buildings)  has  been  given  by 
the  American  people  for  the  work.  Not  a  dollar  of  this  fund  will  be  spent  for 
administration  at  headquarters  or  in  the  field  that  can  be  saved  for  books,  but 
it  must  cover  the  erection  and  maintenance  of  buildings  ;  the  purchase  of  equip- 
ment and  supplies;  salary  and  expenses  of  librarians  and  assistants  (many  of 
whom  are  not  volunteers)  in  camps,  dispatch  offices,  and  in  France;  travel; 
transportation  of  books  and  supplies  (a  very  large  part  of  which  can  not  be  at 
government  expense)  ;  subscriptions  to  periodicals,  and  the  purchase  of  such 
books  (hundreds  of  thousands  of  them)  as  will  not  be  obtained  as  gifts. 

What  Remains  to  Be  Done 

Half  a  million  books  are  still  required  to  meet  the  actual  needs  of  men  in 
the  34  large  camps. 

Another  half  million  are  needed  immediately  for  the  military  forts,  posts 
and  small  camps;  for  the  naval  stations  and.  vessels;  for  the  marine  corps 
training  stations  and  barracks. 

And  for  the  men  on  transports  and  overseas  there  is  need  for  an  almost 
unlimited  supply. 

For  every  man  in  service  there  ought  to  be  a  book  in  service.  That  means 
at  least  a  million  more  books  at  once.  And  books  wear  out  in  use.  They  must 
be  replaced  frequently. 

Hundreds  of  thousands  have  been  purchased  ;  but  every  dollar  available  for 
purchases  is  needed  for  the  sort  of  books  that  can  not  be  expected  as  gifts. 

Therefore  : 

Hundreds  of  thousands  must  be  obtained  as  gifts. 

Of  the  more  than  half  million  books  sent  by  the  Association  to  the  soldiers 
and  sailors  up  to  date,  one  hundred  thousand  were  purchased  out  of  the  fund 
collected.  The  rest  are  gifts.  Books  on  engineering  and  other  technical  sub- 
jects, books  on  the  war,  and  books  on  military  training  —  many  of  them  dupli- 
cated to  a  large  extent  —  have  comprised  the  major  portion  of  the  book  pur- 
chases. This  is  because  the  demand,  next  to  fiction,  is  for  books  of  this  char- 
acter. The  gift  books  which  have  been  sent  to  the  camps  and  elsewhere  are 
not  mere  heaps  of  books.  Every  book  is  carefully  scrutinized  by  a  librarian 
before  it  is  sent  to  a  camp,  and  there  are  several  large  dispatch  offices  in  which 
the  larger  collection  of  gift  books  are  sorted  and  prepared  for  use. 


30  CHICAGO   PUBLIC  LIBRARY 

What  You  Can  Do 

You  can  pass  on  to  the  men  in  khaki  the  books  you  have  enjoyed  but  will 
not  read  again. 

You  can  give  them  some  of  the  books  you  like  best — books  you  would  like 
to  keep.  They  will  like  them  too. 

You  can  send  novels,  tales  of  adventure,  detective  stories  and  standard  fic- 
tion ;  up-to-date  books  on  civil,  mechanical  and  electrical  engineering,  the  trades, 
business,  the  professions  and  agriculture;  recent  text-books  on  military  subjects, 
mathematics,  the  sciences,  and  foreign  languages ;  books  of  travel,  history,  biog- 
raphy, poetry  and  the  present  war;  dictionaries  and  new  encyclopedias;  inter- 
esting books  in  foreign  languages. 

Send  the  books  to  the  Chicago  Public  Library  or  to  any  Branch,  Deposit, 
or  Delivery  Station,  or  to  any  neighborhood  center,  shop  or  other  agency  that 
has  consented  to  become  a  Collecting  Station. 

If  none  of  these  is  accessible,  telephone  the  Public  Library:  "Central  5822, 
Books  for  Soldiers,"  and  leave  your  address,  when  books  will  be  called  for  by 
auto  truck. 

Please  avoid  the  latter  alternative  if  possible,  as  we  expect  to  be  over- 
whelmed with  requests  to  call  for  books. 

Headquarters  of  the  A.  L.  A.  War  Service  are  in  the  Library  of  Congress, 
Washington,  D.  C.,  with  Dr.  Herbert  Putnam  as  General  Director. 

The  Chicago  Public  Library  is  the  principal  Dispatch  Office  for  the  mid- 
dle west  and  the  central  receiving  station  for  this  campaign  in  and  around 
Chicago. 

What  the  Boys  Are  Reading 

Experience  in  the  camps  indicates  that  our  soldiers  and  sailors  want  books 
of  great  variety,  but  there  are  certain  limitations.  The  following  list  of  subjects 
is  based  on  reports  and  recommendations  from  the  camp  librarians : 

Fiction 

Adventure  Love  stories  of  the  best  sort 

Western  stories  Stories  of  business 

Detective  stories  Historical  novels 

Non-Fiction 

Military  science  Automobiles 

Aeroplanes  Electricity 

Engineering  Mechanics 

Agriculture  Horses 

Business  Drawing 

Books  on  the  war,  especially  the  "tales  from  the  trenches" 

Wireless  telegraphy  and  telephony 

Arithmetic,  algebra,  geometry,  etc. 

Letter-writing  (personal  and  business) ;  filing 

Poetry,  old  and  new 

Biography,  especially  autobiography 

Travel  and  history,  especially  of  Western  Europe 

French  conversation  books 

Popular  books  in  foreign  languages 


BOOK  BULLETIN  31 


Two  general  classes  of  reading  are  shown  by  the  reports  from  the  camp 
librarians.  One  of  them  is  recreational,  and  causes  demand  for  fiction,  poetry, 
and  drama.  The  other  is  informational  and  causes  demand  for  books  dealing 
with  the  numerous  aspects  of  military  science,  including  artillery,  aviation,  sig- 
naling, technique  of  drilling,  electricity,  mechanical  and  civil  engineering, 
transportation,  diet,  foodstuffs,  roadmaking,  care  of  horses,  making  and  repair- 
ing equipment,  and  other  topics  relating  to  the  present  business  of  the  men, 
namely,  war.  In  addition,  there  is  much  reading  dealing  with  farming,  trades, 
occupations,  and  the  forms  of  activity  which  engaged  the  men  before  they  went 
into  camp.  There  is  much  interest  in  the  study  of  French,  and  particularly  so 
in  histories  and  descriptions  of  France.  Books  dealing  with  the  war,  its 
causes,  and  the  issues  involved,  and  narratives  of  personal  experiences  in  the 
war,  are  greatly  in  demand. 

"Camp  Library  Service  has  been  established  for  just  one  purpose,"  said 
Burton  E.  Stevenson,  at  Camp  Sherman  recently,  "that  is  to  help  win  the  war, 
and  there  are  three  ways  in  which  it  can  help :  first,  by  helping  to  maintain  the 
morale  of  the  men  by  providing  them  with  interesting  and  entertaining  reading 
matter  to  help  tide  over  the  moments  of  loneliness  and  depression  which  come 
to  everyone;  second,  by  helping  to  educate  them  as  to  the  causes  and  pur- 
poses of  the  war,  and  make  them  realize  that  they  are  not  fighting  France's 
fight,  England's  fight,  or  Italy's  fight,  but  America's  fight — that  it  is  not  Bel- 
gium or  France  or  England  that  Germany  is  seeking  to  destroy,  but  the  ideals 
and  principles  which  form  the  very  foundation  stones  of  this  Republic;  and 
third,  by  providing  the  men  with  special  technical  books  along  their  several 
lines,  and  so  making  them  better  and  more  efficient  soldiers." 


In  the  Field,  France 
Jan.  24,  1918 
Chicago  Public  Library, 
Chicago,  111. 
Dear  Sirs : — 

We  have  received  the  four  boxes  of  books  so 
kindly  addressed  to  the  llth  Engineers  by  you,  and  I 
may  say  candidly  for  all  the  officers  and  men  of  the 
regiment  that  the  volumes  are  highly  appreciated. 
They  will  serve  to  while  away  many  a  tedious  hour 
after  the  day's  toil  is  done. 

Please  accept  the  heartiest  thanks  of  the  llth 
Engineers. 

Yours  sincerely, 
(Signed)     Pierre  H.  Levesque, 

Chaplain.

April  1918 


Chicago  Public  Library 

Book  Bulletin 


Use  Your  Library 

To  Help 

Win  The  War 


Your  Government 
Is  Using  It 

To  Show    You  How 


Books  for  Soldiers 

Sixty  thousand  books  have  been  received  by  the 
Public  Library  during  the  past  month  as  gifts  for 
the  Camp  Libraries.  This  is  but  the  beginning  of 
a  campaign  that  will  continue  throughout  the  sum- 
mer for  a  continuous  supply  of  clean,  bright,  suit- 
able literature  to  replenish  the  stock  in  the  forty 
camps  and  naval  stations  and  the  libraries  at  the 
front.  Books  in  heavy  use  wear  out  rapidly.  The 
more  popular  they  are  the  more  quickly  they  must 
be  replaced.  Our  men  will  need  books  as  long  as 
the  war  lasts  and  the  supply  must  be  constantly  re- 
plenished. During  the  next  six  months  this  Li- 
brary could  place  fifty  thousand  volumes  a  month 
to  advantage  in  the  soldiers'  and  sailors'  libraries. 
Please  continue  to  send  us  your  books  especially 
your  NEW  BOOKS  as  soon  as  you  have  read  them. 


Chicago  Public  Library 

Book  Bulletin 

Volume  8  April,  1918  No.  4 

USE  YOUR  LIBRARY 

To  Help  Win  the  War 

Every  library  from  the  Atlantic  to  the  Pacific  is  feeling  the  surge  of  new  life 
and  energy  as  it  organizes  its  forces  into  effective  order  for  the  long  hard  struggle, 
mobilizing  its  resources  so  as  to  be  most  helpful  to  the  need  of  its  community,  devis- 
ing new  ways  of  presenting  the  Nation's  orders,  appeals,  and  instructions — recog- 
nizing its  responsibility,  and  offering  its  utmost  to  the  Government  and  the  people. 

With  the  declaration  of  war,  American  libraries  began  organizing  and  planning 
their  part,  recognizing  their  opportunity,  great  and  perhaps  vital  in  its  importance,  as 
the  voice  of  the  Government  to  the  people,  in  placing  before  them  its  messages, 
issued  under  the  stress  of  the  national  emergency  and  invoking  the  whole-hearted, 
loyal  cooperation  of  every  citizen. 

Libraries  are  the  munitions  plants  for  the  home  firing-line  and  have  "shot  and 
shell"  with  which  the  home  army  can  effectively  fight  Ignorance,  Apathy,  Misin- 
formation, Unpatriotic  Spirit,  Wastefulness,  and  Selfishness.  It  is  the  time  to  fight 
and  fight  hard  if  we  would  win;  and  win  we  must! 

Use  your  Library  to  help  win  the  war. 

Mr.  Hoover,  realizing  the  importance  of  the  libraries  to  the  Government  and 
to  the  community  at  large  at  this  time,  issued  some  months  ago  an  appeal  emphasiz- 
ing these  points,  and  as  a  part  of  the  efficient  organization  of  the  Food  Administra- 
tion, there  is  today  a  Library  Publicity  Director  in  every  state,  working  under  and 
with  the  state  Food  Administrator,  assisting  libraries  in  the  local  food  campaigns. 

The  departments  at  Washington,  the  emergency  war  organizations,  the  state 
governments  and  state  universities  are  turning  out  thousands  of  helpful  publications 
which  it  is  imperative  that  individuals  shall  read  and  use  in  order  to  meet  efficiently 
the  demands  upon  them.  These  are  being  sent  to  libraries  in  sufficient  quantities 
to  supply  home  circulation. 

The  Chicago  Public  Library  has  all  of  these  in  its  document  reference  section, 
and  they  are  arriving  at  the  rate  of  over  a  thousand  a  month.  In  addition,  the 
library  has  a  circulating  document  collection  of  over  8,000  books  and  pamphlets,  to 
which  daily  additions  of  new  material  are  made.  Books  from  this  collection  may 
be  borrowed  from  the  Civics  and  Documents  Department,  or  obtained  through  any 
of  the  branch  libraries,  which  in  many  cases  have  their  own  copies  of  the  popular 
titles. 


42  CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 

Current  official  news  is  now  being  issued  through  the  Government's  daily  news- 
paper, the  Official  bulletin;  U.  S.  Employment  Service  bulletin;  Journal  of  the 
War  Trade  Board;  "Boy  power,"  the  organ  of  the  Boys'  Working  Reserve;  the 
"State  Council  news,"  the  weekly  bulletin  of  the  State  Council  of  Defense,  and 
other  departmental  bulletins.  These  are  all  on  file  in  the  Civics  and  Documents 
Department,  current  copies  of  some  are  posted  in  all  of  the  branch  libraries  and 
high  school  libraries,  and  also  posted  on  the  bulletin  boards  in  the  Circulating  and 
Reference  Departments,  and  Woman's  Study  Room. 

Food  Production  and  Food  Conservation  are  the  two  leading  questions  of  the 
hour  at  home,  and  scattered  over  the  main  library  and  in  all  of  its  branches  are 
bulletin  boards  which  suggest  reading  material  and  helps  on  these  subjects,  or  carry- 
ing the  message  of  imperative  need  through  posters,  bulletins,  or  graphic  display.  It 
is  worth  while  to  watch  these  boards  as  they  contain  material  of  importance,  and 
of  immediate  interest  and  value. 

Free  pamphlets  are  ready  for  distribution  to  those  who  desire  them.  These  are 
leaflets  giving  tested  recipes,  issued  by  the  Department  of  Agriculture  and  Food 
Administration  jointly  and  instructions  for  the  war  vegetable  gardens,  issued  by  the 
National  War  Garden  Commission.  See  the  lists  below. 

The  Open  Shelf  Department  has  a  collection  of  useful  garden  books  on  display ; 
the  Civics  and  Documents  Department  has  put  the  latest  garden  and  agricultural 
helps,  food  conservation  and  housewifely  thrift  material  on  view  in  a  display  case 
where  the  pamphlets  may  be  examined  easily,  and  selection  made  for  home  use.  Here 
are  the  helps  for  the  amateur  gardener,  school  gardens,  nature  work,  garden 
club  work,  Boys'  Working  Reserve  study  course,  and  supplementary  reading ;  food 
substitutes,  war  menus,  and  cookery  helps  of  all  kinds. 

Package  libraries  have  been  prepared  for  circulation. 
Food  conservation.     355.82. 
Backyard  and  vacant  lot  gardens.    352.557 
School  gardens.    374.4. 

A  member  of  the  Library  staff  is  an  enrollment  officer  in  the  Boys'  Working 
Reserve,  reaching  the  boys  not  now  in  school  and  registration  is  being  made  at  the 
Main  Library  in  the  Thomas  Hughes  Room,  and  in  every  branch  library. 

The  Library,  anticipating  demands,  has  prepared  for  them  along  all  the  lines 
relating  to  war  emergencies,  and  helps  of  all  kinds  will  be  found  for  all  needs  and 
ages.  The  daily  mail  brings  the  latest  decision  on  the  food  question,  the  latest 
word  on  efficient  production,  the  latest  help  for  the  housewife,  and  the  most  impor- 
tant and  popular  of  these  are  listed  in  Sunday  papers  and  throughout  the  week  in 
the  book  columns. 

It  is  interesting  to  note  that  the  Patriotic  Food  Show  of  January  is  still  bring- 
ing many  inquiries  to  the  library  for  material  shown  in  the  library  section,  for  the 


BOOK  BULLETIN  43 


carded  list  of  exhibitors,  for  posters  and  free  material,  and  for  advice  regarding  the 
organization  of  food  shows.  Many  are  being  held  in  the  city,  as  well  as  throughout 
the  entire  state,  and  all  have  resulted  in  a  deepened  spirit  of  patriotism,  of  more 
active  local  cooperation,  and  increased  loyalty  in  carrying  out  the  Government 
orders.  The  Food  Shows  are  in  reality  demonstrations  of  the  need  of  conservation, 
and  the  use  of  substitutes,  the  duty  of  increased  production,  and  above  all  the  value 
of  individual  effort  as  an  active  force  in  winning  the  war. 

A  Food  Production  and  Conservation  Exhibit  is  now  in  progress  on  the  fourth 
floor  corridor,  Washington  street  side  of  the  Library  building.  It  will  be  of  par- 
ticular interest  to  the  women  of  Chicago  to  know  that  the  helpful  and  attractive 
graphic  exhibits  have  been  planned  and  arranged  by  the  Housewifely  Thrift  Sec- 
tion of  the  Chicago  Woman's  Club,  which  enthusiastically  accepted  the  invitation 
to  cooperate  and  participate  in  this  exhibit. 

The  collection  of  twelve  large  numbered  posters  from  a  "Conservation  story" 
will  be  displayed.  These  with  the  other  similar  posters  are  part  of  the  war  service 
work  of  the  art  students  of  the  Chicago  Art  Institute,  and  are  a  contribution  to  the 
State  Food  Administration  for  food  show  work. 

School  posters  designed  by  the  children  of  Chicago  from  first  to  eighth  grades, 
doing  war  work  and  learning  war  lessons  in  vital  ways  which  will  bear  fruit  in  a 
patriotic  citizenship  in  the  days  to  come,  will  form  part  of  the  Library  Food  Show. 

The  exhibit  will  be  continued  for  a  month  or  longer.

BOOK  NOTES 

The  Food  problem  by  Kellogg  and  Taylor  is  a  wonderfully  interesting  and 
clear  statement  of  the  questions  of  today,  with  preface  by  Herbert  Hoover, 
that  everybody  should  read.  L  17856. 

Low  cost  cooking  by  Nesbit,  although  written  before  the  war,  is  a  very 
practical  help  in  the  present  crisis  as  it  gives  the  use  of  substitutes  and 
economical,  yet  nutritious  recipes  and  menus.  K  24706. 

Library  food  notes  is  a  snappy,  practical  monthly  bulletin  issued  by  the 
U.  S.  Food  Administration  for  libraries.  It  contains  lists  of  best  books  and 
pamphlets  and  periodical  references  on  food  conservation  and  production 
topics  and  suggestions  for  food  shows.  It  is  worth  reading.  Circulating  copies 
in  Civics  and  Documents  Depts. 

Community  leaflets  are  a  series  of  lessons  arranged  for  grammar  grades  and 
high  schools  by  the  U.  S.  Bureau  of  Education  and  the  Food  Administration. 
These  lessons  are  well  written  and  in  phraseology  appeal  more  to  the  adult  than 
the  boy  or  girl.  They  are  fine  outlines  for  club  or  debate  work  with  plenty  of 
references.  The  aim  of  the  series  is  to  present  the  history  and  need  of  food  pro- 
duction and  conservation,  and  develop  the  spirit  of  citizenship  through  the  study 
of  elementary  economics.  Circulating  copies  in  Civics  and  Documents  Dept. 

Food  problems  by  Farmer  and  Huntington  is  a  sort  of  war  arithmetic, 
made  to  show  you  one  way  to  win  the  war.  Compiled  primarily  for  the  Evanston 
schools  it  is  now  revised  to  meet  the  needs  of  a  wider  field  and  is  a  campaign 
of  education  for  character  and  thrift. 

Farm  craft  lessons  for  Reserve  Volunteers  is  the  series  of  lessons  for  the 
Boys  Working  Reserve.  Each  lesson  is  on  a  single  topic  describing  some  part 
of  farm  machinery,  farm  life,  farm  work,  but  also  of  interest  to  all  boys.  Sev- 
eral of  the  chapters  are,  Wrenches  and  other  machine  tools,  Some  useful  knots, 
Splicing  a  rope,  Rainy  days  on  the  farm.  Civics  and  Documents  Dept. 

Bibliography  of  food  economy  for  the  housewife  is  the  second  edition  of  the 
best  complete  list  of  books  not  only  for  the  librarian,  but  for  club  workers, 
home  makers  and  domestic  science  teachers.  It  covers  the  subjects  of  present 
problems  and  gives  the  key  to  the  best  material  in  books,  pamphlets  and  maga- 
zines. It  is  issued  by  the  State  College  of  Washington  as  a  library  bulletin. 
Circulating  copies  in  Civics  and  Documents  Dept. 

Visit  The  Library  Food  Show 

On  the  Fourth  Floor,  Washington  Street  Entrance,  of  this  Building 


48 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those 

marked  Doc.  are  in  the  Document  department.   *Ser.  represents 

serials  which  do  not  circulate.

CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Rules  and  Information  for  Patrons 


Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person 
residing  in  Cook  County  having  permanent 
employment  in  the  city,  may  enjoy  the  priv- 
ileges of  the  Library  by  having  a  resident 
of  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear  in  the 
city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon 
application  and  compliance  with  the  rules 
and  regulations  of  the  Board  of  Directors 
a  card  good  for  three  years  will  be  issued, 
which  will  permit  the  applicant  to  draw 
five  books  for  home  use. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges 
upon  application. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and 
may  be  once  renewed  for  the  same  period. 
Books  may  be  renewed  but  once.  Books 
of  recent  purchase  labeled  Seven-day  books 
cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed.  The  card 
must  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 
out  or  renewed. 

Books  issued  at  Circulating  Centers  or 
Delivery  Stations  may  be  returned  at  the 
Main  Library,  but  books  cannot  be  applied 
for  at  the  Main  Library  and  delivered  to 
Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery  Stations. 

Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  hab- 
its may  use  the  various  Reading  and  Refer- 
ence Rooms  of  the  Library.  No  card  is 


required  to  use  the  books  on  the  open 
shelves  or  the  books  in  the  stacks,  which 
must  be  applied  for. 

A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported 
to  the  Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books 
drawn  on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  pre- 
sented, and  for  all  fines  incurred.  If  a  card 
is  lost,  immediate  notice  must  be  given  to 
the  Library.  A  new  card  will  be  issued 
thirty  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  one  cent  a  day  on  each  work  of 
non-fiction,  and  three  cents  a  day  on  each 
work  of  fiction,  is  imposed  whether  bound 
in  one  or  more  volumes,  which  is  not  re- 
turned within  two  weeks  from  the  date  of 
issue.  An  additional  charge  of  two  cents 
is  made  for  each  notice  sent  for  the  return 
of  a  book,  and  no  other  books  will  be  de- 
livered to  the  person  incurring  the  fine  until 
it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the 
call  number  is  crossed  off  the  order  slip 
whenever  a  book  is  drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohibited, 
and  all  injuries  to  books  beyond  reasonable 
wear  and  all  losses  must  be  promptly  ad- 
justed to  the  satisfaction  of  the  Librarian. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  De- 
partments, third  floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  8.30 
p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Cata- 
logue, fourth  floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m., 
Sundays  and  holidays,  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People, 
fourth  floor,  open  9:30  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to 
5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room, 
first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street 
entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  closed 
on  Sunday. 


Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and 
newspapers,  fourth  floor,  Randolph  street 
entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays 
and  holidays,  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Music  Room,  fifth  floor,  Washington 
street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulle- 
tin are  issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and 
August,  and  are  distributed  free  of  charge 
in  all  departments  of  the  Main  Library  and 
in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be  mailed  to 
any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year  to 
cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published 
in  January  of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten 
cents  per  copy. 


May  1918 


Chicago  Public  Library

Illustration  from  CHICAGO  By  H.  C.  CHATFIELD-TAYLOR 

Permission  of  Houghton,  Mifflin  Co. 


Sing  me  a  song  of  idle  days, 

When  rosy  and  white  are  the  new-blown  mays, 

And  rosy  and  white  in  the'jwanton  breeze 

The  petals  fall  from*the  apple  trees, 

And  under  the  hedge  where  the  shade  lies  wet, 

Are  children  picking  the  violet! 

Sing  me  a  song  of  idle  days, 

When  Spring  is  queen  over  woods  and  ways  ! 

F.  W.  Bourdillon

Volume  8  May,  1918  No.  5 

Vacation   Reading:    A  Symposium 

T  T  EREWITH  is  presented  a  collection  o  f  lists  of  vacation  books,  contributed  by 
fl  members  of  the  Library  staff,  who  compiled  them  out  of  their  own  experiences 
and  according  to  their  own  tastes — and  derived  much  pleasure  from  their  compila- 
tion, which  they  hope  may  be  shared  by  many  readers. 

Among  other  interesting  things,  these  lists  point  to  the  conclusion  that  there  are 
at  least  as  many  kinds  of  vacation  books  as  there  are  kinds  of  vacations.  Or,  per- 
haps, that  there  are  no  such  things  as  vacation  books  at  all.  Here  are  books  for 
divers  moods  and  minds.  Books  that  will  prove  as  companionable  at  home  on  wintry 
evenings  as  in  bosky  dells  or  under  blue  skies ;  for  armchair  travels  as  well  as  for 
the  more  expensive  and  less  restful  kind.  Neither  background  nor  setting,  long 
journeys  nor  other  tiresome  preliminaries  are  required  to  make  vacations  with  these 
books — only  a  state  of  mind.  And  after  all,  is  not  that  the  sole  and  ultimate  essen- 
tial of  any  true  vacation  ?  That,  and  the  leisure  to  read — even  for  librarians ! 

So  far  as  the  Library  is  concerned  any  book  may  be  a  vacation  book,  that  is, 
may  be  one  of  ten  to  be  borrowed  for  all  summer,  excepting  only  the  very  newest 
publications  for  which  the  demand  is  still  heavy.  Five  of  the  ten  may  be  fiction,  but 
not  seven-day  books ;  the  rest  may  be  drawn  according  to  taste  from  any  or  all  other 
classes  of  literature ;  or,  the  entire  ten  may  be  non-fiction.  In  the  composition  of  the 
following  lists,  these  were  the  only  limitations  kept  in  mind.  For  the  rest  they  are 
the  untrammeled,  uncensored  and  unedited  contributions  of  two  score  staff  members 
who  joyfully  responded  to  the  suggestion  that  in  this  way  their  own  reading  hob- 
bies and  adventures  might  be  turned  to  account  by  a  larger  circle.  Occasionally  the 
same  book  appears  on  two  or  three  lists  made  from  entirely  different  points  of 
view,  constituting  a  cumulative  suffrage  not  without  interest.  It  should,  however,  be 
said  that  the  state  of  mind  in  which  the  lists  were  conceived  was  one  of  pleasurable 
sprightliness,  of  summer  fancies  and  the  lighter  sides  of  life.  But  as  to  that  the  lists 
themselves  may  speak.  Eighteen  of  them  are  here  given.  Another  twenty  will 
be  printed  in  the  June  Bulletin. 

Vacation  loans  are  made  on  and  after  June  first  to  any  registered  card-holder. 
Ten  books  or  less  may  be  taken  out,  of  which  not  more  than  five  may  be  fiction. 
Seven-day  books  and  other  very  recent  books  cannot  be  taken  on  vacation  loan.  The 


58 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


loan  must  be  made  in  one  transaction;  ten  books  or  less  will  be  issued  at  one  time 
for  the  whole  vacation  period  and  cannot  be  exchanged,  though  they  may  be  returned 
in  instalments.  Final  returns  must  be  made  on  or  before  October  first.  The  bor- 
rower's regular  library  card  must  be  surrendered  when  the  vacation  loan  is  made, 
to  be  returned  to  him  when  all  obligations  on  account  of  the  loan  are  cancelled.

seaboard.  F217 

A  story  of  adventure  and  daring  exploits  center- 
ing about  treasure  from  a  silver  mine  in  a  turbu- 
lent South  American  republic. 

Kipling,   Rudyard,  and  Balestier,  W.     The 
Naulahka:  a  story  of  the  West  and  East. 

F4724 

In  which  a  Yankee  gains  possession  of  a  neck- 
lace worth  a  king's  ransom  from  the  neck  of  an 
Indian  idol. 

Masefield,    John.    Dauber.    In    Story    of    a 
round-house  and  other  poems.         E  11265 
A  narrative  poem  full  of  the  color  and  action  of 
life  aboard  an  English  ship  rounding  the  Horn. 

Safroni-Middleton,    A.      Sailor    and    beach- 
comber. I  9240 
A  vagabond's  Odyssey.  19219 

Volumes  of  unconventional  travel  describing 
with  vividness  and  sparkle  adventures  in  many 


lands — interesting  especially  for  descriptions  of 
Samoa  where  the  author  met  Robert  Louis  Steven- 
son. 

Snaith,  John  C.    The  sailor.  F  18265 

Although  the  sailor's  treasure  was  neither  silver, 
nor  gold,  nor  precious  stones,  the  man  or  woman 
who  likes  Treasure  Island  will  like  this,  too.

C 11731 

Letters  written  from  the  South  Sea  Islands  by 
the  author  of  Treasure  Island.

Thrilling  tales  of  the  South  Sea  Islands  and  of

Corking  Books  for  Scorching  Days 


Barbusse,  Henri.    Under  fire.  F  10891 

t  A  forceful,  vivid  presentation  of  the  grim  reali- 
ties of  war,  written  by  a  French  soldier,  who  "suc- 
ceeds in  giving  us  an  unforgettable  impression  of 
the  war  as  it  exists." 

Ferber,  Edna.  Fanny  herself.  F 12933 
The  story  of  the  meteoric  rise  of  a  young  busi- 
ness woman,  Fanny  Brandeis,  who  after  achieving 
material  success,  realizes  that  the  ideal  things  in 
life  are  more  worth  while.  Fanny's  mother  is  de- 
lightfully human  and  lovable  and  as  much  of  a 
heroine  as  "Fanny  herself." 

Lagerlof,   Selma.     The   emperor   of   Portu- 

gallia.  F 14618 

A   tragic   story   of   the   love    of   an   old    Swedish 

peasant,    Tan   of   Ruffluck,   for  his  daughter,   Glory 

Goldie.     Simple  and  exquisitely  told. 

Oemler,  Marie  C.    Slippy  McGee.      F  15739 

A  fresh,  pleasant,  rather  melodramatic  story  about 

a  clever  American  crook,  who  through  intercourse 

with  the  right  kind  of  people,  reforms  and  becomes 

a  butterfly  man. 

Poole,  Ernest.     His  family.  F  16547 

A  truly  American  novel  showing  a  typical  Ameri- 
can family.  Besides  the  four  well-drawn  characters, 
Roger  Gale,  the  father,  Edith,  Deborah,  and  Laura, 
in  each  of  whom  Roger  sees  his  own  life  repeated, 
Mr.  Poole  draws  vivid  pen  pictures  of  modern  so- 
cial life  in  America. 


Child,  Richard  Washburn.  Potential  Rus- 
sia. I  7569 
A  friendly,  dispassionate  criticism  of  war-ridden 
Russia  before  the  Revolution.  Wholly  readable  as 
well  as  giving  us  the  desired  information  about  a 
much  talked  of  country. 

Empey,  Arthur  Guy.  Over  the  top.  A  6386 
A  vivid  and  interesting  account  of  trench  life 
"somewhere  in  France,"  by  an  American,  who 
served  for  a  year  and  one-half  under  the  British 
colors.  The  book  holds  the  attention  of  the  reader 
from  the  beginning  to  the  end. 

Ravage,  Marcus  Eli.     An  American  in  the 
making.  C  23888 

A  keenly  interesting^  story  _of  the  struggles  of  a 
young  Rumanian  immigrant  in  the  process  of  be- 
coming Americanized.  His  experiences  are  de- 
picted in  such  a  clear-sighted  and  humorous  style, 
that  one  is  loth  to  leave  the  book  until  it  is  finished. 

Service,  Robert  W.    Rhymes  of  a  red  cross 
man.  E 11520 

Spirited  verses  giving  vivid  descriptions  of  life 
in  the  trenches  and  of  war  in  general.  Very  read- 
able. 

Wald,    Lillian    D.      The    house    on    Henry 
Street.  L  8501 

A  vividly  absorbing  human  record  of  the  interest- 
ing experiences  of  a  social  settlement  worker. 

E.  M.

In  Vacation  America 


Gladding,  Erne  P.    Across  the  continent  by 
the  Lincoln  Highway.  I  4292 

Entertaining  and  at  the  same  time  a  practical 
guide  book.  Has  a  good  map. 

Longstreth,  Thomas  M.    The  Adirondacks. 

14918 

An  account  of  the  early  history,  settlements  and 
Indian  tribes  in  New  York  state's  natural  park,  by 
two  friends  who  walked  and  motored  there.  Has 
generous  guide  book  material. 

Johnson,    Clifton.     Highways    and   byways 
of  New  England.  I  4406 

Written  about  the  picturesque  and  historically 
attractive  country  from  Maine  to  Rhode  Island. 
Valuable  information  concerning  automobile  routes 
and  many  suggestions  of  interest  to  tourists. 

Mills,  Enos.     Your  national  parks.     14306 
A    complete    historical    and    descriptive    guide    to 
National  Parks.     A  list  of  books,  including  Govern- 
ment publications  is  given. 

O'Connor,  Elizabeth  (Mrs.  T.  P.).     My  be- 
loved South.  14457 

Delightful  chatty  sketches  of  life  in  the  Southern 
States  by  one  who  knows  her  South. 


Austin,  Mary.    The  ford.  F  10745 

California  is  the  theme  and  setting  of  the  story. 
Farming,  speculating  in  oil  and  working  to  regain  a 
ranch  lost  in  speculation  make  a  good  story. 

Brown,  Alice.     The  prisoner.  F 11388 

Story  of  a  man  in  a  small  town  in  New  Eng- 
land who  becomes  a  defaulter  to  satisfy  an  extrava- 
gant and  selfish  wife.  At  the  end  of  his  prison  term, 
instead  of  ^beginning  life  in  a  new  country  he  re- 
turns to  his  home  town  where  he  rises  above  the 
old  conditions,  and  triumphs. 

Kelland,    Clarence.      Sudden   Jim.      F 14476 

_A  clothespin  factory  is  left  to  a  young  man  by 
his  father,  who  rather  doubts  whether  there  is  any- 
thing in  his  son.  Sudden  Jim  is  the  name  he  earns 
which  is  descriptive  of  his  business  methods. 

Jackson,  Helen   Hunt.     Ramona.         F  4401 

A  tragic  love  story  written  to  expose  the  wrongs 
of  the  Indians. 

King,  Grace.    Pleasant  ways  of  St.  Medard. 

F 14574 

A  picture  of  New  Orleans  in  the  days  immedi- 
ately following  the  Civil  war.  A  collection  of 
sketches  rather  than  short  stories. 

E.B. 


Idle  Hours  in  the  Mountains 


Galsworthy,   John.     The    dark    flower. 

F  13122 

A  novel  which  is  a  keen  analysis  of  the  emotional 
life  of  a  man  who,  from  the  age  of  nineteen,  loved 
at  cross  purposes.  Told  with  a  sure  insight  and 
with  quick  touches  of  exquisite  feeling  especially 
in  the  first  part — youth. 

Jackson,  Helen  Hunt.     Ramona.         F  4401 

Written  to  expose  the  injustice  of  the  United 
States  government  policy  towards>  the  Indians.  The 
scene  is  laid  in  southern  California  and  the  author- 
ess takes  one  of  the  mission  Indians  as  her  hero. 
A  tragic  love  story  is  revealed  between  this  mission 
Indian  and  a  Spanish  girl  of  Indian  descent. 

Lincoln,  Joseph  C.  Mary  'Gusta.  F 14797 
A  Cape  Cod  story  of  a  little  orphan  girl,  Mary 
'Gusta,  who  is  taken  in  charge  by  her  step-father's 
two  old  partners.  She  makes  one  of  a  unique  house- 
hold of  which  these  two,  together  with  an  old  sea- 
cook,  are  the  members.  Very  delightfully  told  and 
entertaining. 

Locke,  William  J.    The  red  planet.    F  14752 

A  gripping  story  of  England  in  war-time  as  told 

by  Major  Meredyth  who,  invalided  since  the  Boer 


war,  could  at  first  only  watch  those  activities  in 
which  he  longed  to  join.  It  is  Betty  Fairfax,  type 
of  all  true  women  in  the  war,  who  gives  charm  and 
heart  to  the  tale. 

Willsie,  Honore.     Lydia  of  the  pines. 

F 19584 

An  honest  American  quality  radiates  from  the  girl 
Lydia  who,  having  been  left  motherless,  cares  for 
her  little  sister.  _  Her  trials  and  troubles  and  also 
her  friendship  with  John  Levin  all  go  to  make  up 
a  very  interesting  story. 

Barrie,  James  M.     Half  hours.          E  11803 
Contents:    Pantaloon;  Rosalind;  The  twelve-pound 
look;  The  will. 
A  collection  of  entertaining  short  plays. 

O'Shaughnessy,     Edith    L.      A    diplomat's 
wife  in  Mexico.  B  4821 

Letters  from  the  American  embassy  at  Mexico 
City,  covering  the  dramatic  period  between  October 
8th,  1913,  and  the  breaking  off  of  diplomatic  rela- 
tions on  April  23rd,  1914,  together  with  an  account 
of  the  occupation  of  Vera  Cruz. 

Brooke,  Rupert.    Collected  poems.    E  10688 

A  collection  of  verse  written  by  the  young  Eng- 
lish poet  who  was  killed  in  battle.

England.  1 4795 

A  motor  trip,  that  took  the  author  and  illustrator

Leacock,  Stephen  B.    Moonbeams  from  the 

larger  lunacy.  E  4208 

Contents:    Spoof;  a  thousand  guinea  novel. — The 


reading  public. — Afternoon  adventures  at  my  club. 
— Ram  Spudd. — Aristocratic  anecdotes. — Education 
made  agreeable. — An  every-day  experience. — Truth- 
ful oratory. — Our  literary  bureau. — Speeding  up. 
business. — Who  is  also  who. — Passionate  paragraphs. 
— Weejee  the  pet  dog. — Sidelights  on  the  super- 
men.— The  survival  of  the  fittest. — The  first  news- 
paper.— In  the  good  time  after  the  war. 

A   series   of   essays   cleverly   written,   full    of   wit 
and  humor.

Other  Countries — Other  Customs 


Cooper,  Elizabeth.    My  lady  of  the  Chinese 
courtyard.  1 10644 

Charming  letters  written  by  a  high-caste  Chinese 
woman  to  her  husband. 

Ellis,  Havelock.    The  soul  of  Spain.    I  7030 
Delightful  blend  of  the  comments  of  a  traveller,

Fenellosa,  Mary.     The  breath  of  the  gods. 

F 12920 

Story  of  a  Japanese  girl  who  was  educated  in  the 
U.  S.  She  returns  to  Japan  and  marries  a  diplo- 
mat. Throughout  the  book  there  is  a  conflict  be- 
tween American  and  Japanese  ideas  and  customs. 

Graham,  Stephen.    The  way  of  Martha  and 
the  way  of  Mary.  I  7577

Gloom  Dispellers 


Ade,  George.     Knocking  the  neighbors. 

F  10181 

Fables  in  slang  chuck-full  of  pure  American 
humor.  They  radiate  common-sense,  satire  and 
chuckles.  Will  be  re-read  and  read  aloud. 

Jerome,  Jerome  K.     Three  men  in  a  boat. 

16284 

A  comic  history  of  a  picnic  up  the  Thames, 
a  medley  of  buffoonery  and  droll  musings  on  fa- 
miliar things. 

Leacock,    Stephen.      Nonsense    novels. 

F 14683 

STiort  farcical  stories,  ridiculing  tendencies  of 
the  modern  novel.  Exaggeration  personified.  Good 
for  reading  aloud. 

Masson,  Thomas.     The  best  stories  in  the 
world.  E  4448 

About  500  really  funny  short  stories,  arranged  by 
the  editor  of  Life.  A  good  index  makes  it  useful 
for  speakers. 

Street,  Julian.     Abroad  at  home.         14303 

A  descriptive  tour  through  the  principal  cities 
of  the  United  States,  from  coast  to  coast.  The 
interest  lies  in  the  characterization  and  differenti- 
ation of  the  cities.  Needless  to  say,  there  are  no 
lost  opportunities  for  humor. 


Fitch,   George. 


At   good   old   Siwash. 

F 12972 


Will  always  remain  a  favorite.  Mr.  Fitch  knows 
the  small  middle-west  coeducational  college.  A  plot 
in  every  chapter.  Full  of  football,  intrigue,  fra- 
ternities and  rollicking  fun. 

Ford,  Sewell.     Torchy.  F 13026 

Mr.  Ford  is  an  artist  in  the  use  of  picturesque 
slang.  Torchy  is  a  lovable  ultra-ingenius  red- 
haired  office  boy.  A  guaranteed  gloom  dispeller. 

Rinehart,  Mary  Roberts.     Tish.         F  17230 
Ridiculous  tales  of  a  lively  spinster  of  fifty.  Should 
be  read  a  little  at  a  time.     Is  splendid  for  reading 
aloud. 

Wilson,  Harry  Leon.    Ruggles  of  Red  Gap. 

F 19586 

Humor  resulting  from  the  transplanting  of  an  Eng- 
lish butler  from  a  servile  position  in  England,  to 
one  of  perfect  equality  with  his  fellow  men  in  a 
woolly  western  town,  in  the  States.  The  result  is 
too  much  for  Ruggles  and  also  for  the  equanimity 
of  the  reader. 

Wodehouse,    Pelham    Grenville.      Picadilly 
Jim.  F 19699 

A  farcical  tale,  in  which  the  young  ^reprobate  hero 
shares  honors  with  two  meek  millionaires  married  to 
ambitious  commanding  sisters.  The  scene  shifts 
from  New  York  to  London,  a  red-haired  girl  com- 
plicates matters,  and  there  is  the  inevitable  happy 


ending. 


A.  F.

War  Wrinkles 


Aldrich,  Mildred.  The  hilltop  on  the 
Marne.  A  5805 

(Continuation  entitled— On  the  edge  of  the  war 
zone  A  6450). 

Actual  letters  relating  experiences  of  a  Boston 
woman  who  watched  the  battle  of  the  Marne  from 
her  window  and  threw  open  her  home  to  French 
and  English  soldiers. 

Bairnsfather,    Bruce.      Bullets    and    billets. 

A  6343 

Life  at  the  front  during  early  days  of  the  war, 
illustrated  by  clever  cartoons  drawn  by  the  author, 
picturing  humor  in  trench  life. 

Huard,  Frances  Wilson.  My  home  in  the 
field  of  honour.  A  6246 

(Companion  volume  entitled  "My  home  in  the 
field  of  mercy."  A6SS4). 

Interesting    experiences    of   the    author    who    was 
forced  to  flee  from  her  chateau  during  the  German 
occupation,  but  returned  to  establish  a  military  hos- 
pital out  of  chaos. 


Wood,  Eric  Fisher.     The  note  book  of  an 
attache.  A  5881 

Vivid  account  compiled  from  diary  written  by 
American  attache  at  Paris  at  the  outbreak  of  the 
war,  who  assisted  fugitives  from  the  city;  carried 
dispatches  to  other  embassies  of  Europe  and  who 
actually  witnessed  the  battles  of  the  Marne,  the 
Aisne  and  the  struggle  for  Calais. 

Bell,   John    K.     The   Smiths   in   war    time. 

F 10999 

Author  relates  in  humorous  strain  the  trials  and 
tribulations  of  Mr.  Smith,  age  73,  and  his  wife  who 
try  to  do  their  bit  for  England  by  economizing. 

Cholmondeley,    Alice.      Christine.      F 11940 

Rather  pathetic  letters  written  to  her  mother  by 
a  young  English  girl,  studying  music  in  Berlin  in 
1914.  Depicts  vividly  the  mind  ot  the  German  na- 
tion in  regard  to  the  war. 

Geraldy,  Paul.    The  war,  Madame.    F  13189 

Short  story  of  a  soldier's  impressions  upon  his 
one  day's  leave  in  Paris,  relating  the  effect  of  the 
war  upon  himself,  his  friends  and  the  city  itself.

CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Sherwood,    Margaret   P.     The   worn   door- 
step. F 18047 

An  American  girl's  fiance  is  killed  in  the  war 
and  she  forgets  her  loneliness  by  welcoming  with 
open  arms  the  Belgian  refugees  and  the  simple  vil- 
lagers in  her  little  cottage  near  the  North  Sea. 


Stevenson,   Burton   E.     Little   comrade. 

F 18375 

Bradford  Stewart,  an  American  surgeon,  plays 
the  part  of  husband  to  a  beautiful  French  spy  who 
is  carrying  messages  from  Germany  to  Gen.  Joffre 
in  France. 

C.  B. 


Land  of  the  Gods 


Anderson,  Isabel  W.     The  spell  of  Japan. 

19458 

Mrs.  Anderson  lived  in  Japan.  Her  husband  was 
American  ambassador  to  the  court  of  the  Mikado. 
She  writes  in  an  entertaining  fashion  of  social  and 
diplomatic  life  in  New  Japan. 

Edwards,  A.  Herbege.     Kakemona.     1950S 
Dainty   sketches  of  Japan,  her  people,   her  faith, 
her  art  and  her  gods. 
Hitchcock,  Alfred  M.     Over  Japan  way. 

19452 

A  book  made  up  of  the  impressions  of  an  Amer- 
ican tourist  in  Japan.  He  visits  inns  and  hotels, 
schools,  from  kindergarten  to  universities,  goes  shop- 
ping, attends  a  play,  beards  a  volcano,  visits  shrines 
and  sacred  places. 
Ponting,  Herbert  G.  In  lotus-land.  19502 

The  author  takes  us  to  the  less  known  parts 
and  peoples  of  Japan.  He  describes  the  beautiful 
flower  festivals  of  Tokyo. 

Sanders,  T.  H.     My  Japanese  year.     19457 
Gossipy,   readable,   attractively  illustrated  descrip- 
tions of  life  and  customs  in  Japan. 

Babcock,  Winnifred  E.    The  honorable  Miss 

Moonlight.  F  5762 

The  love  story  of  a  geisha  and  a  young  Japanese 


noble  and  the  inability  of  Miss  Moonlight  to  accept 
the  humble  position  of  a  daughter-in-law. 

Macaulay,    Fannie    C.      The    Lady    of    the 
Decoration.  F 14824 

The  story  of  a  yoting  Kentucky  widow  who  ac- 
cepts an  offer  to  go  to  Japan  and  teach  in  a  mis- 
sion school,  told  in  letters  written  to  an  old  friend 
at  home. 

The  Lady  and  Sada  San.  F  14825 

Sequel  to  "The  Lady  of  the  Decoration." 

Fenollosa,,  Mary.     The  breath  of  the  gods. 

F 12920 

The  scene  is  laid  partly  in  Washington,  partly 
in  Japan.  Story  of  an  American  diplomat  and  his 
family  stationed,  in  Japan. 

Long,  John  Luther.     Miss  Cherry  Blossom 

of  Tokyo.  F  4977 

The  romance  of  a  young  American,  Secretary  of 

the  Legation   at  Tokyo,  and  a  high  caste  Japanese 

girl  who  had  been  educated  at  Bryn  Mawr  college. 

Loti,  Pierre,  pseud.    Madame  Chrysantheme. 

F7325 

An  interlude  in  Loti's  own  life  in  Nagasaki.  The 
character  of  the  Japanese  is  unfairly  presented  but 
the  scenery  is  beautifully  done. 

F.  L.  B. 


The  Happy  Valley 


"Let  us  be  glad  we  are  here,  for  the  world  we 
live  in  is  a  weary  place;  sorrow  and  suffering 
haunt  all  our  waking  hours.  The  thunder  of  war 
.  .  .  ever  in  our  _  ears_,  and  the  red  tulips  that 
blossom  this  fair  spring-time  recall  unhappy  lands." 

"But  here,"  said  one  who  lived  in  that  valley, 
"here  is  solace  and  forgetfulness;  yes,  and  mirth, 
wonder,  and  delight." 

— Chronicle  of  Joh.  Bibliothecarius.

work,  to  topics  of  the  day,  and  the  oldest  joke."

Books  Added  to  the  Library 


Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those 

marked  Doc.  are  in  the  Document  department.   *Ser.  represents 

serials  which  do  not  circulate.

Library  War  Fund 
$1,700,000 


What  Has  Been  Done 

36  camp  library  buildings  erected 
117  librarians  now  in  the  field 
464  camps,  stations  and  vessels  served 
109,403  selected  books  sent  overseas 
300,000  books  purchased,  largely  technical 
1 ,349,000  gift  books  sent  to  camps  and  stations 
5,000,000  magazines  distributed  systematically 

(Including  "Buries on"  Magazine) 


A  Scrap  Book  Letter 


The  Chicago  Public  Library, 

Chicago,  Illinois. 
Gentlemen : — 

Last  Fall  when  you  issued  the  Scrap  Books  for  Soldiers,  I 
"did  my  bit" — and  turned  one  in.  The  other  day  just  ever  so 
nice  a  letter  came  from  a  chap  in  a  hospital  camp  in  Nebraska. 
Although  I  never  really  expected  to  hear  from  any  one  in  regard 
to  it,  I  assure  you  when  the  note  came,  I  felt  well  repaid  for  the 
time  and  thought  given  to  filling  the  pages  with  cheeriness  and 
interest-inspiring  articles. 

The  writer  of  the  letter  tells  me  he  is  21  — a  Wyoming 
Rancher;  and  expresses  a  wholesome  appreciation  of  the  book. 

I  have  responded  to  his  request  to  write  to  him  and  assured 
him  that  if  any  sunbeams  were  created  by  my  tiny  effort  to 
please,  I  shall  be  glad  and  glad  and  glad. 

I  might  add  that  he  said,  "I  feel  you  ought  to  know  that 
your  book  is  badly  torn  and  I  am  afraid  it  will  hardly  last  until 
the  war  is  over."  If  another  "Scrap-Book  Ship"  is  launched  I, 
for  one,  shall  be  mighty  glad  to  be  a  passenger. 

I  am  merely  a  by-stander  but  cannot  refrain  from  commend- 
ing you  for  the  loving-kindness  which  resulted  in  bringing  sun- 
shine to  so  many  hundreds  of  "our  Boys"  who  have  had  the 
misfortune  to  be  ill.  It  must  have  been  quite  an  undertaking; 
but,  at  least,  nothing  less  than  one  hundred  per  cent  successful. 

Cordially  yours, 


Patrons  who  still  have  Scrap  Books,  issued  by  the  Library  last 
Fall,  are  urgently  requested  to  return  them  at  once.  The  records 
show  a  considerable  number  still  outstanding.  Much  time  and  work 
will  be  saved  if  these  are  returned  without  further  notice. 


Chicago  Public  Library 

Book  Bulletin 

Volume  8  June,  1918  No.  6 

Vacation   Reading:  A  Symposium 

Part  Two 

TTHE  second  instalment  of  twenty  vacation  lists  is  here  added  to  the  eighteen 
1  printed  in  this  place  a  month  ago.  Again  they  are  the  free  contributions 
of  members  of  the  staff,  who  have  compiled  these  lists,  in  groups  of  ten  books 
each,  out  of  their  own  fancies  and  bookish  acquaintanceships.  Nothing  more 
needs  to  be  added  to  the  introductory  paragraphs  prefixed  to  last  month's  lists, 
but,  for  the  benefit  of  new  readers  the  regulations  governing  the  issue  of  books 
for  the  vacation  period  are  reprinted : 

Vacation  loans  are  made  on  and  after  June  first  to  any  registered  card- 
holder. Ten  books  or  less  may  be  taken  out,  of  which  not  more  than  five  may  be 
fiction.  Seven-day  books  and  other  very  recent  books  cannot  be  taken  on  vaca- 
tion loan.  The  loan  must  be  made  in  one  transaction ;  ten  books  or  less  will  be 
issued  at  one  time  for  the  whole  vacation  period  and  cannot  be  exchanged, 
though  they  may  be  returned  in  instalments.  Final  returns  must  be  made  on 
or  before  October  first.  The  borrower's  regular  library  card  must  be  surren- 
dered when  the  vacation  loan  is  made,  to  be  returned  to  him  when  all  obligations 
on  account  of  the  loan  are  cancelled. 

A  reader  in  New  York  City — for  the  Book  Bulletin  has  a  far-flung  sub- 
scription list — writes  to  express  his  interest  and  "very  particular  personal  pleas- 
ure" in  the  lists  in  the  May  Bulletin.  "And  by  the  way,"  he  continues,  "here  is 
a  service  that  you  conld  render — perhaps  have  already  planned  to  render — to 
many  a  poor  fellow,  and  woman,  too,  for  that  matter,  who  is  destined  to  spend 
a  vacation  imprisoned  in  the  mountains,  and  who  longs  for  the  smell  of  the  sea. 
Couldn't  you  make  up  two  or  three  lists  of  books  purely  of  sea  stories,  a  selec- 
tion of  the  very  best?  Last  summer  I  had  no  end  of  fun  with  "Two  Years 
Before  the  Mast";  "The  Wreck  of  the  Grosvenor";  that  rather  old  fashioned, 
but  still  corking  sailor's  yarn,  Cupple's  "Green  Hand";  that  perfectly  godless 
book  of  Morgan  Robertson's,  "Sinful  Peck" ;  and  Aflalo's  "Call  of  the  Sea."  But 
I  am  lost  for  this  year.  I  have  never  read  any  of  Bullen ;  I  have  never  read  any 
of  Conrad ;  and  don't  know  which  would  be  the  best  after  your  present  selection 
of  "Nostromo" ;  nor  do  I  know  which  of  Clark  Russell's  should  follow  "The 
Wreck  of  the  Grosvenor" ;  nor  which  other  of  Robertson's  to  choose.  I  would 


74 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


like  a  good  baker's  dozen  of  other  sea  stories.  Then,  of  course,  there  must  be  a 
collection  of  sea  verse,  including  some  old  chanteys.  I  wonder  if  there  are  not  a 
lot  like  me  that  such  a  list  would  appeal  to  and  serve." 

Fortunately  the  lists  as  contributed  included  one  set  of  "Sea-sonable  yarns." 
Another  has  been  added  into  which  were  incorporated  the  excellent  titles  men- 
tioned by  our  friend,  supplemented  by  some  others  selected  by  a  member  of  the 
Circulation  staff  to  whom  this  case  was  referred  for  treatment.  As  usual,  the 
term  "best"  proved  more  elusive  than  inclusive,  and  it  is  more  than  possible 
that  the  sea  stories  deemed  best  on  the  shores  of  Lake  Michigan  may  savor  too 
much  of  fresh  water  to  be  entirely  acceptable  within  sound  of  the  briny  Atlantic. 

One  of  the  books  named  by  our  correspondent  is  included  in  the  first  of  the 
following  lists  and  three  in  the  second ;  one — Cupples'  "Green  Hand"  is  not  in 
this  Library. 

The  charming  French  list  was  made  by  a  friendly  patron  of  the  Library  to 
whom  we  express  appreciation  of  his  interest. 


Seasonable  Yarns 


Conrad,  Joseph.    The  typhoon.  F8280 

A  story  based  on  a  single  episode — the  typhoon, 
which  is  most  vividly  described. 

Kipling,  Rudyard.    Captains  courageous. 

F4728 

A  boy  traveling  on  an  ocean  liner  falls  overboard 
and  is  picked  up  by  a  fishing  smack.  His  life  for 
the  next  few  months  with  the  fishermen  off  the 
Grand  Banks  is  very  interesting.  A  real  eea  story.

every  minute.

H2418

tempetes,  son  grand  mystere  attirant  et  souvent 
meutrier.  Et  ce  roman  fait  comprendre  1'ame  de  ce 
peuple  breton  qui  vit  de  la  mer  et  meurt  par  elle. 

France,    Anatole.      Le    crime    de    Sylvestrc 
Bonnard.  CC2106 

L'auteur  aime  1'enfance  et  dans  ce  livre,  un  de  ses 
plus  purs  chefs-d'oeuvre,  elle  est  depeinte  avec  un 
charme  penetrant,  une  comprehension  tres  fine  de  s» 
fragilite  et  de  son  charme. 

Daudet,  Alphonse.     Tartarin  de  Tarascon. 

C 12284 

Ce  livre,  c'est  le  "Don  Quichotte"  de  la  litterature 
franc.aise. 
Balzac.    Honore    de.      Eugenie    Grandet. 

CC2127 

Dans  ce  roman,  Balzac,  a  peinte  en  traits  saisis- 
sants  la  vie  d'un  avare,  dont  le  vice  est  une  veritable 
folie,  en  opposition  avec  la  noblesse  de  coeur  de  sa 
fille  Eugenie,  dont  les  actes  semblent  prouver  la 
verite  du  mot  de  Balzac:  "Sentir,  aimer,  souffrir, 
se  devouer  sera  toujours  le  texte  de  la  vie  des 
femmes." 

Riou,  Gaston.    Aux  ecoutes  de  la  France  qui 
vient.  CC  930

cette  belle  jeunesse  qui  s'est  si  heroiquement  sacri- 
fice sur  les  champs  de  bataille  afin  que  la  Patrie

American  Ideals 


"The  tendencies  of  a  nation  are  all  that  count." — 
Elihu  Root. 

Cooper,  Clayton  S.    American  ideals.    1915. 

14285 

One  hundred  representative  men  were  asked  to 
contribute  to  a  symposium;  of  these  48  named  serv- 
ice to  one's  fellows  as  the  leading  American  ideal. 

Croly,   Herbert    D.      Promise    of   American 
life.    1909.  L6997 

"An  important  contribution  to  our  knowledge  of 
the  underlying  principles  of  American  democracy.' 

Foerster,   Norman  and  Pierson,  W.   D.  Jr. 
ed.    American  ideals.     1917.  E  2976 

"A  collection  of  essays,  addresses  and  state  pa- 
pers that  eacpress,  from  the  point  of  view  of  Ameri- 
can statesmen  and  men  of  letters,  their  ideals,  past 
and  present." — Pref. 

Hill,   David  J.     Americanism:    What   it   is. 
1916.  L 15780 

"This  little  book  is  intended  to  set  forth  as 
clearly  as  possible  what  is  most  original  and  dis- 
tinctive in  American  political  conceptions  and  most 
characteristic  of  the  American  spirit." — Introd. 

Mabie,  Hamilton  W.    American  ideals,  char- 
acter, and   life.      1913.  14266 
Endeavors   to   show   that    "ineradicable   and    con- 
trolling idealism  has  been  a  dominating  element  in 
America." 


Matthews,  Brander.     The  American  of  the 
future,  and  other  essays.     1910.       E  4556 
Entertaining  reading. 

Perry,  Bliss.    The  American  mind.     1912. 

J5659 

The  comments  on  literature,  art,  business,  and 
character,  are  keen,  shrewd  and  penetrating,  and 
have  the  delightful  quality  of  humor. 

Royce,  Josiah.     The  philosophy  of  loyalty. 
1908.  L  6082 

"A  sincere  attempt  to  set  clearly  before  the 
American  people  the  need  for  aiming  at  the  high- 
est ethical  ideals  in  their  daily  life,  in  their  inter- 
course with  one  another,  and  in  their  relations  with 
the  outside  world." 


Van  Dyke,  Henry. 
1910. 


The  spirit  of  America. 
E6216 


A  clear  statement  of  ideas  "that  seem  vital,  sig- 
nificant and  creative  in  the  life  and  character  of 
the  American  people." 

Wilson,  Woodrow.    The  new  freedom.  1913. 

L7596 

An  inspiring  collection  of  portions  of  many  of 
Mr.  Wilson's  campaign  speeches,  showing  the  new 
needs  of  America. 

C.  L.  E. 


Restful  Reading 


Brainerd,  Eleanor  H.    How  could  you  Jean? 

F 11314 

The  heroine  to  support  herself  after  reverses, 
turns  her  only  accomplishment,  cooking,  to  account. 

Howe,  Edgar  W.     The  story  of  a  country 
town.  F  4050 

A   country   town   minister   has   grown   away    from 
a  wife  he  misunderstood.     All  the  narrowness  of  a 
small  town  is  depicted  here,  but  well  done. 
Poole,  Ernest.     The  harbor.  F  16546 

A    semi-autobiographical   story.      Business   life;    a 
pretty  love  thread  interwoven. 
Rouse,,  Adelaide  L.    Under  my  own  roof. 

F9720 

A  newspaper  woman  has  a  house  built  under  her 
own  supervision.  She  employs  a  maid  with  a 
"story."  A  bachelor  next  door  calls  evenings  to 
play  cards.  Interesting. 

Sherwood,    Margaret    P.      The    worn    door 

step.  F 18047 

A  young  woman    takes   an    English   home   where 

she  eases  her  own  grief  by  caring  for  others.     The 

story  is  in  the  form  of  letters  to  a  dead  lover  killed 

in  the  war. 


Baker,  Ray  S.    Adventures  in  contentment. 

E3193 

The   friendly   road;    new   adventures   in 

contentment.  E  3196 

Accounts  of  leisurely  wanderings  as  an  optical 
peddler.  The  people  he  meets  and  the  new  scenes 
are  all  told  in  a  cheerful,  contemplative  style. 

Benson,  Arthur  C.    Father  Payne.        E  1910 
A  story  held  together  by  short  essays  on  things 
in    general.      Epigrammatic.

H'obo  ballads  and  some  others.

bits.  E  5512

jects.

Farm.  H  2633 

Rebecca  is  a  very  real  little  girl  who  has  a  hard

that  little  girls  have  trouble  minding  their   Ps  and 

"  Qs.  Enjoyed  by  grown-up  girls  as  well  as  the 
other  kind. 

Twain,  Mark.     Huckleberry  Finn.        F2797 


Read  Tom   Sawyer  first  and  this  sequel  can't  be 
resisted. 
Tom  Sawyer.  F  323 

A  genuinely  humorous  story  of  a  boy  and  his 
gang  in  a  little  town  on  the  Missouri  river.  Sup- 
posed to  be  based  on  Clemens'  own  life.  Good 
picture  of  early  days  of  river  travel. 

Cowles,  Caroline  R.    Village  life  in  America. 

C 21275 

Charming  diary  kept  by  a  little  girl  before  the 
Civil  war.  The  writer,  a  very  proper  little  girl 
and  her  small  sister,  who  is  not  so  proper,  live  with 
their  grandparents  in_Canandaigua,  N.  Y.  The  life 
in  those  days  of  rigid  up-bringing  is  interestingly 
and  amusingly  told. 

Earle,  Alice  Morse.     Child  life  in  Colonial 
days.  B  2155 

Very  interesting  because  it  is  historically  true. 

Warner,  Charles  Dudley.     Being  a  boy. 

E1597 

The  life  of  a  boy  on  an  old-fashioned  New  Eng- 
land farm.  Will  be  enjoyed  by  those  who  would 
like  to  revive  those  days  of  homely  pleasures  earned 
by  good  hard  work.

A  6637 


A  very  thrilling  book  on  the  war.  Pat  O'Brien

A   Christmas   garland. 

E1886 


A  collection  of  exceedingly  clever  parodies.  Try

Birmingham,  George  A. 


Spanish  gold. 

F 11176 


The  diverting  tale  of  an  Irish  curate  of  much

Irish  coast 

DeMprgan,  William.  Joseph  Vance;  an  ill- 
written  autobiography.  F 12405 
An  intensely  human  story  of  mid- Victorian  Eng- 
land, unfolded  in  a  leisurely  manner  with  many 
humorous  touches.  Not  for  the  hurried  reader,  but 
for  lovers  of  Dickens  and  Thackeray. 

Henry,  O.     The  four  million.  F  13681 

Short  stories  of  New  York  life,  full  of  the  unex- 
pected, pathos,  wit,  and  tragedy.  Told  picturesquely, 
with  far-fetched  metaphors  and  strange  similes,  and 
amazing  ingenuity  in  handling  words. 

Leacock,  Stephen.     Literary  lapses.     E  4251 
Absurd    and   delightful   skits,   bubbling   over   with 


gayety. 
Lindsay,   Vachel. 
poems. 


The    Congo,    and   other 
E  11201 


Pictorial,  musical  and  unusual.  Poems  par  ex- 
cellence for  reading  aloud,  as  the  author  has  fur- 
nished marginal  notes  giving  directions  for  reciting 
them. 

Locke,  William  J.     The  beloved  vagabond. 

F  14843 

The  whimsical  and  charming  story  of  an  irre- 
sponsible philosopher  who  adopts  a  small  boy  and 
wanders  with  him  through  Europe. 

Noyes,  Alfred.  Collected  poems.  E 11352 
A  great  many  of  them  are  excellent  for  reading 
aloud;  special  mention  might  be  made  of  "The 
barrel  organ,"  "My  lady  of  the  twilight,"  and 
"Forty  singing  seamen"  of  the  shorter  poems,  and 
"Drake"  and  "The  companion  of  a  mile"  of  the 
longer. 

Shaw,   Bernard.     Pygmalion.  E  12798 

One  of  Shaw's  most  sparkling  comedies,  illustrat- 
ing the  value  of  "phonetics";  in  other  words  the 
open  sesame  that  a  correct  pronunciation  of  Eng- 
lish is  to  the  door  of  English  society,  as  exemplified 
in  the  case  of  Eliza  the  flower  girl. 

Tarkington,   Booth.      Seventeen;   a   tale   of 

youth  and  summer  time  and  the  Baxter 

family,  especially  William.  F  18550 

A   highly   amusing  story   and   also  a   keen  study 

of  boy  psychology.

marked  Doc.  are  in  the  Document  department.  *Ser.  represents 

serials  which  do  not  circulate.
