September  1918 


Chicago  Public  Library 

Book  Bulletin 


THE  other  organization  that  I  have 
in  mind  is  the  American  Library 
Association.  During  the  past  two 
months  I  have  been  stationed  "west  of 
the  tracks,"  in  Camp  Perry,  and,  later, 
in  Camp  Dewey,  [Great  Lakes].  Mid- 
way between  is  the  building  of  the 
A.  L.  A.,  and  here  I  quickly  found  a 
quiet  haven  for  study,  in  a  big,  warm, 
well-aired  building,  filled  with  books 
that  met  every  desire  of  study  or  relaxa- 
tion, presided  over  by  intelligent  gentle- 
men eager  to  give  their  help  to  the  war 
by  sharing  with  the  boys  their  wider  in- 
tellectual points  of  view. 

From    "Students    of    the    Sea,"    by    Joseph 
Husfeand.    (Atlantic  Monthly,  September.) 


Books  for  Soldiers 

The  other  day  we  read  a  young  soldier's  first  letter  to  his  mother  after  reaching 
Europe.  One  paragraph  said:  "We  were  eighteen  days  crossing;  a  dull  trip  and 
nothing  to  read.  There  were  some  dog-eared  copies  of  popular  magazines  that  I  had 
seen  before  sailing,  and  some  popular  fiction  that  I  had  either  read  or  didn't  care 
for." 

The  American  Library  Association,  which  has  charge  of  supplying  our  fighting 
men  with  reading  matter,  reports  that  the  call  for  books  of  a  serious  educational 
sort — history,  economics,  travel,  biography,  technical  treatises  and  works — is  greater 
than  it  can  meet  at  present. 

This  is  a  national  loss.  In  training  camps  here,  on  shipboard  and  over  there, 
the  young  men  have  many  an  hour  to  themselves.  Wherever  there  is  an  inclination 
to  read  a  useful  book  it  ought  to  be  met ;  and  for  entertainment  there  is  no  better 
recourse  than  reading. 

The  soldiers  have  been  supplied  quite  plentifully  with  the  reading  matter  that 
can  be  got  to  them  in  the  easiest  way,  with  no  bother  and  expense  to  anybody — that 
is,  by  simply  dropping  a  read  magazine  into  the  post  office,  to  be  forwarded  by  the 
Post-Office  Department.  That  is  good  so  far  as  it  goes,  but  inadequate  as  a  method 
of  disposing  of  the  whole  matter.  Of  course,  the  Library  Association  does  go  fur- 
ther than  that,  aiming  to  establish  in  camps,  on  transports  and  at  rest  stations 
libraries  that  cover  a  wide  range.  Its  means  are  not  adequate.  The  question  of  a 
bigger  appropriation  for  this  useful  purpose  ought  to  be  taken  up. — From  the  Satur- 
day Evening  Post,  Sept.  7th. 


Chicago  Public  Library 

Book  Bulletin 

Volume  8  September,  1918  No.   7 


Carry  On! 

I:    RECONSTRUCTION 

Two  tasks  of  vital  and  immediate  bearing  upon  the  future  welfare  of  the  nation 
confront  us  in  the  present  hour.  Both  issue  directly  out  of  the  World  War.  Both 
are  engaging  the  attention  of  rapidly  increasing  numbers  of  people,  and  have  been 
made  the  subject  of  specialized,  official,  institutional  and  volunteer  activities.  Both 
are  educational  in  the  widest  implications  of  that  term,  requiring  preparation  on  the 
part  of  leaders,  and  study  under  direction  of  such  leaders  on  the  part  of  the  individ- 
uals, classes  or  groups  to  be  reached.  Both  call  for  the  use  of  books.  Hence  both 
are  legitimately  within  the  field  of  cooperation  by  and  with  the  Public  Library. 

The  first  is  the  poignantly  appealing  task  of  the  Reconstruction, — Rehabilita- 
tion, Re-education — of  disabled  soldiers  and  sailors.  Vast  in  the  variety  of  its 
possibilities,  though  not,  let  us  still  hope,  in  the  numbers  of  the  men  who  will  re- 
turn to  us  with  broken  bodies  and  shattered  nerves,  this  great  work  is  engaging  the 
highest  skill  and  utmost  devotion  of  the  whole  world  at  war.  Not  only  physical 
reconstruction,  but  mental,  moral,  educational,  and  industrial  readjustment,  the 
recovery  of  lost  faculties  and  facilities,  and  the  training  of  hitherto  undeveloped 
forces  are  comprehended  in  the  great  program  already  set  in  motion.  "Not  char- 
ity, but  a  chance"  is  to  be  offered  the  veteran  whom  the  havoc  of  battle  has  left 
handicapped.  Training  in  reading  for  the  blind  and  in  the  operation  of  machines 
through  the  sense  of  touch,  instruction  in  trades  and  occupations  open  to  the  par- 
tially disabled,  the  recovery  of  mental  equilibrium,  the  ability  to  use  artificial  mem- 
bers— these  are  some  of  the  numerous  branches  of  the  task  for  which  guides  and 
teachers  are  being  sought  and  for  which  hundreds  of  men  and  women  are  preparing 
themselves.  Unfortunately  the  bibliography  of  this  enormous  and  multifarious 
undertaking  is  still  in  a  relatively  chaotic  and  fragmentary  state.  While  great  and 
successful  work  has  been  done  in  France,  England  and  Canada,  the  entire  subject 
is  so  recent  and  so  swift  in  its  developments  that  very  little  printed  material  compre- 
hensive in  scope  or  concise  in  character  has  yet  appeared  in  book  form.  For  the 
time  being  almost  the  sole  resource  of  the  student  must  be  the  large  and  rapidly 
accumulating  mass  of  magazine  literature.  This  however  is  fairly  accessible  through 


94  CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 

the  indexes  and  to  this,  for  the  present,  the  attention  of  interested  readers'  is 
directed  with  the  proffer  of  every  possible  aid  and  convenience  available  in  this 
Library  to  render  the  printed  literature  bearing  upon  any  and  all  phases  of  the  sub- 
ject directly  and  quickly  available.  Successive  issues  of  this  Bulletin  will  call  atten- 
tion to  all  new  books  as  they  appear  and  these  will  be  purchased  as  quickly  as  they 
can  be  had  and  in  quantities  as  closely  related  to  the  demand  as  it  is  possible  to 
determine  it.  Suggestions  and  recommendations  for  the  acquisition  of  such  mate- 
rial and  all  information  as  to  where  it  may  be  secured  are  urgently  requested  and 
will  be  promptly  followed  up. 

Attention  is  called  to  the  splendid  magazine  "Carry  On:  a  Magazine  on  the 
Reconstruction  of  Disabled  Soldiers  and  Sailors"  edited  by  the  office  of  the  Sur- 
geon General,  U.  S.  Army,  and  published  by  the  American  Red  Cross.  The  first 
number  was  issued  in  July.  The  sole  mission  and  purpose  of  the  periodical  is  to 
disseminate  information  upon  the  whole  great  Reconstruction  plan.  It  will  be  sent 
Free  to  anyone  sending  name,  address  and  occupation  to  the  publication  office,  311 
Fourth  Avenue,  New  York  City.  Copies  are  on  file  in  this  Library. 

The  second  great  task  now  occupying  the  attention  of  many  public  spirited 
organizations  and  individuals  is  Americanization — the  process  of  making  good,  or 
better,  Americans  out  of  the  immigrant  multitudes  in  our  midst.  Selected  lists  of 
books  on  the  several  phases  of  this  subject,  together  with  a  concrete  proposal  for 
Cooperation  by  this  Library  will  be  given  in  these  pages  next  month. 


Cheer-Up  Books  for  Blinded  Soldiers 

The  hundreds  of  Chicagoans  who  helped  in  providing  Scrap  Books  for  soldiers 
and  sailors  in  hospitals  will  respond  with  even  greater  zeal  to  the  appeal  for  similar 
books,  called  Cheer-Up  Books,  for  soldiers  returning  with  the  fearful  handicap  of 
blindness.  The  following  particulars  of  the  plan  are  taken  from  a  pamphlet  issued 
by  a  committee  in  Cleveland  organized  to  handle  the  production  and  distribution  of 
these  books. 

It  is  desired  to  work  out  a  plan  by  which  the  ordinary  reading  matter  in  raised 
Braille  type,  which  will  be  available  at  the  library  to  be  maintained  in  connection 
with  the  school  at  Baltimore,  and  such  other  hospitals  or  schools  as  may  hereafter 
be  established,  may  be  supplemented  by  what  may  be  called  "Cheer-Up  Books." 
These  would  be  in  the  nature  of  scrap  books,  a  few  pages  in  length,  containing 
items  of  interest,  such  as  short  stories,  clippings,  letters,  jokes,  verses,  conundrums, 
bits  of  scientific  or  other  information,  etc., — in  short,  the  kind  of  material  with 
which  every  successful  newspaper  or  periodical  abounds.  In  connection  with  the 
re-education  of  blinded  soldiers  in  France  it  has  been  found  possible  to  make  good 
use  of  embossed  illustrations. 

Such  books  should  be  written  or  printed  in  revised  Braille,  grade  \y2,  on 
sheets  about  9^x12  inches  in  size,  of  a  good  quality  of  paper,  so  as  to  take  impres- 
sions which  will  be  easily  legible  to  inexperienced  fingers. 


BOOK  BULLETIN  95 


Nearly  any  large  paper  dealer  can  supply  paper  that  has  been  found  satisfactory 
for  this  purpose.  It  should  be  at  least  as  substantial  as  "New  York  ledger"  or  "K. 
P.  C.  linen  ledger,"  44  Ibs.  to  the  ream,  which  comes  in  sheets  19  in.  x  24  in.  in 
size  and  will  be  cut  by  the  dealer  into  quarters,  thus  making  pages  of  the  proper  size. 

These  Braille  Cheer-Up  Books  can  be  made  in  several  ways.  Where  only  one 
copy  is  desired,  it  may  be  written  by  means  of  a  Braille  typewriter,  which  costs 
from  $15  to  $20,  or  a  Braille  desk  slate  which  costs  only  $1.60.  The  number  of 
blind  persons  in  the  United  States  able  to  write  Braille  is  such  that  it  will  be  pos- 
sible to  find  someone  in.  nearly  every  city  qualified  to  make  such  books,  either  from 
material  of  his  own  or  furnished  by  others.  Again  in  many  cities  of  the  United 
States  groups  of  seeing  persons  have  already  learned  to  read  and  write  Braille,  so 
that  they  may  be  able  to  aid  our  blinded  soldiers  if  an  opportunity  shall  present 
itself.  In  the  making  of  Cheer-Up  Books  lies  a  very  definite  opportunity  for  imme- 
diate service,  for  the  need  of  such  books  is  already  pressing. 

If  it  is  desired  to  make  more  than  one  copy  of  a  book  this  can  readily  be 
arranged  through  the  Howe  Publishing  Society  for  the  Blind,  Cleveland.  There 
are  similar  publishing  societies  in  many  other  cities  throughout  the  United  States. 
The  cost  of  such  publication  is  low — 35c  per  hundred  words  for  making  the  plate 
and  only  Ic  per  hundred  words  for  the  printing  and  binding  of  each  copy  made 
from  the  plate. 

If  the  books  are  written  on  Braille  typewriters,  and  it  is  hoped  that  a  very  large 
number  will  be,  for  a  constantly  growing  stream  of  such  books  will  be  needed  and 
probably  more  quickly  than  most  printing  establishments  for  the  blind  can  issue 
them,  the  finished  work  should  be  checked  by  some  one  used  to  reading  Braille  by 
touch  so  as  to  make  certain  that  it  is  readily  legible. 

For  a  few  dollars  it  will  be  possible  for  anyone  to  send  words  of  good  cheer 
and  encouragement  to  blinded  soldiers  in  every  English-speaking  country  of  the 
world.  The  name  and  address  of  the  donor  with  any  other  appropriate  facts  in 
regard  to  the  gift  may  be  inserted  in  each  book,  if  desired.  It  is  believed  that  many 
will  wish  to  give  such  books  in  memory  of  some  relative  or  friend,  for  it  is  difficult 
to  conceive  of  a  more  fitting  living  memorial. 

All  Cheer-Up  Books  for  the  use  of  our  soldiers  and  sailors  are  to  be  sent  to 
Mrs.  Gertrude  T.  Rider,  In  Charge  Reading  Room  for  the  Blind,  Library  of  Con- 
gress, Washington,  D.  C.  If  marked  "Books  for  the  Blind"  they  will  be  carried 
post-free. 

For  any  further  information  desired,  kindly  address — 

HAROLD  T.  CLARK, 
1201  Leader-News  Building, 

Cleveland,  Ohio. 


96  CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


BOOKS  BY  MAIL  TO  TROOPS 

Library  Association  in  France  Broadens  Work  for  U.  S.  Soldiers 
BY  EUNICE  TIETJENS 

Special  Cable  to  the  Daily  News 

Paris,  France,  Sept.  6. — The  American  Library  Association  in  France  has  just 
received  permission  from  Gen.  Pershing  to  extend  the  circulating  library  privilege 
direct  to  any  individual  member  of  the  American  expeditionary  forces  without  cost 
to  the  soldier.  Books  will  be  sent  and  returned  through  the  mails  free  of  charge. 
The  men  until  now  have  been  able  to  get  certain  books  through  such  organizations 
as  the  Red  Cross,  the  Y.  M.  C.  A.  and  the  Salvation  Army.  Under  the  new  arrange- 
ment they  can  write  to  Paris  for  any  particular  book  and  it  will  be  mailed  to  them 
immediately. 

The  association  has  also  established  a  large  American  circulating  library  and 
distributing  center  in  Paris.  The  large  home-like  rooms  were  opened  to  the  public 
five  days  ago  and  they  are  already  full  of  readers.  Anyone  who  has  seen  the  lonely 
boys  strolling  listlessly  around  outlying  villages  in  France  will  appreciate  the  great 
value  to  the  morale  of  the  soldiers  of  this  new  service. 


From  a  letter  of  a  French  poilu  to  his  Chicago  "maraine": 

On  passe  aussi  un  bon  temps  de  nos  loisirs  a  lire,  voila  encore  une  des  belles 
distractions  du  poilu,  mais  c'est  difficile  a  y  procurer  des  livres  et  bien  souvent  on  en 
manque.  Mais  on  ne  tout  fait  pas  pour  cela;  que  faire,  il  faut  bien  avoir  patience. 

Translation: 

We  pass  a  good  deal  of  our  leisure  in  reading,  after  all  one  of  the  poilu's  best 
distractions.  But  it  is  difficult  to  get  books  and  often  we  haven't  any.  Not  all  is 
done  about  this  that  might  be.  Then  there  is  nothing  left  but  to  have  patience. 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


97 


Books  Added  to  the  Library 

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those 

marked  Doc.  are  in  the  Document  department.    *Ser.  represents 

serials  which  do  not  circulate.

Beresford,  John  D.  and  Richmond,  K.  W. 
E.  Ford:  a  biography.  1917.  F 11106 
Although  the  book  takes  the  form  of  a  biography, 
it  seems  probable  that  "W.  E.  Ford"  is  a  hypothet- 
ical character.  He  is  depicted  as  a  young  English- 
man whose  gift  for  teaching,  coupled  with  his  pro- 
gressive ideas,  leads  him  to  found  a  small  private 
school  in  which  he  can  work  out  his  educational 
theories.

secret  of  the  Marne.  1918.  F  11103 

A  French  sergeant,  with  the  aid  of  his  sweet- 
heart, kidnaps  the  German  general,  Von  Kluck,  and 
then  impersonating  the  general,  gives  his  orders  in 
such  a  way,  as  to  bring  about  the  defeat  of  the 
Germans,  at  the  first  battle  of  the  Marne.

Book  Bulletin 


LONG,  TOO  LONG,  AMERICA 

Long,  too  long,  America. 

Travelling  roads  all  even  and  peaceful, 
you  learn'd  from  joys  and  prosperity 
only; 

But  now,  ah  now,  to  learn  from  crises 
of  anguish — advancing,  grappling 
with  direst  fate,  and  recoiling  not; 

And  now  to  conceive,  and  show  to  the 
world,  what  your  children  en-masse 

really  are.  • 

—Walt  Whitman. 


LIBRARY  WAR  SERVICE 

of  the 
American  Library  Association 


t|  Builds,  Equips  and  Operates  Libraries  for  Soldiers  and 
Sailors,  on  this  Side  and  Overseas. 

Forty-four  libraries  of  30,000  volumes  at  large  camps,  300 
smaller  camps  and  posts,  148  Naval  and  Marine  Stations,  350 
libraries  and  branches  overseas. 

€[  Supplies  Books  and  Magazines  for  the  Red  Cross, 
Y.  M.  C.  A.,  K.  of  C.,  Y.  W.  C.  A.,  Jewish  Welfare 
Board,  War  Camp  Community  Service  and  Salvation 
Army. 

1,547  branch  libraries  and  stations  have  been  established  in 
the  huts,  canteens,  club  rooms  and  hostess  houses. 

€J  Places  Libraries  on  War  Ships,  Transports  and  Govern- 
ment Cargo  Ships. 

Two  hundred  and  fifty  vessels  have  libraries;  there  is  a  deck 
library  on  every  transport. 

€J  Makes  the  Public's  Gifts  of  Books  Available  for  Our 
Fighting  Men. 

3,011,510  volumes  have  been  collected  and  placed  in  circula- 
tion. 

€J  Buys  Educational  and  Technical  Books  to  Meet  the 
Demand  of  the  Men  for  "Books  That  Help." 

560,271  volumes  have  been  purchased  thus  far,  of  which  198,267 
have  been  sent  overseas. 

CJ  Distributes  the  Magazines  Contributed  by  the  Public 
through  the  Post-Office  Department. 

More  than  5,000,000  copies  of  periodicals  have  been  placed  in 
the  hands  of  our  forces. 

€j[  Maintains  Library  Service  in  Every  Ward  of  Every 
Military  Hospital. 

Trained  librarians  serve  our  wounded  as  well  as  their  more 
fortunate  brothers  in  arms. 

«I  Needs  $3,500,000  to  Buy  More  Books  and  Expand  the 
Service. 

We   have   sent   more  than   a  million  books   overseas;    we  must 
'      send  millions  more. 


In  Giving  to  the  UNITED  WAR  WORK  CAMPAIGN 
You  are  Giving  to  Library  War  Service. 


Chicago  Public  Library 

Book  Bulletin 

Volume  8  October,  1918  No.  8 


Carry  On! 

II:    AMERICANIZATION 

To  mould  the  new  American  into  America  is  the  concise  phrase 
of  Chicago  Commerce  in  characterizing  the  great  program  of  Ameri- 
canization which  the  Association  of  Commerce  has  taken  up  in 
response  to  the  government  appeal.  The  movement  is  nation-wide, 
initiated  by  the  Bureau  of  Education  and  Secretary  of  the  Interior 
Lane,  and  has  for  its  object  "the  education  of  our  alien  population 
toward  a  better  understanding  of  our  language,  ideals,  institutions 
and  customs."  The  Americanization  Committee  of  the  C.  A.  of  C. 
has  sent  a  letter  and  questionnaire  to  a  selected  list  of  employers  in 
five  hundred  industries  and  is  planning  to  establish  at  least  two 
hundred  classes  in  the  larger  plants  of  Chicago.  The  Board  of 
Education  is  cooperating  in  supplying  teachers;  adult  classes  are  be- 
ing added  to  the  public  night  schools;  scores  of  societies  and  clubs 
are  organizing  study  groups  among  the  foreign-born.  Institutes 
for  training  teachers  were  conducted  during  the  summer  by 
the  Americanization  Committee  of  the  Council  of  National  Defense. 
On  every  hand  indications  appear  that  among  the  chief  pre-occupa- 
tions  of  organizations  and  individuals  interested  in  the  public  wel- 
fare will  be  this  great  and  many-sided  movement  to  lead  our  people 
of  foreign  nativity  "towards  a  better  understanding." 

The  Public  Library  desires  to  participate  in  this  enterprise. 
Confident  that  its  resources  can  be  of  great  help,  it  is  equally  con- 
fident that  they  cannot  be  employed  in  a  better  cause,  and  hereby 
places  its  entire  machinery,  divested  of  all  possible  restrictions  and 
limitations  at  the  disposal  of  any  and  all  local  agencies  engaged 
in  Americanization  work.  On  the  following  pages  is  a  list  of  books 
now  in  the  Library  bearing  upon  the  chief  divisions  of  the  subject. 
The  list  has  intentionally  been  made  long  and  comprehensive  in 
order  that  something  may  be  included — if  only  by  way  of  sugges- 


114  CHICAGO    PUBLIC    LIBRARY 

tion — on  every  phase  of  the  work.  New  books  will  be  purchased  as 
they  appear  and  duplicates  of  such  as  prove  most  usable  will  be 
added  as  far  as  the  Library  funds  will  permit. 

Societies,  clubs,  study  groups,  employers  and  class  leaders  con- 
ducting or  promoting  Americanization  work  are  therefore  invited  to 
call  upon  the  Public  Library  for  collections  of  books,  as  large  or  as 
small  as  can  be  used — from  these  lists  or  from  other  authentic 
recommendations — to  be  used  by  students  in  such  classes  or  groups 
under  direction  of  competent  teachers  or  leaders.  Such  collections 
will  be  installed  in  the  places  where  classes  are  held  and  may  be  kept 
for  the  duration  of  the  course  or  study  period.  The  Library  will 
require  the  signature  of  a  responsible  custodian  or  representative  of 
each  organization  receiving  such  installation,  acknowledging  receipt 
of  the  books,  guaranteeing  their  safe  return  and  assuming  definite 
liability  for  damages  or  loss  beyond  that  incident  to  proper  and  con- 
tinuous use.  Requests  for  installations  of  books  should  be  addressed 
to  the  Deposit  Department  and  should  be  made  as  far  as  possible  in 
advance  of  the  time  when  they  will  be  needed,  since  in  many  cases 
duplicate  copies  will  have  to  be  bought.

Steiner,  Edward  A.  Nationalizing  America. 
1916.  L 16090 

Lectures  delivered  at  Chautauqua  during  a  week 
devoted  to  the  consideration  of  Americanization. 
Some  of  the  subjects  covered  are:  Nationalism; 
Language  and  the  nation;  Schools  and  the  nation; 
Churches  and  the  nation;  and  Nationality  and  the 
immigrant. 

Talbot,  Winthrop,  comp.  Americanization; 
principles  of  Americanism,  essentials  of 
Americanization,  technic  of  race-assimila- 
tion. 1917.  L 14272 

Excerpts  from  books,  addresses,  and  magazine 
articles  covering  the  various  phases  of  Americaniza- 
tion— the  principles  of  American  philosophy  and 
ideals,  and  the  methods  of  race  amalgamation  and 


assimilation  through  education,  industry,  politics,  and 
every  day  life.     Contains  an  extensive  bibliography. 

Tupper,     George     William.       Foreign-born 
neighbors.     1914.  L  6854 

Manual  of  methods  for  Americanizing  foreign- 
speaking  men,  based  on  the  experience  of  the 
Y.  M.  C.  A.  in  Massachusetts  and  Rhode  Island. 

Wald,    Lillian    D.      The    house    on    Henry 
Street.     1915.  L8501 

An  account  of  twenty  years'  experience  in  a  New 
York  settlement.  Contains  an  interesting  chapter  on 
New  Americans  and  our  policies. 

Woods,     Robert     A.,     ed.       Americans     in 
process.     1902.  L  8507 

Of  especial  interest  in  this  collection  of  papers  by 
residents  of  South  End  House,  Boston,  is  an  article 
by  R.  A.  Woods  entitled:  Assimilation,  a  two  edged 
sword. 


Foreign-born  Americans  in  the  Making 


Antin,  Mary.     The  promised  land. 


1912. 
C  20605 


A  vivid  account  of  the  long  and  often  tragic 
process  by  which  a  Russian  Jewess  was  transformed 
into  an  American. 

Hasanovitz,  Elizabeth.    One  of  them.    1918. 

C  22339 

Autobiography  of  a  Jewish  girl  who  emigrated 
from  Russia  to  America  and  became  a  garment 
maker  in  New  York.  Its  chief  interest  lies  in  her 
reaction  to  labor  conditions  there. 

Ravage,  M.  E.    An  American  in  the  making. 
1917.  C  23888 

Life  story  of  a  Rumanian  from  his  boyhood  in 
Vaslui  to  his  sophomore  year  in  the  University  of 
Missouri.  "The  value  of  this  autobiography  lies  in 
the  fact  that  the  author  did  achieve  Americanization, 
and  from  this  vantage  point  could  look  back  over  his 
heartrending  struggles  with  an  eye  that  fully  appre- 
ciated what  he  had  lost  and  gained." — Springfield 
Republican. 

Rihbany,  Abraham   Mitrie.     A   far  voyage. 
1914.  C  23945 

The  author,  now  pastor  of  the  Church  of  the 
Disciples  in  Boston,  reviews  the  story  of  his  life 
from  his  childhood  in  Syria,  through  the  period  of 


his  immigration  and  readjustment  to  American  sur- 
roundings. 

Riis,  Jacob  A.     The  making  of  an  Ameri- 
can.    1901.  C 18810 

The  first  autobiography  of  a  foreign-bora  American. 
The  author,  who  was  born  in  Denmark,  became  a 
well-known  American  journalist,  philanthropist,  and 
reformer. 

Steiner,   Edward   A.     Against   the    current. 
1910.  C  24359 

From  alien  to  citizen.     1914.        C  24358 

The  confession  of  a  hyphenated  Ameri- 
can.    1916.  B6004 

The  first  of  these  three  books  describes  the  boy- 
hood of  the  author  in  Hungary;  the  second,  his  later 
life  in  the  United  States;  and  the  third,  his  response 
to  the  need  of  'his  adopted  country  during  the  present 
crisis.  Dr.  Steiner  has  been  for  many  years  an 
active  worker  in  Americanization  and  is  the  anther 
of  Nationalizing  America,  listed  above. 

Stern,  E.  G.     My  mother  and  I.     1917. 

C  24370 

S'tory  of  the  Americanizing  of  a  young  Jewish  girl 
whose  parents  came  from  a  small  town  in  Russian 
Poland  to  the  ghetto  of  a  Middle  West  city.

Book  Bulletin 


In  Giving  to  the 

UNITED  WAR  WORK 
CAMPAIGN 

You  Are  Giving  to 

LIBRARY      WAR 
SERVICE 


LIBRARY  WAR  SERVICE 

of  the 
American  Library  Association 


'""P'HE  American  soldier  does  not  expect  al- 
•*•  ways  to  be  a  soldier.  Fighting  the  battles 
of  his  country  now,  later  he  expects  to  be  fight- 
ing the  battle  of  life.  His  ambition  to  succeed 
in  business  or  profession  is  reflected  by  his  de- 
sire for  educational  books  which  he  may  study 
with  profit. 

Library  War  Service  of  the  American 
Library  Association  seeks  to  place  in  the 
hands  of  every  member  of  our  fighting  forces 
the  book  he  needs  when  he  wants  it,  whether 
he  wishes  to  read  for  recreation  or  to  fit  him- 
self to  be  a  better  fighter  or  a  better  American, 
to  help  him  win  success  after  the  war. 

IT  takes  money  to  maintain  and  expand  this 
*  service.  While  gifts  of  books  are  welcome, 
gifts  of  money  to  buy  the  special  books  the 
soldiers  need  are  doubly  welcome. 


In  Giving  to  the  UNITED  WAR  WORK  CAMPAIGN 
You  are  Giving  to  Library  War  Service. 


Chicago  Public  Library

*-*  Americanization  work,  comprising  books  on  the  study  of  Eng-

letin, the  Library  is  prepared  to  supply  deposits  of  these  books  to 
clubs  and  study  groups  engaged  in  this  work.

Books  Added  to  the  Library 

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those 

marked  Doc.  are  in  the  Document  department.     *Ser .  represents 

serials  which  do  not  circulate.

CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Rules  and  Information  for  Patrons 


Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person 
residing  in  Cook  County  having  permanent 
employment  in  the  city,  may  enjoy  the  priv- 
ileges of  the  Library  by  having  a  resident 
of  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear  in  the 
city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon 
application  and  compliance  with  the  rules 
and  regulations  of  the  Board  of  Directors 
a  card  good  for  three  years  will  be  issued, 
which  will  permit  the  applicant  to  draw 
five  books  for  home  use. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges 
upon  application. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and 
may  be  once  renewed  for  the  same  period. 
Books  may  be  renewed  but  once.  Books 
of  recent  p.urhase  labeled  Seven-day  books 
cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed.  The  card 
must  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 
out  or  renewed. 

Books  issued  at  Circulating  Centers  or 
Delivery  Stations  may  be  returned  at  the 
Main  Library,  but  books  cannot  be  applied 
for  at  the  Main  Library  and  delivered  to 
Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery  Stations. 

Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  hab- 
its may  use  the  various  Reading  and  Refer- 
ence Rooms  of  the  Library.  No  card  is 


required  to  use  the  books  on  the  open 
shelves  or  the  books  in  the  stacks,  which 
must  be  applied  for. 

A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported 
to  the  Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books 
drawn  on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  pre- 
sented, and  for  all  fines  incurred.  If  a  card 
is  lost,  immediate  notice  must  be  given  to 
the  Library.  A  new  card  will  be  issued 
thirty  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  one  cent  a  day  on  each  work  of 
non-fiction,  and  three  cents  a  day  on  each 
work  of  fiction,  is  imposed  whether  bound 
in  one  or  more  volumes,  which  is  not  re- 
turned within  two  weeks  from  the  date  of 
issue.  An  additional  charge  of  two  cents 
is  made  for  each  notice  sent  for  the  return 
of  a  book,  and  no  other  books  will  be  de- 
livered to  the  person  incurring  the  fine  until 
it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the 
call  number  is  crossed  off  the  order  slip 
whenever  a  book  is  drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohibited, 
and  all  injuries  to  books  beyond  reasonable 
wear  and  all  losses  must  be  promptly  ad- 
justed  to  the  satisfaction  of  the  Librarian. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  De- 
partments, third  floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:30 
p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Cata- 
logue, fourth  floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m., 
Sundays  and  holidays,  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People, 
fourth  floor,  open  9:30  a.  m.  \.o  6  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to 
5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday 

Patents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room, 
first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a  m.  to  5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street 
entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  closed 
on  Sunday. 


Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and 
newspapers,  fourth  floor,  Randolph  street 
entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays 
and  holidays,  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Music  Room,  fifth  floor,  Washington 
street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulle- 
tin are  issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and 
August,  and  are  distributed  free  of  charge 
in  all  departments  of  the  Main  Library  and 
in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be  mailed  to 
any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year  to 
cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published 
in  January  of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten 
cents  per  copy.

Volume  8  December,  1918  No.  10 


following  lists  represent  an  attempt  to  indicate  to  Christmas 
purchasers  and  others,  a  few — a  carefully  sifted  few — of  the 
books  that  librarians  deem  it  every  child's  right  to  know  and  to  own. 
They  will  be  found  to  comprise  an  astonishing  preponderance  of 
old  familiar  friends  and  but  a  meager  representation  of  the  very 
new.  They  were  made  to  remind  patrons  who  are  privileged  to 
minister  to  the  festival  pleasures  of  children  that  these  fine  old  books 
cherished  among  their  own  childhood  memories  are  still  as  fine  as 
ever  and  still  extant  and  very  much  alive  in  charming  reincarnations 
of  modern  print  and  pictures. 

The  lists  have  been  issued  in  separate  form  on  slips  of  bookmark 
size  to  supplement  an  exhibit  of  Christmas  books  displayed  in  the 
central  library  during  December.  They  are  reprinted  here  for  pos- 
sible future  reference.

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those 

marked  Doc.  are  in  the  Document  department.    *Ser.  represents 

serials  which  do  not  circulate.

BRANCHES 

Independent  collections  of  3,000  to  20,000 
volumes  in  quarters  owned  or  rented  by  the 
Library,  or  occupied  by  arrangement  with  the 
Park  Boards,  and  administered  by  a  librarian 
and  staff. 

Branches  marked  *  are  open  from  9  a.  m. 
to  10  p.  m. ;  those  marked  t  are  open  from 
9  a.  m.  to  9  p.  m. ;  all  others  are  open  from 

1  to  9  p.  m.;  Sundays  and  holidays  open  from 

2  to  6  p.  m.

DEPOSIT  STATIONS 

Small  collections  placed  in  outlying  and  re- 
mote districts  in  charge  of  a  local  organiza- 
tion, church,  settlement  house,  etc.,  but  open 
to  the  general  public.  Administered  by  an 
attendant  not  regularly  on  the  Library  Staff 
but  paid  a  fee  based  on  volume  of  circula- 
tion.

HIGH  SCHOOL  LIBRARIES 

Branch  libraries  maintained  in  quarters  pro- 
vided  and   equipped   in  high   schools   by   the 
Board  of  Education.    Books  and  staff  supplied 
by  the  Public  Library.    Primarily  for  the  fac- 
ulty and  student  body. 
Austin  High  School.    200  N.  Lotos  Ave. 
Englewood  High  School.    6220  S.  Stewart  Ave. 
Harrison  Technical  High  School.     2850  24th 

St.  Blvd. 
Lake  View  High   School.     4015  N.  Ashland 

Ave. 
John  Marshall  High  School.    3250  W.  Adams 

St. 

Nicholas  Senn  High  School.    5900  N.  Glen- 
wood  Ave. 

DELIVERY  STATIONS 

Points  of  call  for  Library  delivery  auto- 
mobiles. Located  in  shops  of  various  sorts 
and  have  no  collections  of  books.  Patrons 
may  leave  orders  which  will  be  filled  at  the 
Main  Library  and  delivered  to  the  station.
